topics.title: Intelligent Agents - Types of Intelligent Agents, Problem-Solving & Search Algorithms (Uninformed and Informed Search)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intelligent Agents - Types of Intelligent Agents, Problem-Solving & Search Algorithms (Uninformed and Informed Search)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Intelligent Agent?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that **perceives its environment through sensors** and **acts upon that environment through actuators** based on a rational **agent function** that maps a perceptual history to an action, with the goal of maximizing a **performance measure**.

Mathematically, an agent can be abstracted as a function:

$$
f : \mathcal{P}^* \rightarrow \mathcal{A}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{P}^*$ = the set of all possible **percept sequences** (history of what the agent has perceived)
- $\mathcal{A}$ = the set of all possible **actions** the agent can execute

### Conceptual Analogy: The Self-Driving Car

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of an agent like a **self-driving car**.
> - Its **sensors** (cameras, LIDAR, GPS, accelerometers) are the "eyes and ears" perceiving the road.
> - Its **actuators** (steering, accelerator, brake) are the "hands and feet" acting on the world.
> - Its **agent program** is the on-board AI software deciding *what to do* given what it sees.
> - Its **performance measure** is a safe, fast, comfortable, legal journey to the destination.
>
> Just as a rational driver doesn't randomly steer, a rational agent picks the action expected to maximize its performance measure based on evidence and prior knowledge.

## 1.2 The PEAS Framework (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors)

PEAS is the **standard KTU-specified framework** for describing a task environment. Every AI problem must begin with a PEAS specification.

| Component | Description | Self-Driving Car Example |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **P**erformance Measure | Criteria defining success | Safety, speed, comfort, fuel efficiency, legality |
| **E**nvironment | External world the agent operates in | Roads, traffic, weather, pedestrians |
| **A**ctuators | Mechanism to act on environment | Steering wheel, brake, accelerator, signals |
| **S**ensors | Mechanism to perceive environment | Cameras, LIDAR, radar, GPS, speedometer |

## 1.3 The Structure of an Agent

```
Agent = Architecture + Agent Program
```

- **Architecture**: The physical/computational body (sensors + actuators + computation platform, e.g., a robot chassis with a CPU).
- **Agent Program**: The function that implements the mapping from percepts to actions.

The skeleton of *any* generic agent program is:

$$
\text{function AGENT(percept) returns action} \\
\text{  static: memory (state of the world)} \\
\text{  state} \leftarrow \text{UPDATE-STATE(state, percept)} \\
\text{  rule} \leftarrow \text{RULE-MATCH(state, rules)} \\
\text{  action} \leftarrow \text{rule.ACTION} \\
\text{  return action}
$$

## 1.4 Types of Intelligent Agents (KTU High-Yield!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus mandates the study of **four canonical agent types** (some textbooks include a fifth, *Learning Agent*). These form a **strict progression of increasing capability**.

### 1.4.1 Simple Reflex Agent

- Acts **only on the current percept**, ignoring the rest of the percept history.
- Uses **condition-action rules**: `IF condition THEN action`.
- Analogy: A **thermostat** — if temperature < 20°C, turn heater ON.
- Limitation: Fails in **partially observable** environments because the current percept alone is insufficient.

### 1.4.2 Model-Based Reflex Agent

- Maintains an **internal state** that depends on the **percept history**.
- The internal state tracks how the world evolves (a **model** of the world) and how the agent's own actions affect it.
- Two types of knowledge required:
  - **Model of world dynamics** (how the world changes independently).
  - **Model of agent's own effects** (how its actions change the world).
- Analogy: A driver who remembers the road behind a blind curve, not just what's directly ahead.

### 1.4.3 Goal-Based Agent

- Has an explicit **goal** description (a desired world state).
- Chooses actions that **lead to the goal** by reasoning about the future (via search or planning).
- More flexible than reflex agents because the goal is *explicit* — change the goal, change the behavior, without rewriting rules.
- Analogy: A GPS navigator — given a destination, it plans a route rather than just reacting.

### 1.4.4 Utility-Based Agent

- Has a **utility function** that maps a state (or sequence of states) to a real number representing **happiness/desirability**.
- Used when goals are **insufficient** — e.g., multiple paths to the same goal with trade-offs (time, fuel, safety, comfort).
- Maximizes expected utility, handling **conflicting goals** and **uncertainty**.
- Analogy: A ride-hailing app that picks the *best* route considering traffic, cost, and time — not just any route to the destination.

## 1.5 What is a Problem-Solving Agent?

> [!NOTE]
> A **Problem-Solving Agent** is a *goal-based* agent that uses **search algorithms** to find an **action sequence** (a path) from an initial state to a goal state. It is the canonical entry point to classical AI search.

It works in three phases:
1. **Goal Formulation** — Define what success looks like.
2. **Problem Formulation** — Decide what actions and states to consider (abstraction).
3. **Search** — Compute a solution path.

## 1.6 Uninformed vs Informed Search

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **directly examinable distinction** in KTU 2024 papers.

| Aspect | Uninformed (Blind) Search | Informed (Heuristic) Search |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Uses **heuristic** $h(n)$? | **No** — only problem definition | **Yes** — uses problem-specific knowledge |
| Information available | Just the *structure* of the problem | Adds an *estimate* of distance to goal |
| Optimal? | Sometimes (BFS, UCS) | Often (A*), given admissible heuristic |
| Examples | BFS, UCS, DFS, DLS, IDS, Bidirectional | Greedy Best-First Search, A*, IDA*, SMA* |
| Efficiency | Poor on large spaces | Significantly better in practice |

## 1.7 Real-World Applications

- **Intelligent Agents**: Autonomous vehicles, smart home assistants (Alexa, Siri), industrial robots, recommendation systems, game-playing agents (chess engines, AlphaGo).
- **Search Algorithms**: Route planning (Google Maps), puzzle solving (Rubik's cube), logistics (UPS ORION), scheduling, theorem proving, protein folding (AlphaFold uses A*-like search), game AI pathfinding (NPC movement in video games).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Environment Properties (Crucial for KTU 2-marker)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fully specified task environment is described along **seven orthogonal dimensions**. KTU questions often ask: *"Classify the environment of [application]."*

| Property | Options | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Observable** | Fully / Partially | Are all relevant aspects of the world knowable from sensors? |
| **Deterministic** | Deterministic / Stochastic | Does the next state depend *only* on current state + action? |
| **Episodic** | Episodic / Sequential | Is the agent's action divided into independent episodes? |
| **Static** | Static / Dynamic | Can the world change while the agent deliberates? |
| **Discrete** | Discrete / Continuous | Are percepts, actions, and states finite/countable? |
| **Single-Agent** | Single / Multi-Agent | Is the agent the *only* entity acting to optimize its own performance? |
| **Known** | Known / Unknown | Are the rules/outcomes of actions known to the agent? |

**Quick Example Classifications:**
- **Chess with a clock**: Fully observable, deterministic, sequential, semi-dynamic, discrete, multi-agent, known.
- **Self-driving car**: Partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, continuous, multi-agent, known.

## 2.2 Rationality

> [!NOTE]
> **A rational agent** does whatever action is expected to **maximize its performance measure**, given the percept sequence and the agent's built-in knowledge.

A *rational* action differs from an *omniscient* action: an agent may act rationally **without** knowing the actual outcome, because rationality depends on the *expected* performance given what is *known up to the percept*.

## 2.3 Problem Formulation (Five-Component Tuple)

A search problem is formally defined as the tuple:

$$
\mathcal{P} = \langle S, s_0, A(s), T(s, a), G, C \rangle
$$

Where:
- $S$ — Set of all **states** in the state space.
- $s_0 \in S$ — The **initial state** where the agent starts.
- $A(s)$ — Set of **actions** available in state $s$.
- $T(s, a) \rightarrow s'$ — The **transition (successor) function**; the deterministic result of doing action $a$ in state $s$.
- $G \subseteq S$ — The **goal test**; a function that checks if a state is a goal state.
- $C(s, a, s')$ — The **path cost (step cost)**; a non-negative number for taking action $a$ in state $s$ to reach $s'$.

**Solution**: A sequence of actions $(a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n)$ that transforms $s_0$ into some $g \in G$.

**Optimal Solution**: A solution with the **minimum path cost**.

## 2.4 Tree Search vs Graph Search

- A **search tree** records the path; the same state can appear **multiple times** (different paths).
- A **graph search** keeps a *closed set* (explored set) and *open set* (frontier) to **avoid revisiting states**, eliminating redundant work.
- The state space is a *graph*; the search frontier is a *tree* over it.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet: Search Algorithm Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-tested comparison table in KTU 2024 AI/ML papers.** Memorize the columns.

Let $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = maximum depth of the search tree, $C^*$ = cost of optimal solution, $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost.

| Criterion | BFS | UCS | DFS | DLS | IDS | Bidirectional |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|----------------|
| **Complete?** | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes | No (fails in infinite spaces, dead-ends) | No | Yes | Yes (if both BFS applicable) |
| **Time** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^{\ell})$ | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ |
| **Space** | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(b\ell)$ | $O(bd)$ | $O(b^{d/2})$ |
| **Optimal?** | Yes (unit costs) | Yes | No | No | Yes (unit costs) | Yes (unit costs) |

## 2.6 Informed Search Formula Sheet

Let $f(n)$ = evaluation function for node $n$, $g(n)$ = cost-so-far from start, $h(n)$ = estimated cost from $n$ to nearest goal.

| Algorithm | Evaluation Function $f(n)$ | Strategy |
|-----------|-----------------------------|----------|
| **Greedy Best-First Search** | $f(n) = h(n)$ | Expands node with lowest $h(n)$ — fastest but not optimal |
| **A* Search** | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Combines path cost and heuristic — optimal if $h$ is admissible (and consistent for graph search) |

### 2.6.1 Properties of Heuristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favorite 7-mark question:** *"Define admissible and consistent heuristics. Prove A* optimality."*

- **Admissible Heuristic**: $0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ for all $n$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost to the nearest goal. Never overestimates.
- **Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic**: $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$ for every action $a$ taking $n$ to $n'$. A stronger condition that implies admissibility.
- **Dominance**: $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ if $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$ (and both admissible). A* with $h_2$ expands no more nodes than A* with $h_1$.

### 2.6.2 Classical Heuristics for the 8-Puzzle

- $h_1$ = number of **misplaced tiles**. Admissible.
- $h_2$ = **Manhattan distance** (sum of horizontal + vertical distances of each tile from its goal position). Admissible. **Dominates $h_1$.**

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **PEAS / Environment Classification**: Every real AI product starts here — e.g., a vacuum cleaner robot's task environment is *partially observable, stochastic, episodic, dynamic, continuous, multi-agent, known*.
- **BFS/DFS**: BFS powers shortest-hop social network queries ("degrees of separation"); DFS is used in topological sorting, cycle detection, solving mazes.
- **A* Search**: Backbone of navigation systems (Google Maps), robotics motion planning, video-game pathfinding, VLSI chip layout, natural language processing parsing.
- **Heuristic Design**: The quality of $h(n)$ often makes the difference between an infeasible search and a sub-second solution — a principle behind AlphaGo's strength.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: A* Optimality for Tree Search with an Admissible Heuristic

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a **standard 7-mark proof** in KTU 2024 papers. Practice until you can reproduce it cold.**

We want to show: *A* tree search with an admissible heuristic $h$ returns an optimal solution.*

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Let $C^*$ be the cost of the optimal goal node $G^*$. A* expands nodes in order of increasing $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.

**Step 2 — First goal selected must be optimal.**
Suppose A* terminates by selecting a *non-optimal* goal node $G$ with $f(G) = g(G) = C(G) > C^*$. Then there exists some optimal goal $G^*$ on the frontier with $f(G^*) = g(G^*) + h(G^*) = C^* + h(G^*)$.

**Step 3 — Apply admissibility.**
By admissibility, $h(G^*) = 0$ (since $G^*$ is a goal). So $f(G^*) = C^*$.

**Step 4 — Show $G$ is not picked first.**
A* picks the node with the *minimum* $f$. We have:

$$
f(G) = C(G) > C^* = f(G^*)
$$

But this contradicts the assumption that A* selected $G$ before $G^*$. A* would have popped $G^*$ off the frontier first, found it is a goal, and terminated optimally.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
Therefore A* cannot terminate at a non-optimal goal node. **Hence, A* tree search with an admissible heuristic is optimal.** $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Consistency Implies $f(n)$ is Non-Decreasing Along a Path

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Consistency is required for **graph search** optimality and is also a common 7-mark sub-question.

**Claim:** If $h$ is consistent, then for any node $n$ and any successor $n'$ via action $a$ with cost $c(n, a, n')$, we have $f(n') \geq f(n)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the consistency inequality.**

$$
h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')
$$

**Step 2 — Add $g(n) + c(n, a, n')$ to both sides (since $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, a, n')$).**

$$
g(n) + h(n) \leq g(n) + c(n, a, n') + h(n')
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the LHS as $f(n)$ and the RHS as $f(n')$.**

$$
f(n) \leq f(n')
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**
So $f$-values never decrease along any path. This means the first time A* removes a state from the frontier, it has found the *optimal path* to that state — which is exactly the condition needed for **graph-search optimality**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Iterative Deepening Search (IDS) Optimality and Complexity

> [!NOTE]
> IDS combines BFS's optimality with DFS's space efficiency — a beautiful trick that KTU loves to ask about.

**Step 2.1 — Optimality.**
IDS performs depth-limited DFS with limits $0, 1, 2, \dots, d$. The first limit at which a goal is found equals the depth of the shallowest goal, $d$. With **unit step costs**, this is the optimal cost. Hence IDS is **optimal** for unit costs.

**Step 2.2 — Time Complexity.**
Nodes at depth $k$ are generated $d - k + 1$ times (once for each iteration limit $\geq k$). Total nodes generated:

$$
N(\text{IDS}) = (d+1) b^0 + d \cdot b^1 + (d-1) \cdot b^2 + \dots + 1 \cdot b^d
$$

**Step 2.3 — Asymptotic bound.**
For large $b$, the dominant term is $b^d$. We factor:

$$
N(\text{IDS}) = b^d \left[ (1 + \tfrac{1}{b} + \tfrac{1}{b^2} + \dots) + O(1) \right] \approx b^d \cdot \frac{b}{b-1} = O(b^d)
$$

**Step 2.4 — Space.**
Only one iterative DFS path is stored at a time: $O(bd)$.

**Conclusion:** IDS is **complete, optimal (unit costs), and uses $O(bd)$ space** — the best of BFS and DFS combined.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Path-Cost Computation for A* on a Toy Graph

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical example** — expect this in the KTU numerical section.

Consider a graph with the following nodes and step costs:

| Edge | Step cost |
|------|-----------|
| S → A | 1 |
| S → B | 4 |
| A → B | 2 |
| A → G | 5 |
| B → G | 2 |

Heuristic estimates: $h(S) = 6$, $h(A) = 4$, $h(B) = 2$, $h(G) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Initialize frontier with S.** $f(S) = g(S) + h(S) = 0 + 6 = 6$.

**Step 2 — Expand S.** Children A and B:

$$
f(A) = g(A) + h(A) = 1 + 4 = 5
$$

$$
f(B) = g(B) + h(B) = 4 + 2 = 6
$$

**Step 3 — Pop minimum, A (f=5).** Expand A's children:

Path S → A → B: $g = 1 + 2 = 3$, $f = 3 + 2 = 5$.
Path S → A → G: $g = 1 + 5 = 6$, $f = 6 + 0 = 6$.

**Step 4 — Frontier contents:** B (f=6), S→A→B (f=5), S→A→G (f=6).

**Step 5 — Pop S→A→B (f=5).** Expand:

Path S → A → B → G: $g = 3 + 2 = 5$, $f = 5 + 0 = 5$.

**Step 6 — Pop S→A→B→G (f=5).** It is a goal. **Solution path cost = 5.**

**Step 7 — Verify against alternative.** S → B → G has cost $4 + 2 = 6$. So A* correctly returns the cheaper path. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Search Strategies Side-by-Side

| Feature | BFS | DFS | UCS | Greedy | A* |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|--------|----|
| Uses $g(n)$? | Implicit (uniform) | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Uses $h(n)$? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Optimal? | ✓ (unit) | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ (admissible $h$) |
| Complete? | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Time | $b^d$ | $b^m$ | $\approx b^{C^*/\epsilon}$ | exponential worst | exponential worst |
| Space | $b^d$ | $b \cdot m$ | $\approx b^{C^*/\epsilon}$ | $b^m$ (worst) | $b^d$ (worst) |
| Implementation | FIFO queue | LIFO stack | priority queue by $g$ | priority queue by $h$ | priority queue by $f = g+h$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Intelligent Agent — Functional Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block diagrams the generic agent loop — *Architecture* + *Agent Program* — showing the closed interaction between perception, state update, decision, and action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    env[Environment]
    sens[Sensors]
    arch[Agent Architecture]
    percept[Percept]
    perceive[PERCEIVE]
    update[UPDATE-STATE]
    state[Internal State]
    match[RULE-MATCH]
    action[Action]
    actuate[ACTUATE]
    actr[Actuators]
    perf[Performance Measure]

    env -->|Stimuli| sens
    sens --> perceive
    perceive --> percept
    percept --> arch
    arch --> update
    update --> state
    state --> match
    match --> action
    action --> actuate
    actuate --> actr
    actr -->|Effect| env
    state -.->|Evaluates against| perf
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Agent Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This diagram captures the **progression from simple reflex → utility-based**, and where learning fits in (extension expected for higher marks).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intelligent Agents] --> B[Simple Reflex]
    A --> C[Model-Based Reflex]
    A --> D[Goal-Based]
    A --> E[Utility-Based]
    A --> F[Learning Agent]

    B --> B1[Condition-Action Rules]
    B --> B2[No Internal State]

    C --> C1[Maintains Internal State]
    C --> C2[World Model]

    D --> D1[Goal Description]
    D --> D2[Search or Planning]

    E --> E1[Utility Function]
    E --> E2[Trade-off Among Goals]

    F --> F1[Critic gives Feedback]
    F --> F2[Learning Element Updates]
    F --> F3[Performance Element Acts]
```

## 4.3 Problem-Solving Agent Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> Shows the canonical three-phase pipeline: Goal → Problem Formulation → Search → Action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Goal Formulation] --> P2[Problem Formulation]
    P2 --> P3[Search]
    P3 --> P4{Goal Reached?}
    P4 -->|No| P5[Execute Next Action]
    P5 --> P6[Percept New State]
    P6 --> P3
    P4 -->|Yes| P7[Stop: Solution Found]
```

## 4.4 Search Algorithm Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Search Algorithms] --> U[Uninformed Blind]
    S --> I[Informed Heuristic]

    U --> U1[BFS]
    U --> U2[Uniform Cost]
    U --> U3[DFS]
    U --> U4[Depth Limited]
    U --> U5[Iterative Deepening]
    U --> U6[Bidirectional]

    I --> I1[Greedy Best First]
    I --> I2[A Star]
    I2 --> I2a[Admissible Heuristic]
    I2 --> I2b[Consistent Heuristic]

    I1 --> I1a[Uses h n Only]
    I2 --> I2c[Uses g n Plus h n]
```

## 4.5 A* Search Frontier Expansion (Schematic of OPEN/CLOSED sets)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[OPEN Priority Queue by f n] -->|Pop min f| E[Expand Node n]
    E --> C[CLOSED Set Visited]
    E --> G[Generate Successors n prime]
    G --> H{Already in OPEN or CLOSED with lower g?}
    H -->|No| I[Insert into OPEN with f n prime equals g n prime plus h n prime]
    H -->|Yes lower g| J[Skip or Update Pointer]
    I --> K{Goal in OPEN with min f?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Return Optimal Path]
    K -->|No| O
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
