# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define an Intelligent Agent. List and briefly explain the four main types of intelligent agents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Intelligent Agent** is any entity that perceives its environment via **sensors** and acts upon it via **actuators**, choosing actions to maximize a **performance measure** (1 mark).

The four types (1.5 marks, 0.5 each if listed with one-line description):

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Reacts to the *current* percept only, using `IF-THEN` condition-action rules. No memory.
2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Maintains an **internal state** that depends on percept history, using a *model* of how the world evolves.
3. **Goal-Based Agent** — Holds an explicit **goal** and uses **search/planning** to find action sequences that achieve the goal.
4. **Utility-Based Agent** — Uses a **utility function** over states to choose the *best* action when trade-offs exist, even when goals are achieved.

(0.5 mark for diagram/sketch or concluding statement distinguishing the progression in capability.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Uninformed and Informed Search Strategies. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uninformed (Blind) Search | Informed (Heuristic) Search |
|--------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Uses problem-specific knowledge? | No | Yes, via heuristic $h(n)$ |
| Information used | Only problem definition | Adds estimate of distance to goal |
| Optimal? | Sometimes (BFS, UCS) | A* with admissible $h$ |
| Examples (1 mark) | BFS, DFS, UCS, IDS, Bidirectional | Greedy Best-First, A* |

(2 marks for the table content; 1 mark for the two examples on each side.)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):**
> Students most often lose marks by: **(i)** forgetting to state the **PEAS specification** explicitly before discussing an agent; **(ii)** writing algorithm *names* without explaining *why* a heuristic is admissible (just stating "it never overestimates" is **not** a proof — show the inequality $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$); **(iii)** mixing up the **f(n), g(n), h(n)** definitions in A* derivations; **(iv)** forgetting to mention whether the answer is for **tree** or **graph** search (admissibility suffices for tree, *consistency* is needed for graph). Always state your assumptions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) Define the PEAS framework. With a suitable example, specify PEAS for an Automated Medical Diagnosis System. Classify its task environment along all seven dimensions. (7 marks)**

**(b) What is a heuristic function? Define admissible and consistent heuristics. Prove that A* tree search with an admissible heuristic returns an optimal solution. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**PEAS Framework (1 mark):**
PEAS stands for **Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors** — the standard descriptors for an AI task environment.

**PEAS for Automated Medical Diagnosis (3 marks):**

- **Performance Measure**: Correct diagnosis rate, minimized false negatives, patient recovery rate, cost of tests.
- **Environment**: Patient, hospital, clinic, medical tests, prior patient history.
- **Actuators**: Display questions, screen for diagnosis, prescription printer, alerts to doctor.
- **Sensors**: Keyboard for patient inputs, touchscreen, microphone (voice symptoms), connection to electronic health records.

**Seven-Dimension Classification (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Classification | Reason |
|-----------|----------------|--------|
| Observable | **Partially** | Symptoms alone don't give full state; tests may be hidden |
| Deterministic | **Stochastic** | Diseases and patient responses are uncertain |
| Episodic | **Sequential** | Earlier questions/tests affect later diagnosis |
| Static | **Dynamic** | Patient's condition can change during diagnosis |
| Discrete | **Discrete** | Symptoms and tests are categorical |
| Single-Agent | **Single-Agent** | Doctor may assist, but optimizing agent is the system |
| Known | **Known** | Medical knowledge base is part of the agent's design |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Heuristic function definition (1 mark):**
A **heuristic function** $h(n)$ estimates the cost of the cheapest path from a node $n$ to a goal. It's problem-specific knowledge that guides search.

**Admissible heuristic (1.5 marks):**
A heuristic $h$ is **admissible** if for every node $n$,

$$
0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)
$$

where $h^*(n)$ is the true optimal cost from $n$ to a goal. Admissibility says $h$ **never overestimates**.

**Consistent (monotone) heuristic (1.5 marks):**
A heuristic $h$ is **consistent** if for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ reachable by action $a$ with cost $c(n, a, n')$

$$
h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')
$$

Consistency implies admissibility, and is needed for **graph-search** optimality of A*.

**Proof of A* Tree Search Optimality (3 marks):**
*Step 1:* Let $C^*$ be the cost of an optimal solution; let $G^*$ be an optimal goal node.
*Step 2:* Suppose for contradiction that A* terminates at a non-optimal goal $G$ with $f(G) = g(G) = C(G) > C^*$.
*Step 3:* At termination, $G^*$ was still on the frontier with $f(G^*) = g(G^*) + h(G^*) = C^* + h(G^*)$. Since $G^*$ is a goal and $h$ is admissible, $h(G^*) = 0$. So $f(G^*) = C^*$.
*Step 4:* Since $f(G) > f(G^*)$, A* would have popped $G^*$ (with smaller $f$) before $G$ — contradiction.
*Step 5:* Therefore A* terminates at an optimal goal. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the structure of an intelligent agent. With neat block diagrams, explain any three types of agents. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compare BFS, DFS, UCS, Greedy Best-First, and A* Search along the dimensions of: completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity. State the role of heuristics in A* search. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Agent Structure (2 marks):**

```
Agent = Architecture + Agent Program
```

- **Architecture** is the physical body — sensors, actuators, computational platform.
- **Agent Program** is the function that maps percept sequences to actions.
- The generic skeleton is: `function AGENT(percept) returns action` that (i) updates internal state, (ii) matches against rules, (iii) returns the chosen action.

**Three types of agents (5 marks, ~1.5–2 marks each):**

1. **Simple Reflex Agent** — Uses condition-action rules; ignores percept history. Suitable for fully observable environments. Diagram: percept → rule base → action. *Example: Thermostat.*

2. **Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Holds an internal state encoding unobserved aspects of the world. Has two models: how the world evolves *independently* and how *the agent's actions* affect the world. Diagram: percept + state → updated state → rules → action. *Example: Self-driving car maintaining position estimate.*

3. **Goal-Based Agent** — Stores goal information, then chooses actions that lead *toward* the goal via search/planning. Diagram: state + goal → search → action selection. *Example: GPS navigation system.*

(0.5 mark reserved for a labelled diagram of any one of the three; full marks require mentioning limitation of each.)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time | Space |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------|-------|
| **BFS** | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes (unit costs) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **DFS** | No | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| **UCS** | Yes | Yes | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| **Greedy Best-First** | No | No | exponential (worst) | $O(b^m)$ |
| **A*** | Yes (if $h$ is admissible for tree; consistent for graph) | Yes (under same conditions) | exponential (worst) | exponential (keeps all generated nodes) |

**Role of Heuristics in A\* (2 marks):**
A* uses $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$, where:
- $g(n)$ = **cost-so-far** (what we've already paid).
- $h(n)$ = **estimated cost-to-go** (heuristic — problem-specific estimate).
- $f(n)$ = **estimated total cost** of the cheapest solution passing through $n$.

A good $h$ is **admissible** (never overestimates) and ideally **consistent** (so $f$ is non-decreasing along paths, enabling efficient graph search). A* with such a heuristic explores only nodes whose $f \leq C^*$, giving optimality *and* efficiency superior to uninformed search.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam, ensure you can tick every box below.**

- [ ] An **intelligent agent** is `architecture + agent program`, mapping percepts → actions to maximize a **performance measure**.
- [ ] **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors — *always start* a problem here.
- [ ] **Four agent types** in increasing sophistication: Simple Reflex → Model-Based → Goal-Based → Utility-Based (and Learning Agent as the fifth).
- [ ] **Environment dimensions** (7): observable, deterministic, episodic, static, discrete, single/multi-agent, known.
- [ ] **Rational** ≠ **omniscient**; rationality depends on the *expected* performance given percept history.
- [ ] A **search problem** is a tuple $\langle S, s_0, A(s), T, G, C \rangle$.
- [ ] **Uninformed search** uses *no* heuristic: BFS, UCS, DFS, DLS, IDS, Bidirectional.
- [ ] **BFS** is complete + optimal for unit costs; time/space $O(b^d)$.
- [ ] **DFS** is space-efficient $O(bm)$ but neither complete nor optimal.
- [ ] **IDS** gives BFS-optimality with DFS-space: complete, optimal (unit costs), space $O(bd)$.
- [ ] **UCS** = Dijkstra-style; expands lowest $g(n)$; optimal for any non-negative step costs.
- [ ] **Informed search** uses $h(n)$: Greedy Best-First (uses $h$ only) and A* (uses $g+h$).
- [ ] **Admissible heuristic**: $0 \leq h(n) \leq h^*(n)$; never overestimates.
- [ ] **Consistent heuristic**: $h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$; implies admissibility + non-decreasing $f$.
- [ ] **A\* tree search + admissible $h$ ⇒ optimal**; **A\* graph search + consistent $h$ ⇒ optimal**.
- [ ] $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$ if $h_2(n) \geq h_1(n)$ for all $n$ (and both admissible) → A* with $h_2$ expands ≤ nodes than with $h_1$.
- [ ] For the **8-puzzle**, $h_1$ (misplaced tiles) and $h_2$ (Manhattan distance) are admissible; $h_2$ dominates $h_1$.
- [ ] **Heuristic quality** is the lever between an infeasible search and a sub-second solution.
- [ ] KTU valuation: always specify **tree vs graph**; always show the **inequality** for admissibility proofs, not just a verbal claim.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Intelligent Agents & Search Algorithms**
> *Best of luck with your KTU 2024 examinations!* 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge Representation and Reasoning - Propositional Logic, First-Order Logic, Reasoning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Knowledge Representation and Reasoning - Propositional Logic, First-Order Logic, Reasoning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR&R)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR&R) is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence dedicated to representing information about the world in a form that a computer system can utilize to solve complex tasks, such as diagnosing a medical condition or holding a natural-language dialogue. It is the formal encoding of knowledge along with the inference machinery to derive new knowledge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *KR&R = Formal Knowledge Base (KB) + Logical Inference Engine.* The KB stores facts and rules; the inference engine reasons over them to produce conclusions.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a detective's notebook. The notebook contains *facts* (the victim was in the garden, the gun is missing) and *rules* (if the victim was shot and the gun is missing, someone took the gun). The detective uses these to *reason* and conclude "the butler did it." KR is the notebook; Reasoning is the deduction.

---

## 1.2 Propositional Logic (PL)

**Formal Definition:**
Propositional Logic (also called **Sentential Logic** or **Boolean Logic**) is a branch of logic that deals with **propositions** — declarative statements that are either strictly **TRUE (T)** or strictly **FALSE (F)**, with no "in-between." It uses logical connectives to combine simple propositions into compound ones.

**Key Components of a Propositional Language:**
- **Atomic Propositions (Atoms):** Indivisible declarative statements (e.g., $P$, $Q$, $R$).
- **Logical Connectives:** $\neg$ (NOT), $\wedge$ (AND), $\vee$ (OR), $\rightarrow$ (IMPLIES), $\leftrightarrow$ (IFF).
- **Well-Formed Formula (WFF):** A syntactically correct expression built from atoms and connectives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Propositional Logic Limitation:** PL cannot express relations between objects or quantify over them. "All humans are mortal" cannot be directly written; it must be enumerated as $H_1 \rightarrow M_1 \wedge H_2 \rightarrow M_2 \wedge \dots$ — this is why FOL exists.

---

## 1.3 First-Order Logic (FOL) / Predicate Logic

**Formal Definition:**
First-Order Logic (FOL), also called **Predicate Calculus**, is an extension of propositional logic that introduces **variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers** ($\forall$, $\exists$). It allows reasoning about *properties of objects* and *relations between objects* in the domain.

**Key Components of FOL:**
- **Constants:** Specific objects (e.g., $John$, $India$).
- **Variables:** $x, y, z$ — represent arbitrary objects.
- **Predicates:** Relations or properties — e.g., $Mortal(x)$, $Loves(x, y)$.
- **Functions:** Map objects to objects — e.g., $Father(x)$.
- **Quantifiers:** $\forall$ (For All — Universal), $\exists$ (There Exists — Existential).

> [!NOTE]
> **"First-Order" Meaning:** Quantifiers can range over **objects**, but **not over predicates or functions**. Second-Order Logic permits that — but is undecidable in general.

**Intuitive Analogy for FOL:**
Propositional logic is like Lego blocks of two colors. FOL is a full Lego kit with **typed pieces, names, and instructions** — you can build specific structures like "for every student, there exists a teacher who teaches them," which is impossible in PL.

---

## 1.4 Reasoning Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A **Reasoning System** (or Inference Engine) is an algorithm that derives new sentences (conclusions) from a knowledge base using formal **inference rules** in a sound and (ideally) complete manner.

**Three Principal Reasoning Strategies:**

| Strategy | Direction | KTU Standard Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Chaining** | Data-Driven (Facts → Conclusions) | Production systems, CLIPS, monitoring |
| **Backward Chaining** | Goal-Driven (Hypothesis → Sub-goals) | Prolog, diagnostic expert systems |
| **Resolution** | Refutation-based (¬Goal + KB → Contradiction) | Automated Theorem Provers, Prolog's engine |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soundness vs. Completeness (Board-Favorite Distinction):**
> - A system is **Sound** if it derives only *true* conclusions (no false positives).
> - A system is **Complete** if it can derive *all* true conclusions (no false negatives).
> Propositional Resolution is **both sound and complete**. Forward Chaining is sound but **not complete** for Horn clauses only when no negation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Syntax of Propositional Logic — Stepwise Construction

A propositional language $\mathcal{L}$ is built using the **Backus–Naur Form (BNF)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sentence} &\rightarrow \text{AtomicSentence} \mid \text{ComplexSentence} \\
\text{AtomicSentence} &\rightarrow \text{True} \mid \text{False} \mid \text{PropSymbol} \\
\text{ComplexSentence} &\rightarrow \text{(Sentence)} \mid \text{Sentence Connective Sentence} \\
\text{Connective} &\rightarrow \wedge \mid \vee \mid \Rightarrow \mid \Leftrightarrow \mid \neg
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operator Precedence (Highest → Lowest):**
1. $\neg$ (NOT)
2. $\wedge$ (AND)
3. $\vee$ (OR)
4. $\Rightarrow$ (IMPLIES)
5. $\Leftrightarrow$ (IFF)

## 2.2 Logical Connectives — Truth Table Cheat Sheet

| $P$ | $Q$ | $\neg P$ | $P \wedge Q$ | $P \vee Q$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $P \leftrightarrow Q$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | F | T | T | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | T | F | F |
| F | T | T | F | T | T | F |
| F | F | T | F | F | T | T |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** $P \rightarrow Q$ is logically equivalent to $\neg P \vee Q$. This is heavily used in CNF conversion.

## 2.3 Validity, Satisfiability, and Entailment — KTU Definitions

| Concept | Notation | Meaning | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Valid (Tautology)** | $\models \alpha$ | True in **every** model | "True always" |
| **Satisfiable** | $M(\alpha) = \text{T}$ | True in **at least one** model | "Has a witness" |
| **Unsatisfiable (Contradiction)** | $\not\models \alpha$ | True in **no** model | "Contradiction" |
| **Entailment** | $KB \models \alpha$ | $\alpha$ is true in **all** models where $KB$ is true | "KB implies $\alpha$" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Cornerstones of Logical Inference (KTU board-favorite trio):**
> 1. **Logical Entailment:** $KB \models \alpha$
> 2. **Deduction Theorem:** $KB \models \alpha \iff \models (KB \rightarrow \alpha)$
> 3. **Model Checking:** Enumerate models where $KB$ holds and check $\alpha$.

## 2.4 First-Order Logic — Syntactic Building Blocks (BNF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sentence} &\rightarrow \text{AtomicSentence} \mid \text{ComplexSentence} \\
\text{AtomicSentence} &\rightarrow \text{Predicate}(\text{Term}, \dots) \mid \text{Function}(\text{Term}, \dots) \\
\text{Term} &\rightarrow \text{Function}(\text{Term}, \dots) \mid \text{Constant} \mid \text{Variable} \\
\text{ComplexSentence} &\rightarrow \text{(Sentence)} \mid \text{Sentence Connective Sentence} \\
&\quad \mid \text{Quantifier Variable, Sentence} \\
\text{Quantifier} &\rightarrow \forall \mid \exists \\
\text{Connective} &\rightarrow \wedge \mid \vee \mid \Rightarrow \mid \Leftrightarrow \mid \neg
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Expression / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modus Ponens | From $P \rightarrow Q$ and $P$, infer $Q$ | Most-used rule |
| 2 | Modus Tollens | From $P \rightarrow Q$ and $\neg Q$, infer $\neg P$ | Used in backward chaining |
| 3 | And-Elimination | From $P \wedge Q$, infer $P$ (or $Q$) | Fact extraction |
| 4 | Universal Elimination | From $\forall x \; P(x)$, infer $P(c)$ for any constant $c$ | Instantiate variable |
| 5 | Existential Introduction | From $P(c)$, infer $\exists x \; P(x)$ | Reverse of above |
| 6 | Implication equivalence | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ | CNF conversion |
| 7 | Biconditional equivalence | $P \leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$ | CNF conversion |
| 8 | Negation of $\forall$ | $\neg \forall x \; P(x) \equiv \exists x \; \neg P(x)$ | De Morgan for quantifiers |
| 9 | Negation of $\exists$ | $\neg \exists x \; P(x) \equiv \forall x \; \neg P(x)$ | De Morgan for quantifiers |
| 10 | Unification | $\text{UNIFY}(p, q) = \theta$ s.t. $p\theta = q\theta$ | Engine of FOL inference |

## 2.6 Horn Clauses — The "Industry Standard" Subset

A **Horn Clause** is a clause (disjunction of literals) with **at most one positive literal**.

$$
(P_1 \wedge P_2 \wedge \dots \wedge P_n) \rightarrow Q
$$

- **Definite Horn Clause:** Exactly one positive literal — used in forward/backward chaining.
- **Goal Clause:** Zero positive literals — used in refutation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Propositional logic restricted to Horn clauses is **decidable in linear time** — that's why production systems prefer them. Deciding general propositional logic is **NP-complete** (SAT problem).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of KR&R |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis (MYCIN)** | Backward chaining over rules to identify bacterial infections |
| **Semantic Web (RDF/OWL)** | FOL-based representation for linked open data |
| **Software Verification (Coq, Isabelle)** | Higher-order logic for proving program correctness |
| **Robotics (STRIPS, PDDL)** | FOL planning languages for autonomous agents |
| **Cybersecurity (Snort IDS)** | Propositional rule-based intrusion detection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Proofs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Propositional Logic Inference Using Modus Ponens

**Problem:** From the knowledge base below, prove $Q$.

**Knowledge Base (KB):**
1. $P$
2. $P \rightarrow Q$
3. $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R$

**Step-by-Step Derivation (with explicit justifications):**

| Step | Derived Sentence | Justification / Rule | Premise References |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $P$ | Premise (Given) | (1) |
| 2 | $P \rightarrow Q$ | Premise (Given) | (2) |
| 3 | $Q$ | **Modus Ponens** applied to (1) and (2) | (1), (2) |

**Conclusion:** $KB \models Q$ — derived in **3 steps**, hence proven.

---

## 3.2 Conversion to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) — Full Algorithm

**Why CNF?** Resolution refutation requires the KB to be in CNF. CNF is a **conjunction of disjunctions of literals**:
$$
(C_{1,1} \vee C_{1,2} \vee \dots) \wedge \dots \wedge (C_{n,1} \vee \dots)
$$

**Algorithm (6 mandatory steps):**

1. **Eliminate $\rightarrow$ and $\leftrightarrow$** using equivalences.
2. **Move $\neg$ inwards** (apply De Morgan's laws + quantifier negation rules).
3. **Standardize variables** (rename bound variables to be unique).
4. **Skolemize** (eliminate $\exists$ by introducing Skolem functions/constants).
5. **Drop universal quantifiers** (assume all remaining variables are universally quantified).
6. **Distribute $\wedge$ over $\vee$** to get CNF.

**Worked Example:** Convert $\forall x \; [P(x) \rightarrow \exists y \; (Q(x,y) \wedge \neg R(x,y))]$ to CNF.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Eliminate } &\rightarrow \\
&\forall x \; [\neg P(x) \vee \exists y \; (Q(x,y) \wedge \neg R(x,y))]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Negation inwards} &\text{ — no outer negation present.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Skolemize} \quad & \text{Introduce Skolem function } f(x) \text{ for } y \text{ (y depends on x).} \\
&\forall x \; [\neg P(x) \vee (Q(x, f(x)) \wedge \neg R(x, f(x)))]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 5: Drop } \forall &\text{ quantifier.} \\
&\neg P(x) \vee (Q(x, f(x)) \wedge \neg R(x, f(x)))
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 6: Distribute } \vee \text{ over } \wedge &\text{ using the distributive law:} \\
&(A \vee (B \wedge C)) \equiv (A \vee B) \wedge (A \vee C) \\
\text{Resulting CNF:} \quad & [\neg P(x) \vee Q(x, f(x))] \wedge [\neg P(x) \vee \neg R(x, f(x))]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common student error is forgetting to introduce a **Skolem function** when the existential variable is inside a universal quantifier's scope. The variable must be replaced by a function of the *outer universally quantified variable(s)*.

---

## 3.3 Resolution Refutation — Full Worked Proof

**Theorem to Prove (from Russell & Norvig, KTU standard):**
*"West is a criminal."*

**KB Sentences:**
- (1) It is a crime for an American to sell weapons to hostile nations. $\quad \forall x, y, z \; [\text{American}(x) \wedge \text{Weapon}(y) \wedge \text{Sells}(x, y, z) \wedge \text{Hostile}(z)] \rightarrow \text{Criminal}(x)$
- (2) The country Nono, an enemy of America, has some missiles. $\quad \forall x \; [\text{Enemy}(x, \text{America}) \rightarrow \text{Hostile}(x)]$
- (3) All its missiles were sold to it by Colonel West. $\quad \forall x \; [\text{Missile}(x) \wedge \text{Owns}(Nono, x) \rightarrow \text{Sells}(\text{West}, x, Nono)]$
- (4) Missiles are weapons. $\quad \forall x \; [\text{Missile}(x) \rightarrow \text{Weapon}(x)]$
- (5) An enemy of America counts as hostile. $\quad \text{Enemy}(Nono, \text{America})$

**Proof Strategy:** To prove $KB \models \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$, we add $\neg \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$ to KB and derive a contradiction (empty clause $\square$).

**Resolution Proof Steps:**

| Step | Clause Resolved | Substitution $\theta$ | Resulting Clause |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\neg \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$ (negated goal) | — | $\neg \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$ |
| 2 | Resolve (1) with negated $\text{Criminal}(\text{West})$: substitute $x/\text{West}$ | $\{x \mapsto \text{West}\}$ | $\neg \text{American}(\text{West}) \vee \neg \text{Weapon}(y) \vee \neg \text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, z) \vee \neg \text{Hostile}(z)$ |
| 3 | Resolve with $\text{Enemy}(Nono, \text{America})$ and rule (2) | $\{x \mapsto Nono, z \mapsto Nono\}$ | $\neg \text{American}(\text{West}) \vee \neg \text{Weapon}(y) \vee \neg \text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, Nono)$ |
| 4 | Unify with rule (3): $\text{Missile}(y) \wedge \text{Owns}(Nono, y) \rightarrow \text{Sells}(\text{West}, y, Nono)$ | — | $\neg \text{American}(\text{West}) \vee \neg \text{Weapon}(y) \vee \neg \text{Missile}(y) \vee \neg \text{Owns}(Nono, y)$ |
| 5 | Apply rule (4) repeatedly | — | $\neg \text{American}(\text{West}) \vee \neg \text{Owns}(Nono, y) \vee \neg \text{Missile}(y)$ |
| 6 | Fact: $\text{Owns}(Nono, M_1)$ and $\text{Missile}(M_1)$ | $\{y \mapsto M_1\}$ | $\neg \text{American}(\text{West})$ |
| 7 | Fact: $\text{American}(\text{West})$ | — | $\square$ (Empty clause → **Contradiction**) |

**Conclusion:** The empty clause $\square$ proves that $KB \cup \{\neg \text{Criminal}(\text{West})\}$ is unsatisfiable, hence $KB \models \text{Criminal}(\text{West})$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Forward Chaining Algorithm — Step-by-Step

**Algorithm (Pseudo-formal, used in production systems):**

```
FORWARD-CHAIN(KB):
    REPEAT
        FOR each rule (P1 ∧ P2 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) → Q in KB DO
            FOR each fact P in KB DO
                Remove P from condition list of rule
            END FOR
            IF all Pi present THEN
                ADD Q to KB
                IF Q = Goal THEN RETURN Success
            END IF
        END FOR
    UNTIL no new facts added
    RETURN Failure
```

**Worked Example (KTU Typical Question):**

**KB:**
- (R1) $A \wedge B \rightarrow C$
- (R2) $C \wedge D \rightarrow E$
- (R3) $B \wedge E \rightarrow F$
- Facts: $\{A, B, D\}$

**Trace:**

| Iteration | Rule Fired | Pre-conditions Checked | New Fact Added |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | R1 | $A$ ✓, $B$ ✓ | $C$ |
| 2 | R2 | $C$ ✓, $D$ ✓ | $E$ |
| 3 | R3 | $B$ ✓, $E$ ✓ | $F$ |
| 4 | — | No new rule fires | **STOP** |

**Final KB:** $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ — $F$ is the answer.

---

## 3.5 Backward Chaining Algorithm — Step-by-Step

```
BACKWARD-CHAIN(KB, Goal):
    IF Goal in KB facts THEN RETURN True
    FOR each rule (P1 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) → Goal in KB DO
        IF BACKWARD-CHAIN(KB, P1) AND ... AND BACKWARD-CHAIN(KB, Pn) THEN
            RETURN True
    RETURN False
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Comparison Insight:** Backward chaining is **goal-directed** — it only explores the *relevant* rule space. Forward chaining is **data-driven** — it explores *all* matching rules. Prolog uses backward chaining; CLIPS uses forward chaining.

---

## 3.6 Unification Algorithm — Analytical Matrix

The **Unification Algorithm** finds the Most General Unifier (MGU) — the simplest substitution $\theta$ that makes two expressions syntactically identical.

**Pseudocode (Robinson's Algorithm):**

```
UNIFY(x, y, θ = {}):
    IF x = y THEN RETURN θ
    IF x is variable THEN RETURN UNIFY-VAR(x, y, θ)
    IF y is variable THEN RETURN UNIFY-VAR(y, x, θ)
    IF x = (x1, ..., xn) AND y = (y1, ..., yn) THEN
        RETURN UNIFY(xn, yn, UNIFY(..., ...))
    RETURN FAILURE

UNIFY-VAR(var, x, θ):
    IF var occurs in x (OCCUR-CHECK) THEN RETURN FAILURE
    Add substitution {var/x} to θ
    Apply θ to remaining expressions
    RETURN updated θ
```

**Worked Example:** Unify $P(x, f(y), B)$ with $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$.

| Step | Substitution | Result of Substituted Sides | Status |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\{x \mapsto A\}$ | $P(A, f(y), B)$ vs $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ | Partial |
| 2 | $\{y \mapsto g(z)\}$ | $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ vs $P(A, f(g(z)), B)$ | **Unified** |

**MGU:** $\theta = \{x \mapsto A, \; y \mapsto g(z)\}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **Occurs Check** is mandatory. Unifying $x$ with $F(x)$ would create an infinite term $F(F(F(\dots)))$. Standard Prolog *omits* the occurs check for efficiency — a famous logic-programming quirk.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Reasoning System to Choose?

| Criterion | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data → Goals | Goals → Sub-goals | Refutation |
| **Best For** | Monitoring, Planning | Diagnosis, Query answering | Theorem proving |
| **Completeness (Horn)** | Yes (when KB entails goal) | Yes | Yes (general FOL) |
| **Decidability (FOL)** | Semi-decidable | Semi-decidable | Semi-decidable |
| **Implementation** | Rete Algorithm | Recursive Search | SAT/SMT Solvers |
| **Example System** | CLIPS, Drools | Prolog | Vampire, E, Lean |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Knowledge Representation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Knowledge Representation Languages] --> B[Propositional Logic PL]
    A --> C[First Order Logic FOL]
    A --> D[Higher Order Logic HOL]
    A --> E[Non Monotonic Logic]
    B --> B1[Atomic Propositions]
    B --> B2[Logical Connectives]
    B --> B3[Truth Values TRUE FALSE]
    C --> C1[Constants and Variables]
    C --> C2[Predicates and Functions]
    C --> C3[Quantifiers FORALL EXISTS]
    C2 --> C2a[Unification]
    C2 --> C2b[Resolution]
    D --> D1[Quantifies over Predicates]
    D --> D2[Expressive but Undecidable]
    E --> E1[Default Logic]
    E --> E2[Modal Logic]
    E --> E3[Circumscription]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Reasoning Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input Facts and Rules] --> KB[Knowledge Base KB]
    KB --> INF[Inference Engine]
    INF --> FC[Forward Chaining Module]
    INF --> BC[Backward Chaining Module]
    INF --> RES[Resolution Refutation Module]
    FC --> MATCH[Pattern Matcher RETE]
    BC --> GOAL[Goal Stack]
    RES --> UNIF[Unification Algorithm]
    MATCH --> OUT[Derived Conclusions]
    GOAL --> OUT
    UNIF --> OUT
    OUT --> VAL[Validation Layer Soundness Check]
    VAL --> RES2[Final Result]
```

## 4.3 Forward Chaining — Data-Driven Inference Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Initial Facts] --> B[Scan Rule Base]
    B --> C{All Premises<br>Satisfied?}
    C -- No --> D[Skip Rule]
    D --> B
    C -- Yes --> E[Fire Rule]
    E --> F[Add Conclusion to KB]
    F --> G{New Goal<br>Reached?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Return SUCCESS]
    G -- No --> I{New Facts<br>Added?}
    I -- Yes --> B
    I -- No --> J[Return FAILURE]
```

## 4.4 Backward Chaining — Goal-Driven Inference Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    G[Goal Query] --> H{Goal is<br>Known Fact?}
    H -- Yes --> Z[Return TRUE]
    H -- No --> R[Find Rules with<br>Goal in Head]
    R --> S[Decompose Body into<br>Sub Goals]
    S --> T[Recursive Call on<br>Each Sub Goal]
    T --> U{All Sub Goals<br>Proven?}
    U -- Yes --> Z
    U -- No --> V[Return FALSE]
```

## 4.5 Resolution Refutation — Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input KB in English] --> B[Translate to FOL]
    B --> C[Negate Goal]
    C --> D[Convert to CNF]
    D --> E[Skolemize]
    E --> F[Apply Resolution Rule]
    F --> G{Empty Clause<br>Derived?}
    G -- Yes --> H[GOAL PROVED]
    G -- No --> I{More Clauses<br>to Resolve?}
    I -- Yes --> F
    I -- No --> J[GOAL NOT PROVABLE]
```

## 4.6 Inference Rule Network — Modus Ponens, Tollens, and Chaining

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[P] --> MP[Modus Ponens]
    IMP[P IMPLIES Q] --> MP
    MP --> Q[Q]
    Q --> MT[Modus Tollens]
    IMP --> MT
    NEGQ[NOT Q] --> MT
    MT --> NOTP[NOT P]
    P1[P1] --> ANDEL[AND Elimination]
    P2[P2] --> ANDEL
    ANDEL --> OUT1[P1 or P2]
    FORALL[FORALL x P of x] --> UE[Universal Elimination]
    UE --> OUT2[P of c]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Knowledge Representation. List any four properties a good knowledge representation system should possess.**

**Model Answer:**

**Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the field of AI concerned with encoding information about the world into a formal language that a computer can use to solve complex tasks, combined with inference procedures to derive new information.

**Four Desirable Properties of a Good KR System (Stated by KTU Board):**
1. **Representational Adequacy:** Ability to represent all kinds of required knowledge.
2. **Inferential Adequacy:** Ability to manipulate the represented knowledge to derive new knowledge.
3. **Inferential Efficiency:** Ability to incorporate heuristics for efficient reasoning.
4. **Acquisitional Efficiency:** Ability to acquire new knowledge easily (via learning or direct insertion).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **R-I-I-A** → Representational, Inferential, Inferential (Efficiency), Acquisitional.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Propositional Logic and First-Order Logic. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Propositional Logic (PL) | First-Order Logic (FOL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Unit** | Atomic propositions (whole facts) | Predicates applied to terms |
| **Variables** | None | $x, y, z$ allowed |
| **Quantifiers** | Not available | $\forall$ (For All), $\exists$ (There Exists) |
| **Expressive Power** | Limited — cannot represent "All X are Y" | Can express general rules and relations |
| **Example (Natural Language)** | "It is raining." | "For all $x$, if $x$ is a human, then $x$ is mortal." |
| **Formal Example** | $P$: It is raining. | $\forall x \; (Human(x) \rightarrow Mortal(x))$ |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** 14-mark questions typically have two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both parts must be answered.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the following sentence into CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form). Show every step clearly.

> *"Every person who likes all animals is loved by someone. No person who kills an animal is loved by anyone."*

Use the following predicates:
- $Person(x)$, $Animal(y)$, $Loves(x,y)$, $Kills(x,y)$, $\exists z \; Loves(z,x)$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Formalize in FOL.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{P1:} \quad & [\forall x \; [Person(x) \wedge \forall y \; (Animal(y) \rightarrow Loves(x,y))] \rightarrow \exists y \; Loves(y,x)] \\
\text{P2:} \quad & \neg \exists x \; [Person(x) \wedge \exists y \; (Animal(y) \wedge Kills(x,y)) \wedge \forall z \; Loves(z,x)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Eliminate $\rightarrow$ and biconditionals (P1):**

$$
\forall x \; [\neg (Person(x) \wedge \forall y \; (\neg Animal(y) \vee Loves(x,y))) \vee \exists y \; Loves(y,x)]
$$

**Step 3: Push $\neg$ inwards (P2):**

$$
\forall x \; [\neg Person(x) \vee \forall y \; (Animal(y) \rightarrow \neg Kills(x,y)) \vee \exists z \; \neg Loves(z,x)]
$$

After applying equivalences on the $\neg (A \wedge B) \equiv \neg A \vee \neg B$ pattern in P1:

$$
\forall x \; [\neg Person(x) \vee \exists y \; \neg Loves(x,y) \vee \exists y \; Loves(y,x)]
$$

**Step 4: Skolemize.**

Introduce Skolem function $F(x)$ for the existential $y$ in P1 and $G(x)$ in P2:

$$
\forall x \; [\neg Person(x) \vee \neg Loves(x, F(x)) \vee Loves(G(x), x)]
$$

**Step 5: Drop universal quantifiers and write as CNF clauses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C1:} \quad & \neg Person(x) \vee \neg Loves(x, F(x)) \vee Loves(G(x), x) \\
\text{C2:} \quad & \neg Person(x) \vee Animal(y) \vee \neg Kills(x, y) \vee \neg Loves(z, x)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply **Resolution Refutation** to prove: *"If the course is easy, then some students are happy. The course is easy. Therefore, some students are happy."*

Use predicates: $Easy(c)$, $Student(s)$, $Happy(s)$.

**Model Solution:**

**KB in FOL:**
1. $Easy(c)$
2. $\forall s \; [Student(s) \rightarrow Happy(s)] \quad \text{(if student, happy)}$

**Goal:** $\exists s \; (Student(s) \wedge Happy(s))$

**Refutation Method — Add $\neg Goal$ and derive contradiction:**

**Step 1:** Negate the goal:

$$
\neg \exists s \; (Student(s) \wedge Happy(s)) \equiv \forall s \; (\neg Student(s) \vee \neg Happy(s))
$$

**Step 2:** Convert KB to CNF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C1:} \quad & Easy(c) \\
\text{C2:} \quad & \neg Student(s) \vee Happy(s) \\
\text{C3:} \quad & \neg Student(s) \vee \neg Happy(s) \quad \text{(from negated goal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Resolution Steps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3.1:} \quad & \text{Resolve C2 and C3 on } Happy(s): \\
& \theta = \{s \mapsto s_0\} \text{ (fresh variable)} \\
& \text{Result: } \neg Student(s_0)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3.2:} \quad & \text{Resolve with premise (implicitly assume some student exists as a fact } Student(John)) \\
& \text{Result: } \square \text{ (Empty clause)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The empty clause $\square$ confirms $KB \models \exists s \; (Student(s) \wedge Happy(s))$. The theorem is **proven**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the goal** before refutation — the most common 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Skolemizing incorrectly**: Existential variables *inside* a universal quantifier must be replaced by a *function* of the outer variable, not by a fresh constant.
> 3. **Missing the Occurs Check** in unification: Unifying $x$ with $P(x)$ must FAIL.
> 4. **Implication mis-conversion**: $A \rightarrow B$ is $\neg A \vee B$, **not** $A \vee \neg B$.
> 5. **CNF step skipped**: Resolution requires CNF — applying it on raw FOL is a major mark loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Forward Chaining** and **Backward Chaining** with their algorithms. Compare them on **five parameters**.

**Model Answer:**

**Forward Chaining (FC):** A *data-driven* reasoning method. It starts from known facts and applies rules repeatedly to derive new facts until the goal is reached or no new facts can be derived.

**Algorithm (KTU Standard Pseudocode):**

```
FC(KB, Goal):
    Agenda ← all facts in KB
    WHILE Agenda not empty DO
        fact ← POP(Agenda)
        IF fact = Goal THEN RETURN TRUE
        FOR each rule (P1 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) → Q in KB DO
            IF fact is one of the Pi THEN
                IF all Pi are in KB THEN
                    Add Q to KB
                    PUSH(Q, Agenda)
        END FOR
    END WHILE
    RETURN FALSE
```

**Backward Chaining (BC):** A *goal-driven* reasoning method. It starts from the goal and works backward, finding rules that could conclude the goal, then recursively trying to prove the rule's premises.

**Algorithm:**

```
BC(KB, Goal):
    IF Goal is a fact in KB THEN RETURN TRUE
    FOR each rule (P1 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) → Goal in KB DO
        IF BC(KB, P1) AND ... AND BC(KB, Pn) THEN
            RETURN TRUE
    RETURN FALSE
```

**Comparison on Five Parameters:**

| Parameter | Forward Chaining | Backward Chaining |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Data-driven (bottom-up) | Goal-driven (top-down) |
| **Search Strategy** | Breadth-First over facts | Depth-First over goals |
| **Best Suited For** | Monitoring, Planning, Control | Diagnosis, Query Answering, Prolog |
| **Number of Inferences** | May derive many irrelevant facts | Only derives facts needed for the goal |
| **Termination Guarantee** | May not terminate on large KB | More controlled recursion depth |
| **Conflict Resolution** | Required (multiple rules may fire) | Usually not required (Prolog's depth-first) |
| **Typical Use** | CLIPS, Drools, Rete-based systems | Prolog, Datalog, expert system shells |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Perform **Unification** on the following pairs and show the MGU (Most General Unifier):

(i) $P(x, y, B, B)$ with $P(A, z, z, w)$

(ii) $Q(F(x), G(y))$ with $Q(F(A), u)$

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Unify $P(x, y, B, B)$ with $P(A, z, z, w)$**

| Position | Substituted Sides | Substitution | Result |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $x$ vs $A$ | $\{x \mapsto A\}$ | $P(A, y, B, B)$ vs $P(A, z, z, w)$ |
| 2 | $y$ vs $z$ | $\{y \mapsto z\}$ | $P(A, z, B, B)$ vs $P(A, z, z, w)$ |
| 3 | $B$ vs $z$ | $\{z \mapsto B\}$ | $P(A, B, B, B)$ vs $P(A, B, B, w)$ |
| 4 | $B$ vs $w$ | $\{w \mapsto B\}$ | $P(A, B, B, B)$ vs $P(A, B, B, B)$ ✓ |

**MGU $\theta_1$:** $\{x \mapsto A, \; y \mapsto B, \; z \mapsto B, \; w \mapsto B\}$

**(ii) Unify $Q(F(x), G(y))$ with $Q(F(A), u)$**

| Position | Substituted Sides | Substitution | Result |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $F(x)$ vs $F(A)$ | $\{x \mapsto A\}$ | $Q(F(A), G(y))$ vs $Q(F(A), u)$ |
| 2 | $G(y)$ vs $u$ | $\{u \mapsto G(y)\}$ | $Q(F(A), G(y))$ vs $Q(F(A), G(y))$ ✓ |

**MGU $\theta_2$:** $\{x \mapsto A, \; u \mapsto G(y)\}$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** In (ii), $y$ remains un-unified because the second expression has the *variable* $u$, not a concrete function application — so the algorithm binds $u$ to whatever $G(y)$ becomes.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Save This Section!**

- **Knowledge Representation (KR):** Encoding knowledge formally. *Properties:* Representational, Inferential, Inferential Efficiency, Acquisitional adequacy.
- **Propositional Logic:** Uses atoms $P, Q, R$ and connectives $\neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$. Truth values: T/F only.
- **First-Order Logic (FOL):** Adds **constants, variables, predicates, functions, quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$).
- **Key Equivalences:** $A \rightarrow B \equiv \neg A \vee B$; $\neg \forall x \; P(x) \equiv \exists x \; \neg P(x)$; $\neg \exists x \; P(x) \equiv \forall x \; \neg P(x)$.
- **Modus Ponens:** $A, A \rightarrow B \vdash B$. **Modus Tollens:** $A \rightarrow B, \neg B \vdash \neg A$.
- **CNF Conversion:** 6 mandatory steps — Eliminate $\rightarrow$ → Negation inwards → Standardize variables → Skolemize → Drop $\forall$ → Distribute $\vee$ over $\wedge$.
- **Skolemization Rule:** Replace $\exists y$ with **constant** if no outer $\forall$; replace with **function of outer universally quantified variables** if nested.
- **Resolution:** Single inference rule; clause + complementary literal unify → resolvent. Refutation proves by deriving empty clause $\square$.
- **Horn Clauses:** At most one positive literal. Linear time inference. Used in production systems.
- **Forward Chaining:** Data-driven, fires all matching rules, breadth-first, complete for Horn KBs.
- **Backward Chaining:** Goal-driven, depth-first, only explores relevant rules, Prolog-style.
- **Unification:** MGU = Most General Unifier. **Occurs Check** mandatory; Prolog omits it for speed.
- **Soundness:** Only true conclusions derived. **Completeness:** All true conclusions derivable.
- **Decidability:** PL is decidable (SAT — NP-complete). PL with Horn clauses is decidable in linear time. FOL is **semi-decidable**.
- **Real-World Systems:** CLIPS, Drools (FC); Prolog (BC); Lean, Coq, Isabelle (Theorem Provers); RDF/OWL (Semantic Web).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Last-Word Mantra:** *"If the question asks for inference — show Modus Ponens. If it asks for theorem proving — show Resolution with CNF. If it asks 'which reasoning system' — match to use case."* $\square$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Machine Learning: Machine Learning vs. Traditional Programming, Types of Learning (Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Machine Learning: Machine Learning vs. Traditional Programming, Types of Learning (Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement Learning)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Traditional Programming — The Classical Way

**Traditional Programming** (also called *rule-based* or *deterministic programming*) is the classical paradigm where a human developer writes explicit instructions (rules) that take input data and produce an output. The developer must fully understand the problem and hand-code the logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> Traditional Programming is a paradigm in which the **Program (Logic + Rules)** and the **Input Data** are both fed into a computer, which then produces the **Output**. The logic is hand-crafted by the programmer.

The classical equation representing traditional programming is:

$$\text{Output} = \text{Program}(\text{Input})$$

Here, the **Program** is essentially a function $f$ hand-coded by a human, i.e.,

$$y = f(x)$$

where $f$ is explicitly defined using `if-else`, loops, mathematical formulas, etc.

## 1.2 Machine Learning — The Self-Learning Way

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables computers to learn patterns and make decisions **from data** without being explicitly programmed for every rule. Instead of writing the function, the machine *discovers* the function from examples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Arthur Samuel, 1959 — KTU Board Favourite):**
> *"Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*

> [!NOTE]
> **More formal (Mitchell, 1997):**
> *"A computer program is said to learn from experience $E$ with respect to some class of tasks $T$ and performance measure $P$, if its performance at tasks in $T$, as measured by $P$, improves with experience $E$."*

The governing equation of Machine Learning reverses the traditional one:

$$\text{Program} = \text{Machine}(\text{Input},\ \text{Output})$$

The machine figures out the function $f$ such that $y \approx f(x)$ using historical $(x, y)$ pairs.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe vs. Taste Test"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Teaching a child to identify fruits 🍎🍌**
>
> - **Traditional Programming:** You write a *huge rulebook*: "If the object is round, red, and has a small stem → it is an Apple." You must anticipate every fruit and every feature.
> - **Machine Learning:** You show the child **1000 pictures of apples and bananas** along with their labels. The child (model) *learns* the distinguishing features by itself. Next time it sees a new fruit, it predicts the label from experience.
>
> In short: **Traditional Programming = You write the rules. Machine Learning = The machine learns the rules.**

## 1.4 Why Machine Learning? (Motivation)

- Problems too complex for manual rules (e.g., image recognition, speech, fraud detection).
- Systems that must **adapt** to changing data (spam filters, recommendation engines).
- Problems where human expertise does not exist or is hard to articulate (genomics, cosmic phenomena).
- It is the engine behind modern AI: Chatbots, self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, predictive analytics.

## 1.5 Machine Learning vs. Traditional Programming — Comparison Table

| Aspect | Traditional Programming | Machine Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic Source** | Hand-coded by human | Learned from data |
| **Input** | Data + Program | Data + Output (examples) |
| **Output** | Result / Answer | Result + a learned model (rules) |
| **Adaptability** | Poor (needs reprogramming) | Excellent (retrain on new data) |
| **Data Dependency** | Low | Very high |
| **Example** | Calculator, Sorting | Spam filter, Face recognition |
| **Equation** | $y = f(x)$ where $f$ is given | $f = \text{Learn}(X, Y)$ |
| **Error Handling** | Explicit `try-catch` | Statistical / probabilistic |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of a Machine Learning System

A typical ML system follows a 6-step pipeline:

1. **Data Collection** — Gather raw, relevant datasets $(x_i, y_i)$.
2. **Data Pre-processing** — Clean, normalize, handle missing values, encode categorical variables.
3. **Feature Engineering** — Extract or select the most informative features $x$.
4. **Model Selection** — Choose an algorithm (Linear Regression, Decision Tree, Neural Network, etc.).
5. **Training** — The model learns parameters $\theta$ by minimizing a loss function.
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Test on unseen data, then deploy for inference.

## 2.2 The Core Learning Objective

Almost every ML algorithm solves an **optimization problem**. Given a dataset:

$$D = \{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}),\ (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}),\ \dots,\ (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$$

where $m$ is the number of training examples, the goal is to find a hypothesis function $h_\theta(x)$ such that:

$$h_\theta(x) \approx y$$

The **cost (loss) function** $J(\theta)$ measures the error between predicted and actual values. For regression, the most common choice is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

The learning algorithm adjusts $\theta$ to **minimize** $J(\theta)$:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} J(\theta)$$

This is typically done via **Gradient Descent**:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (a small positive scalar, typically $0 < \alpha \le 1$).

## 2.3 Types of Machine Learning — The Three Pillars

Machine Learning is broadly classified into **three** primary types based on the nature of the learning signal (feedback) available to the system.

### 2.3.1 Supervised Learning
- The model is trained on **labeled data** — every input $x$ has a corresponding known output $y$ (the "supervisor" or "teacher").
- **Goal:** Learn the mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$.
- **Two sub-tasks:**
  - **Classification** — Output $y$ is a discrete category (e.g., spam / not-spam, cat / dog).
  - **Regression** — Output $y$ is a continuous real number (e.g., house price, temperature).
- **Algorithms:** Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, k-NN, Random Forest, Neural Networks.
- **Use case:** Email spam detection, medical diagnosis, credit scoring.

### 2.3.2 Unsupervised Learning
- The model is given **unlabeled data** — only inputs $x$, no corresponding $y$.
- **Goal:** Discover hidden structure, patterns, or groupings in the data.
- **Common sub-tasks:**
  - **Clustering** — Group similar data points (e.g., customer segmentation).
  - **Dimensionality Reduction** — Compress high-dimensional data (e.g., PCA, t-SNE).
  - **Association** — Find rules like "people who buy X also buy Y".
- **Algorithms:** K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical Clustering, PCA, Apriori, Autoencoders.
- **Use case:** Market basket analysis, anomaly detection, gene sequencing.

### 2.3.3 Reinforcement Learning (RL)
- An **agent** interacts with an **environment**, takes **actions**, and receives **rewards** (or penalties).
- **Goal:** Learn a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ that maximizes the cumulative long-term reward.
- **Key elements:** Agent, Environment, State $s_t$, Action $a_t$, Reward $R_t$, Policy $\pi$.
- **Algorithms:** Q-Learning, SARSA, Deep Q-Network (DQN), Policy Gradient, Actor-Critic.
- **Use case:** Game playing (AlphaGo), robotics, self-driving cars, dynamic pricing.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Traditional Programming** | $y = f(x)$, $f$ hand-coded | Logic given by programmer |
| **Machine Learning** | $f = \text{Learn}(X, Y)$ | Function discovered from data |
| **Hypothesis Function** | $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ (linear) | Approximates true mapping |
| **Loss Function (MSE)** | $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$ | Used in regression |
| **Gradient Descent Update** | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Iterative minimization |
| **Learning Rate** | $\alpha \in (0, 1]$, typically $\alpha = 0.01$ to $0.1$ | Step size in parameter space |
| **Supervised Learning** | Labeled: $D = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$ | $y$ is the teacher signal |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | Unlabeled: $D = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$ | No teacher signal |
| **RL Cumulative Reward** | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ = discount factor |
| **RL Policy** | $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$ | Probabilistic action selector |
| **Mitchell's Definition** | Learns task $T$ from experience $E$ measured by $P$ | KTU favourite one-liner |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | ML Type Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Healthcare | Supervised | Disease prediction from X-rays |
| Finance | Supervised + Unsupervised | Credit scoring + fraud anomaly detection |
| E-commerce | Unsupervised | Customer segmentation, recommendation |
| Automotive (Self-driving) | Reinforcement | Lane keeping, obstacle avoidance |
| Manufacturing | Unsupervised | Defect detection via clustering |
| Gaming | Reinforcement | AlphaGo, OpenAI Five |
| NLP | Supervised | Chatbots, sentiment analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility in engineering:** Modern *Industry 4.0* smart factories use a **hybrid of all three** — supervised models predict equipment failure, unsupervised models detect anomalous sensor patterns, and RL agents dynamically optimize HVAC / energy consumption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Traditional Programming to the ML Equation

Let us rigorously derive why Machine Learning "inverts" the traditional programming equation.

### Step 1 — Start with the traditional paradigm
A human designs a function $f$ based on domain knowledge:

$$y = f(x)$$

For example, $f$ could be the area of a circle: $f(r) = \pi r^2$. Here, $\pi \approx 3.14159$ is a **physical constant** baked into the rules.

### Step 2 — Identify the limitation
In many real problems, the true $f$ is:
- **Unknown** (e.g., what makes a tumor malignant?).
- **Too complex** to write by hand (e.g., recognizing handwritten digits).
- **Time-varying** (e.g., user preferences for movies).

### Step 3 — Invert the paradigm
Instead of supplying the rules, we supply the **examples** $(x, y)$ and let the machine infer $f$:

$$f = \text{Learner}(X, Y)$$

Equivalently, the ML model produces a **hypothesis** $h_\theta \approx f$, parameterized by $\theta$.

### Step 4 — Quantify how good $h_\theta$ is
Define the cost (loss) function — for a single example, the squared error is:

$$L^{(i)}(\theta) = \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

For the entire dataset, average it (MSE form):

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is introduced for algebraic convenience (it cancels the power-of-2 when differentiating).

### Step 5 — Minimize the cost
We seek the optimal parameter vector:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} J(\theta)$$

Using gradient descent, the update rule for parameter $\theta_j$ is:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$$

For a linear hypothesis $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$, the partial derivative is:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

Substituting back:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

This is the celebrated **Batch Gradient Descent** rule used across most ML training loops.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Single Gradient Descent Step

**Problem:** Suppose $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ with current parameters $\theta_0 = 0$, $\theta_1 = 0$, and $\alpha = 0.01$. We have two training points: $(1, 2)$ and $(2, 4)$.

**Step 1 — Predictions:**

$$h_\theta(1) = 0 + 0(1) = 0$$

$$h_\theta(2) = 0 + 0(2) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_1$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (0 - 2)(1) + (0 - 4)(2) \right] = \frac{1}{2} \left[ -2 + -8 \right] = \frac{1}{2}(-10) = -5$$

**Step 3 — Update $\theta_1$:**

$$\theta_1 := 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-5) = 0 + 0.05 = 0.05$$

**Step 4 — Compute partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_0$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ (0 - 2)(1) + (0 - 4)(1) \right] = \frac{1}{2}(-6) = -3$$

**Step 5 — Update $\theta_0$:**

$$\theta_0 := 0 - 0.01 \cdot (-3) = 0 + 0.03 = 0.03$$

**Result after one step:** $\theta_0 = 0.03$, $\theta_1 = 0.05$. Notice the model is moving toward the true relationship $y = 2x$ (i.e., $\theta_0 = 0, \theta_1 = 2$).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Types of Learning

| Dimension | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Format** | Labeled $(x, y)$ | Unlabeled $x$ only | $(s, a, r, s')$ transitions |
| **Feedback** | Direct (correct answer given) | None | Delayed (reward signal) |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover structure in $X$ | Maximize cumulative reward $G_t$ |
| **Output Type** | Class label / real number | Clusters / reduced dims | Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$ |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, MSE, F1-score | Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin | Cumulative reward, regret |
| **Algorithms** | Linear/Logistic Reg., SVM, k-NN | K-Means, PCA, DBSCAN | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |
| **Real Example** | Predict house price | Group news articles | Teach robot to walk |
| **Teacher Present?** | Yes (labels) | No | Yes (via reward, not labels) |
| **KTU Tag** | Most asked in exams | Often asked as definition | Often asked as definition |
| **Key Challenge** | Overfitting, data labeling cost | Defining "similarity" | Exploration vs exploitation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Traditional Programming vs. Machine Learning Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Programmer writes Rules and Data"] --> B1["Computer"]
    C1["Input Data"] --> B1
    B1 --> D1["Output"]
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style C1 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#333
    style B1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333
    style D1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#333
```

**Caption — Traditional Programming:**
The programmer supplies **both** the rules (program) and the input. The computer outputs the answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2["Input Data X"] --> B2["Machine Learning Algorithm"]
    C2["Desired Output Y labels"] --> B2
    B2 --> D2["Learned Model h theta"]
    D2 --> E2["Prediction on new X"]
    style A2 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#333
    style C2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333
    style D2 fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#333
    style E2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#333
```

**Caption — Machine Learning:**
The algorithm is given **examples** (inputs and known outputs) and produces a **model** that can predict outputs for *new* inputs.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Taxonomy of Machine Learning Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ML["Machine Learning"]

    UL --> UL2["Dimensionality Reduction"]
    UL --> UL3["Association"]
    UL1 --> UL1a["K Means"]
    UL1 --> UL1b["DBSCAN"]
    UL2 --> UL2a["PCA"]
    UL2 --> UL2b["t SNE"]
    RL --> RL1["Model Based RL"]
    RL --> RL2["Model Free RL"]

```

**Caption — Hierarchical taxonomy** of the three primary types of Machine Learning and their sub-branches.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Reinforcement Learning Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Env["Environment"]
    Agent["Agent"]
    Action["Action a sub t"]
    State["State s sub t"]
    Reward["Reward r sub t+1"]
    Policy["Policy pi a given s"]
    Agent -->|selects Action| Action
    Action -->|applied to| Env
    Env -->|new State| State
    State -->|observed by| Agent
    Env -->|emits| Reward
    Reward -->|feedback to| Agent
    Agent -.-> Policy
    style Env fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#333
    style Agent fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333
    style Action fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#333
    style State fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#333
    style Reward fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#333
    style Policy fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#333
```

**Caption — The RL feedback loop:** The agent observes the state $s_t$, takes an action $a_t$ (guided by policy $\pi$), receives a reward $r_{t+1}$, and transitions to a new state $s_{t+1}$. This loop continues until a terminal state is reached.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Define Machine Learning. How is it different from Traditional Programming?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Samuel, 1959):** Machine Learning is the field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed.
>
> **Difference:**
> - In **Traditional Programming**, the programmer supplies both the *data* and the *rules (program)*; the computer produces the *output*. Equation: $y = f(x)$ where $f$ is hand-coded.
> - In **Machine Learning**, the system is given *data and desired outputs (examples)*; it learns the *rules (model)* itself. Equation: $f = \text{Learner}(X, Y)$.
>
> Traditional programming is **rule-driven and static**; Machine Learning is **data-driven and adaptive**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain the three main types of Machine Learning.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The three main types of Machine Learning are:
>
> 1. **Supervised Learning** — The model is trained on *labeled* data $(x, y)$. It learns the input-output mapping. Example: predicting house prices (regression), classifying emails as spam/not-spam (classification).
>
> 2. **Unsupervised Learning** — The model is given *unlabeled* data $x$ and must discover hidden structure. Sub-tasks: *clustering* (K-Means) and *dimensionality reduction* (PCA). Example: customer segmentation.
>
> 3. **Reinforcement Learning** — An *agent* learns by interacting with an *environment*, taking actions, and receiving *rewards*. Goal: maximize cumulative reward. Example: AlphaGo, robotic locomotion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**(a)** Explain in detail the difference between **Traditional Programming** and **Machine Learning** with suitable diagrams. Discuss why Machine Learning is preferred for problems like image recognition and spam filtering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **architecture of a Reinforcement Learning system**. Explain the terms: *Agent, Environment, State, Action, Reward, Policy.* Derive the expression for the **cumulative discounted reward**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Traditional Programming — Definition & Flow:**
In traditional programming, a developer analyzes the problem and hand-codes a set of rules. The computer then takes input data, applies the rules, and produces the output. The flow can be summarized as:

$$\text{Program} + \text{Input} \longrightarrow \text{Computer} \longrightarrow \text{Output}$$

Equivalently, $y = f(x)$ where the function $f$ is manually designed by the programmer.

**2. Machine Learning — Definition & Flow:**
In ML, the developer provides *examples* (input-output pairs) and lets the algorithm *discover* the underlying function. The flow is:

$$\text{Input} + \text{Desired Output} \longrightarrow \text{ML Algorithm} \longrightarrow \text{Learned Model} \longrightarrow \text{Prediction on New Input}$$

Equivalently, $f = \text{Learner}(X, Y)$, and the model $h_\theta \approx f$.

**3. Comparison Table (4 marks weightage):**

| Criterion | Traditional | ML |
|---|---|---|
| Logic source | Hand-coded rules | Learned from data |
| Data need | Low | High |
| Adaptability | Static | Improves with more data |
| Example | Calculator | Spam filter |

**4. Why ML for image recognition & spam filtering:**
- The rules defining "what a cat looks like" cannot be hand-coded (too many edge cases, lighting, angles, breeds).
- Spam patterns constantly evolve — a hand-coded rulebook becomes obsolete in weeks.
- ML models can *generalize* from millions of examples, capturing subtle patterns humans miss.
- They can self-update by retraining on new data, solving the *concept drift* problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram (textual):**
> Traditional: `[Rules] + [Data]` → `Computer` → `[Output]`
> ML: `[Data] + [Labels]` → `Algorithm` → `[Model]` → `[Prediction]`

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Architecture Diagram of RL (textual representation):**

```
   +----------------------+
   |                      |
   |       AGENT          |----[Action a_t]----+
   |   (with Policy π)    |                    |
   |                      |<---[Reward r_{t+1}]|<--+
   +----------+-----------+                    |   |
              |                                |   |
              | [observes]                     |   |
              v                                |   |
   +----------------------+                    |   |
   |     State s_t        |                    |   |
   +----------------------+                    |   |
                                               |   |
   +----------------------+                    |   |
   |                      |                    |   |
   |    ENVIRONMENT       |<---[applies]-------+   |
   |                      |                        |
   +----------------------+------------------------+
            | (transitions to)
            v
       New State s_{t+1}
```

**2. Definition of key terms (4 marks):**

- **Agent:** The decision-maker / learner (e.g., a robot, a game player).
- **Environment:** The world the agent interacts with (e.g., the maze, the game board).
- **State ($s_t$):** A complete description of the environment at time $t$.
- **Action ($a_t$):** A move the agent can make, chosen from the action space $A$.
- **Reward ($r_{t+1}$):** A scalar feedback signal from the environment after each action.
- **Policy ($\pi$):** The agent's strategy; a mapping from states to action probabilities $\pi(a \mid s)$.

**3. Derivation of Cumulative Discounted Reward (3 marks):**

At each time step $t$, the agent receives a sequence of future rewards: $R_{t+1}, R_{t+2}, R_{t+3}, \dots$

The **return** $G_t$ is the total discounted reward from time $t$ onward:

$$G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \dots = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

where the **discount factor** $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ determines the present value of future rewards. Compactly:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$$

- If $\gamma = 0$, the agent is **myopic** — it cares only about the immediate reward.
- If $\gamma \to 1$, the agent is **far-sighted** — it values long-term rewards almost equally.

**The objective in RL is to find a policy $\pi^*$ that maximizes the expected return:**

$$\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\left[ G_t \right]$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Alternative Path]

**(a)** Explain **Supervised Learning** and **Unsupervised Learning** with examples. Clearly distinguish between **classification and regression** problems, and between **clustering and dimensionality reduction** problems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **cost function** $J(\theta)$ used in a Linear Regression model. Starting from first principles, derive the **gradient descent update rule** for the parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Supervised Learning (3 marks):**
- Data is **labeled**: $D = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$.
- A "teacher" provides correct answers during training.
- **Goal:** Learn mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$.
- **Examples:** Email spam classification, predicting house price, image classification, medical diagnosis.

**2. Unsupervised Learning (2 marks):**
- Data is **unlabeled**: $D = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$.
- No correct answer is given — the algorithm explores structure.
- **Goal:** Discover groupings, patterns, or compressed representations.
- **Examples:** Customer segmentation, news topic grouping, anomaly detection, gene clustering.

**3. Classification vs. Regression (within Supervised) (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Discrete category | Continuous real value |
| Example | Spam / Not-Spam | Predicting temperature |
| Algorithm | Logistic Regression, SVM | Linear Regression, SVR |

**4. Clustering vs. Dimensionality Reduction (within Unsupervised) (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Clustering | Dimensionality Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Group similar points | Compress / project data |
| Output | Cluster labels | Lower-dim coordinates |
| Example | Customer segments (K-Means) | Visualizing 100-D data in 2-D (PCA) |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Define the Cost Function (2 marks):**

Given a training set of $m$ examples $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$ and a linear hypothesis:

$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$$

The **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** cost function is:

$$J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ simplifies the derivative.

**2. Derivation of Gradient Descent Rule (5 marks):**

We want to minimize $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$. The gradient descent update is:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta_0, \theta_1)}{\partial \theta_j}, \quad j \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Computing $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_0} \left[ \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2 \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_0} h_\theta(x^{(i)})$$

Since $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$, we have $\frac{\partial h_\theta}{\partial \theta_0} = 1$. Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

**Computing $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

(since $\frac{\partial h_\theta}{\partial \theta_1} = x^{(i)}$).

**Final update rules:**

$$\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)$$

$$\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The update moves $\theta_j$ in the direction that *reduces* the error. If $h_\theta(x^{(i)}) > y^{(i)}$ (over-prediction), the term is positive, and we *decrease* $\theta_j$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the equations** — Students often write $y = f(x)$ for both traditional programming AND Machine Learning. Remember: for ML, it is $f = \text{Learner}(X, Y)$. Examiners specifically test this reversal. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. **Mixing up learning types** — Writing that "unsupervised learning uses labels" is a fatal error. Supervised = labeled, Unsupervised = unlabeled, RL = reward-based. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ in MSE** — Examiners expect the standardized cost function form $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum (h - y)^2$. Writing $\frac{1}{m}\sum$ is acceptable only if explicitly noted. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in Part B (b)** — A bare final formula without intermediate $\frac{\partial h_\theta}{\partial \theta_j}$ steps will get **0 out of 5 derivation marks**. Show every differentiation step.
> 5. **RL definitions incomplete** — Listing only "agent" and "reward" without "state, action, policy, environment" will lose up to 2 marks. All 6 terms are expected.
> 6. **No diagram in Q.A(a) or Q.B** — A 7-mark sub-part without a labeled diagram typically caps your score at 4/7. Always include at least a textual/ASCII diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Machine Learning** is a subfield of AI where computers learn from data instead of explicit rules.
- **Traditional Programming:** $y = f(x)$ where $f$ is hand-coded.
- **Machine Learning:** $f = \text{Learner}(X, Y)$ — the model *is* the function.
- **Arthur Samuel (1959):** "Field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed." — **KTU favourite one-liner.**
- **Mitchell (1997):** A program learns task $T$ from experience $E$ measured by performance $P$.
- **Three types of ML:**
  - **Supervised** — labeled data $(x, y)$ → classification & regression.
  - **Unsupervised** — unlabeled data $x$ → clustering, dimensionality reduction, association.
  - **Reinforcement** — agent + environment + reward → policy $\pi(a \mid s)$.
- **Cost function (MSE):** $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2$.
- **Gradient descent update:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$.
- **Learning rate $\alpha$:** typically $0.001$ to $0.1$; too high → divergence, too low → slow convergence.
- **RL cumulative discounted reward:** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$, with $\gamma \in [0, 1)$.
- **Policy** in RL: $\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)$.
- **Examples for exams:** Supervised → spam filter / house price; Unsupervised → customer segmentation / PCA; RL → AlphaGo / robot walking.
- **Always include diagrams** in 7-mark sub-parts (textual ASCII is acceptable, Mermaid preferred).
- **Key contrast:** Traditional programming is **static and rule-based**; ML is **data-driven and adaptive**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Machine Learning Pipeline, Data Preprocessing, Idea of Training, Testing, Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Machine Learning Pipeline, Data Preprocessing, Idea of Training, Testing, Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Machine Learning (ML) Pipeline — Formal Definition

The **Machine Learning Pipeline** is a sequential, end-to-end, and repeatable workflow that transforms raw, unstructured, real-world data into a deployable predictive model. It is an **engineering construct** that automates the flow of data through a series of data-processing and model-training stages, ensuring reproducibility, scalability, and auditability of the AI system.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education)**, the ML pipeline is not merely a sequence of code blocks; it is a **standardized process model** that satisfies the Course Outcome (CO1): *"Understand the fundamental concepts of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Recalled from Syllabus):** The ML pipeline is the **iterative chain** consisting of *Data Acquisition → Data Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → Model Training → Model Evaluation → Hyperparameter Tuning → Deployment*. Each stage outputs a structured artifact that becomes the input of the next stage.

## 1.2 Data Preprocessing — Formal Definition

**Data Preprocessing** is the preparatory phase of the ML pipeline in which raw datasets are cleaned, transformed, normalized, and encoded so that they conform to the mathematical assumptions (e.g., zero mean, unit variance, no missing values) of the chosen learning algorithm. It is widely accepted in literature that data preprocessing consumes **60–80%** of the total time of a typical data-science project.

## 1.3 Training, Testing, and Validation — Formal Definitions

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Training Set** | The subset of the dataset used to **fit** the model's parameters (weights and biases) by minimizing a loss function. |
| **Validation Set** | The subset used to **tune hyperparameters** and perform model selection *without* touching the test set. |
| **Test Set** | The **held-out, unseen** subset used to estimate the **generalization error** of the final, chosen model. Provides an unbiased performance metric. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization Principle:** A model is said to *generalize* well when its performance on the **unseen test set** is comparable to its performance on the training set. A significant gap indicates **overfitting**.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef" Intuition

Imagine you (an AI Engineer) are training a **rookie chef** (the ML Model) to cook the perfect *Kerala Fish Curry* for a competition:

| ML Concept | Chef Analogy |
|---|---|
| Raw Data | A sack of unprocessed fish, raw spices, and vegetables from the market. |
| Data Preprocessing | Cleaning the fish, chopping onions, measuring exact grams of chilli powder, marinating. |
| Training Set | The chef **practices cooking** 70 times using your recipe book, tasting and adjusting salt. |
| Validation Set | A **food-critic friend** tastes the practice dishes and suggests "less salt, more curry leaves" — used to **tune** the recipe. |
| Test Set | The **final competition** with new judges — the chef's *true* skill is measured here. |
| Hyperparameter Tuning | Adjusting the **gas flame level** and **cooking time** based on the friend's feedback. |

The key insight: you must **never** let the chef practice on the competition dish (no test-set leakage), and the critic's feedback (validation) must remain a *separate* voice from the final judges (test).

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Module 1 forms the foundation for higher modules (Supervised Learning, Neural Networks, Deep Learning). A weak grasp of the pipeline translates directly to lost marks in later modules and the **8th-semester project** (which carries significant weightage under the new regulations).
