# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of the ML Pipeline (Decomposed)

The pipeline is executed in the following **mandatory logical order**. Skipping or reordering a step leads to data leakage or model failure.

1. **Problem Definition & Data Collection**
   - Define the business/research problem.
   - Acquire data from databases (`SQL`), APIs, web-scraping, or IoT sensors.

2. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**
   - Visualize distributions, correlations, and outliers using histograms, box-plots, and heatmaps.
   - Identify class imbalance, missing-value patterns, and feature skewness.

3. **Data Preprocessing** *(See Section 2.2 for full breakdown)*

4. **Feature Engineering & Selection**
   - Create new features (e.g., polynomial features, date → day-of-week).
   - Apply techniques: **Pearson correlation, Mutual Information, Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE)**.

5. **Model Training**
   - Feed the training set $(\mathbf{X}_{\text{train}}, \mathbf{y}_{\text{train}})$ into a chosen algorithm.
   - The model learns a function $f_\theta : \mathbf{X} \rightarrow \mathbf{y}$ parameterized by $\theta$.

6. **Model Evaluation & Validation**
   - Compute metrics on validation and test sets.
   - Use cross-validation to assess stability.

7. **Hyperparameter Tuning & Deployment**
   - Use **Grid Search, Random Search, or Bayesian Optimization**.
   - Deploy the final model via REST APIs, mobile apps, or edge devices.

## 2.2 Data Preprocessing — Sub-Techniques

| Sub-Technique | Mathematical Form | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Handling Missing Values** | Mean/Median/Mode Imputation, KNN Imputation | When `NaN` exists in numerical or categorical columns. |
| **Min-Max Normalization** | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Neural Networks, KNN, distance-based algorithms. |
| **Z-Score Standardization** | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Linear/Logistic Regression, SVM, PCA. |
| **One-Hot Encoding** | Categorical $\rightarrow$ binary vector of length $k$ | Nominal categorical features (no order). |
| **Label Encoding** | Categorical $\rightarrow$ integer $0, 1, \ldots, k-1$ | Ordinal categorical features (preserved order). |
| **Outlier Removal** | IQR rule: drop $x$ if $x < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ or $x > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ | When outliers are *errors* (not true extremes). |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute-value notation in all mathematical expressions below to comply with markdown-table syntax safety.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Min-Max Scaling | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Output range: $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | Z-Score Standardization | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Output: zero mean, unit variance |
| 3 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Used in regression; lower is better |
| 4 | Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 5 | Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 6 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | For balanced classification |
| 7 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | "Of those predicted positive, how many are correct?" |
| 8 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | "Of all actual positives, how many did we find?" |
| 9 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of $P$ and $R$ |
| 10 | Train-Validation-Test Split | e.g., $70\% : 15\% : 15\%$ | Industry standard for medium datasets |
| 11 | K-Fold Cross-Validation | $\text{CV}_k = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} M_i$ | $M_i$ = metric on $i$-th fold |
| 12 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_\varepsilon$ | $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ = irreducible noise |
| 13 | Learning Rate Update (SGD) | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla L(\theta_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate, $L$ = loss |
| 14 | L2 Regularization Term | $\Omega(\theta) = \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{p} \theta_j^2$ | $\lambda$ = regularization strength |
| 15 | Hold-Out Validation | $\text{Error}_{\text{gen}} \approx \text{Error}_{\text{test}}$ | Holds when test set is *truly* unseen |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the ML Pipeline in Engineering

- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring — sensors feed data through a pipeline to predict crack propagation.
- **Biomedical Engineering (KTU Project Hot-Topic):** ECG signal classification using a pipeline: filter noise $\rightarrow$ extract R-peaks $\rightarrow$ train CNN $\rightarrow$ deploy on wearable device.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Smart-grid load forecasting using a pipeline built on historical consumption data.
- **Industrial AI 4.0:** Predictive maintenance on CNC machines — the pipeline reduces downtime by **30–50%** in published case studies.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 viva voce**, examiners frequently ask: *"If your test accuracy is 95% but your training accuracy is 99%, what does it imply?"* — The expected answer is **overfitting** (high variance), and the remedy is more data, regularization, or dropout.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Min-Max Normalization to [0, 1] Range

We are given a feature column $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ with observed minimum $x_{\min}$ and maximum $x_{\max}$. We wish to map every value to the closed interval $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1: Define the linear affine transformation.**

$$
x' = a \cdot x + b
$$

We require the boundary conditions: the smallest value maps to $0$ and the largest value maps to $1$.

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions.**

When $x = x_{\min}$, we want $x' = 0$:

$$
0 = a \cdot x_{\min} + b
$$

When $x = x_{\max}$, we want $x' = 1$:

$$
1 = a \cdot x_{\max} + b
$$

**Step 3: Solve the two-equation system for $a$ and $b$.**

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$
1 - 0 = a \cdot x_{\max} - a \cdot x_{\min} = a \cdot (x_{\max} - x_{\min})
$$

Solving for $a$:

$$
a = \frac{1}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}
$$

Substituting back into the first equation:

$$
b = -a \cdot x_{\min} = -\frac{x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}
$$

**Step 4: Combine into the final normalized expression.**

$$
\boxed{x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Let $X = \{2, 5, 8, 10\}$. Then $x_{\min} = 2$, $x_{\max} = 10$, and $x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 8$.

For $x = 5$:

$$
x' = \frac{5 - 2}{8} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375
$$

For $x = 10$:

$$
x' = \frac{10 - 2}{8} = 1.000
$$

The output range is now $[0, 1]$. This is the form required by K-Nearest Neighbors, K-Means, and the input layer of Neural Networks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Z-Score Standardization

We are given a feature column $X$ with sample mean $\mu$ and sample standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}
$$

**Step 1: Subtract the mean (centering).**

Define the centered variable $x_c = x - \mu$. This shifts the distribution so that the new mean is $0$.

**Step 2: Divide by the standard deviation (scaling).**

Define the standardized variable $z = \dfrac{x_c}{\sigma} = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$.

**Step 3: Verify the new mean and standard deviation.**

New mean:

$$
\mathbb{E}[z] = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma}\mathbb{E}[x - \mu] = \frac{1}{\sigma}\cdot 0 = 0
$$

New standard deviation:

$$
\text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\text{Var}(x - \mu) = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} = 1
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}}
$$

This is the form required by **Logistic Regression, SVM, PCA, and Linear Regression with regularization**. The output distribution has **mean = 0** and **standard deviation = 1**.

## 3.3 Derivation: K-Fold Cross-Validation Error Estimate

The goal of K-Fold Cross-Validation is to obtain a **stable, low-variance estimate** of the generalization error when the dataset is too small for a clean train/validation/test split.

**Step 1: Partition the dataset.**

Randomly shuffle the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $n$ and split it into $K$ *approximately equal* disjoint folds:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{F}_1 \cup \mathcal{F}_2 \cup \ldots \cup \mathcal{F}_K, \quad \vert \mathcal{F}_i \vert \approx \frac{n}{K}
$$

**Step 2: For each fold $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$, perform the following.**

Use $\mathcal{F}_i$ as the validation (test) fold and the remaining $K-1$ folds as the training fold:

$$
\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}^{(i)} = \mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{F}_i
$$

Train the model on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}^{(i)}$ and compute the chosen metric $M_i$ (e.g., accuracy) on $\mathcal{F}_i$.

**Step 3: Aggregate the K scores.**

$$
\boxed{\text{CV}_K = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} M_i}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the standard deviation across folds** (used as a *stability indicator*):

$$
\sigma_{\text{CV}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\left(M_i - \text{CV}_K\right)^2}
$$

**Special case analysis:**

- $K = 2$: Simple 50/50 split (high variance).
- $K = 5$ or $K = 10$: **Industry standard** (best bias-variance tradeoff).
- $K = n$: Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (**LOOCV**). Low bias, very high variance, computationally expensive.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs. Preprocessing Requirement

| Algorithm | Needs Scaling? | Sensitive to Outliers? | Handles Missing Values? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | Recommended | Yes | No (needs imputation) | Assumes linearity & homoscedasticity. |
| Logistic Regression | **Mandatory** | Yes | No | Uses gradient descent; needs scaled inputs. |
| Decision Tree / Random Forest | **No** required | No | Yes (handles natively) | Splits are scale-invariant. |
| K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) | **Mandatory** | Yes | No | Distance metric is highly scale-sensitive. |
| Support Vector Machine (SVM) | **Mandatory** | Yes | No | Kernel computations depend on scale. |
| Naive Bayes | No required | No | Yes (with smoothing) | Probabilistic, scale-invariant. |
| Neural Networks (ANN/CNN) | **Mandatory** | Yes | No | Activation functions saturate on unscaled inputs. |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — Confusion Matrix Metrics

Suppose a binary classifier produces the following results on a test set of $200$ samples:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | $80$ (TP) | $20$ (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | $10$ (FP) | $90$ (TN) |

**Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score.**

**Step 1: Total samples.**

$$
n = 80 + 20 + 10 + 90 = 200
$$

**Step 2: Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 90}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85
$$

**Step 3: Precision.**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} \approx 0.8889
$$

**Step 4: Recall.**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80
$$

**Step 5: F1-Score (harmonic mean of P and R).**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8889 \cdot 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80}
$$

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7111}{1.6889} \approx 2 \cdot 0.4210 \approx 0.8421
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The model has high precision ($\approx 89\%$) — when it predicts positive, it is usually correct. However, recall is lower ($80\%$) — it is missing **1 in 5** actual positives. In medical diagnosis, this trade-off is critical: missing a positive case (cancer) is costlier than a false alarm.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The End-to-End ML Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Sources] --> B[Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    C --> D[Data Preprocessing]
    D --> E[Feature Engineering and Selection]
    E --> F[Model Training Phase]
    F --> G[Model Evaluation and Validation]
    G --> H{Tune Hyperparameters}
    H -->|Yes| F
    H -->|No| I[Final Model Selection]
    I --> J[Test Set Evaluation]
    J --> K{Generalization Acceptable}
    K -->|Yes| L[Model Deployment]
    K -->|No| F
    L --> M[Monitoring and Maintenance]
    M --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pipeline is **cyclic, not linear**. After deployment, monitoring feeds new data back into the preprocessing stage, creating a continuous-learning loop (a precursor to MLOps in Module 5).

## 4.2 Train / Validation / Test Data Split (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DS[Original Dataset 100 percent] --> T1[Training Set 70 percent]
    DS --> V1[Validation Set 15 percent]
    DS --> TE1[Test Set 15 percent]
    T1 --> TR[Train the Model]
    TR --> VAL[Evaluate on Validation Set]
    VAL --> TUNE[Tune Hyperparameters]
    TUNE --> TR
    TUNE --> FINE[Select Best Model]
    FINE --> TEST[Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    TEST --> OUT[Report Unbiased Generalization Error]
```

## 4.3 K-Fold Cross-Validation Workflow (5-Fold Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FULL[Full Dataset N samples] --> S1[Shuffle and Split into 5 Folds]
    S1 --> F1[Fold 1]
    S1 --> F2[Fold 2]
    S1 --> F3[Fold 3]
    S1 --> F4[Fold 4]
    S1 --> F5[Fold 5]
    F1 --> R1[Iteration 1: Train on F2 F3 F4 F5, Test on F1]
    F2 --> R2[Iteration 2: Train on F1 F3 F4 F5, Test on F2]
    F3 --> R3[Iteration 3: Train on F1 F2 F4 F5, Test on F3]
    F4 --> R4[Iteration 4: Train on F1 F2 F3 F5, Test on F4]
    F5 --> R5[Iteration 5: Train on F1 F2 F3 F4, Test on F5]
    R1 --> AGG[Aggregate M1 to M5 and Average]
    R2 --> AGG
    R3 --> AGG
    R4 --> AGG
    R5 --> AGG
    AGG --> RES[Final Cross Validated Score]
```

## 4.4 Data Preprocessing Sub-Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Raw DataFrame] --> CK1{Missing Values Present}
    CK1 -->|Yes| IM[Apply Imputation Strategy]
    CK1 -->|No| CK2{Categorical Columns}
    IM --> CK2
    CK2 -->|Yes| EN[Apply One Hot or Label Encoding]
    CK2 -->|No| CK3{Outliers Detected}
    EN --> CK3
    CK3 -->|Yes| OUT[Apply IQR or Z Score Removal]
    CK3 -->|No| SC{Algorithm Needs Scaling}
    OUT --> SC
    SC -->|Yes| NORM[Apply Min Max or Z Score]
    SC -->|No| DONE[Cleaned Dataset Ready for Training]
    NORM --> DONE
```

## 4.5 Bias-Variance Tradeoff Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Model Complexity Increases] --> B[Training Error Decreases]
    A --> C[Validation Error]
    C -->|Initial| D[Decreases]
    C -->|Later| E[Increases Due to Overfitting]
    F[Optimal Point] --> G[Low Bias and Low Variance]
    H[Underfitting Region] --> I[High Bias and Low Variance]
    J[Overfitting Region] --> K[Low Bias and High Variance]
```

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3-Mark Conceptual)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the term "Machine Learning Pipeline." List any four stages of a typical ML pipeline in the correct order of execution.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Machine Learning Pipeline** is a structured, automated, and reproducible sequence of steps that transforms raw input data into a deployable predictive model.

The four core stages in correct order are:

1. **Data Collection and Preprocessing** — acquiring and cleaning the raw dataset (handling missing values, scaling, encoding).
2. **Feature Engineering and Selection** — extracting, transforming, and selecting the most informative variables.
3. **Model Training** — feeding the training subset into a learning algorithm to fit parameters $\theta$.
4. **Model Evaluation and Deployment** — measuring performance on the unseen test set, then deploying the model.

> *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly ordered list.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between Training Set, Validation Set, and Test Set in machine learning.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Training Set | Validation Set | Test Set |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Fit model parameters $\theta$ | Tune hyperparameters & select best model | Estimate final generalization error |
| **Used By** | Learning algorithm | Data scientist / engineer | No one until the very end |
| **Touched How Many Times?** | Many times (epochs) | Many times during tuning | **Exactly once** (for unbiased evaluation) |
| **Typical Size** | 60–70% | 15–20% | 15–20% |
| **Example** | Recipe practice sessions | Food-critic feedback | Final competition judging |

> *(3 marks: Tabular differentiation with one-mark per valid distinguishing criterion.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **end-to-end Machine Learning Pipeline**. List and briefly describe any **five data preprocessing techniques** used in the pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a dataset column $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 200\}$ where the value $200$ is an outlier. Apply **Min-Max Normalization** to scale the values to the range $[0, 1]$. Also, briefly explain why **outlier removal is recommended before normalization**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Flowchart of the ML Pipeline:**

```
[Problem Definition]
        ↓
[Data Collection]
        ↓
[Exploratory Data Analysis]
        ↓
[Data Preprocessing] ← (imputation, scaling, encoding)
        ↓
[Feature Engineering / Selection]
        ↓
[Model Training on Training Set]
        ↓
[Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning]
        ↓
[Final Test Set Evaluation]
        ↓
[Deployment & Monitoring]
        ↓
   (cyclic loop)
```

**Five Data Preprocessing Techniques:**

1. **Handling Missing Values:** Replace missing entries using Mean (numerical, no outliers), Median (numerical, with outliers), Mode (categorical), or KNN-Imputation (multivariate).
2. **Feature Scaling (Min-Max Normalization):** Rescales features to $[0, 1]$ using $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$. Required for distance-based and gradient-descent-based algorithms.
3. **Z-Score Standardization:** Transforms features to have $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$ using $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$. Required for SVM, Logistic Regression, PCA.
4. **Categorical Encoding (One-Hot / Label):** Converts non-nategorical string values into numeric form. One-hot creates $k$ binary columns; label encoding assigns ordinal integers.
5. **Outlier Detection and Removal:** Uses the IQR rule ($x \notin [Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$) or the Z-score method ($\vert z \vert > 3$). Essential because outliers distort the mean and inflate variance.

> *(7 marks: 3 marks for flowchart + 4 marks for the five techniques.)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Identify the outlier.**

The value $200$ is the outlier (it is more than **3× the next highest value** of $50$).

**Step 2: Decision — Remove outlier first or normalize first?**

We **remove the outlier first** because the outlier ($200$) will dominate $x_{\max}$, compressing all other values into a near-zero range.

**Step 3: Remove the outlier and recompute min-max.**

After removing $200$, the cleaned dataset is:

$$
X_{\text{clean}} = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}
$$

$$
x_{\min} = 10, \quad x_{\max} = 50, \quad x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 40
$$

**Step 4: Apply Min-Max Normalization** $x' = \dfrac{x - 10}{40}$:

- For $x = 10$: $\;x' = \dfrac{10 - 10}{40} = 0.00$
- For $x = 20$: $\;x' = \dfrac{20 - 10}{40} = 0.25$
- For $x = 30$: $\;x' = \dfrac{30 - 10}{40} = 0.50$
- For $x = 40$: $\;x' = \dfrac{40 - 10}{40} = 0.75$
- For $x = 50$: $\;x' = \dfrac{50 - 10}{40} = 1.00$

**Step 5: Counter-example — What happens if we do NOT remove the outlier?**

Using the original $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 200\}$:

- $x_{\min} = 10$, $x_{\max} = 200$, range = $190$.

For $x = 50$:

$$
x' = \frac{50 - 10}{190} = \frac{40}{190} \approx 0.21
$$

The values $10, 20, 30, 40, 50$ all collapse into $[0, 0.21]$, losing all discriminative power.

**Conclusion:** Outlier removal **must precede** Min-Max Normalization, or the outlier will dominate the range and the algorithm will lose the ability to distinguish between normal data points.

> *(7 marks: 2 marks for outlier identification + 3 marks for the cleaned normalization + 2 marks for the explanation with counter-example.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define the terms **Training Set, Validation Set, and Test Set**. Explain the **70:15:15 split** strategy and state one disadvantage of using a fixed split. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **5-Fold Cross-Validation** with a neat diagram. Given the following 5 accuracy scores obtained from the folds, compute the **mean cross-validation accuracy** and the **standard deviation**: $\{0.82,\; 0.86,\; 0.84,\; 0.88,\; 0.85\}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Definitions:**

- **Training Set:** The portion of the data used to **train** the model (fit parameters $\theta$).
- **Validation Set:** The portion used **during development** to tune hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization strength, number of hidden layers) and to perform model selection.
- **Test Set:** A **held-out, untouched** portion used **only once** at the very end to estimate the generalization performance of the *final* selected model.

**The 70:15:15 Split Strategy:**

Out of the total $N$ samples:

$$
N_{\text{train}} = 0.70 \cdot N, \quad N_{\text{val}} = 0.15 \cdot N, \quad N_{\text{test}} = 0.15 \cdot N
$$

The three subsets must be **disjoint** and **stratified** (preserve class distribution) when the dataset is small or imbalanced.

**Why this split?**
- $70\%$ training: sufficient to learn meaningful patterns.
- $15\%$ validation: enough to reliably detect overfitting.
- $15\%$ test: statistically meaningful for the final accuracy estimate.

**Disadvantage of a Fixed Split:**

> The single validation score **depends heavily on the random seed**. Different splits produce different scores, leading to **high-variance** model selection. For small datasets, $15\%$ may be only a few hundred samples — too few for a stable estimate.

**Remedy:** Use **K-Fold Cross-Validation** (covered in part b).

> *(7 marks: 3 marks for definitions + 2 marks for 70:15:15 logic + 2 marks for disadvantage and remedy.)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**5-Fold Cross-Validation Diagram:**

```
Iteration 1:  [TEST=20%] [TRAIN=80%]
Iteration 2:  [TRAIN=20%] [TEST=20%] [TRAIN=60%]
Iteration 3:  [TRAIN=40%] [TEST=20%] [TRAIN=40%]
Iteration 4:  [TRAIN=60%] [TEST=20%] [TRAIN=20%]
Iteration 5:  [TRAIN=80%] [TEST=20%]
```

In each iteration, the model is trained on $4/5$ of the data and tested on the remaining $1/5$. The 5 accuracy scores are then averaged.

**Step 1: Compute the mean cross-validation accuracy** from the given scores $\{0.82, 0.86, 0.84, 0.88, 0.85\}$:

$$
\bar{M} = \frac{0.82 + 0.86 + 0.84 + 0.88 + 0.85}{5} = \frac{4.25}{5} = 0.85
$$

**Step 2: Compute the standard deviation across folds.**

First, compute the squared deviations:

- $(0.82 - 0.85)^2 = (-0.03)^2 = 0.0009$
- $(0.86 - 0.85)^2 = (0.01)^2 = 0.0001$
- $(0.84 - 0.85)^2 = (-0.01)^2 = 0.0001$
- $(0.88 - 0.85)^2 = (0.03)^2 = 0.0009$
- $(0.85 - 0.85)^2 = (0.00)^2 = 0.0000$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum (M_i - \bar{M})^2 = 0.0009 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 + 0.0009 + 0.0000 = 0.0020
$$

Standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_{\text{CV}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0020}{5}} = \sqrt{0.0004} = 0.02
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{CV Accuracy} = 0.85 \pm 0.02}
$$

> *(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 3 marks for mean computation + 2 marks for standard deviation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Validation and Test Sets:** Many students report final test accuracy as "validation accuracy." The test set is **touched exactly once**. Reporting multiple test scores is a **serious methodology error** and the examiner will deduct up to **3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the order in the pipeline:** Writing "Training $\rightarrow$ Testing $\rightarrow$ Preprocessing" is **wrong**. Preprocessing must precede training. Examiners explicitly look for correct sequencing — wrong order = **2 marks lost**.
>
> 3. **Applying Min-Max normalization on data with outliers:** The KTU paper frequently tests this trap. Always check for outliers *first*, then scale. Forgetting this loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Using the wrong metric:** Reporting accuracy on an imbalanced dataset (e.g., 95% negative class) gives a misleadingly high score. Use **F1-score, Precision, Recall** — examiners reward metric awareness with **1–2 bonus marks**.
>
> 5. **Data leakage:** Computing normalization statistics (mean, std, min, max) on the *entire* dataset before splitting leaks test-set information into training. Always **fit the scaler on training data only**, then apply to validation and test sets. This is a **2-mark penalty item**.
>
> 6. **Confusing Cross-Validation with Test Set:** K-Fold CV gives a *training-phase* performance estimate. The held-out test set is still required for the final unbiased report.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- [x] The **ML Pipeline** is a **7-stage cyclic process**: Data → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → Training → Evaluation → Tuning → Deployment.
- [x] **Data Preprocessing** consumes **60–80%** of project time. It includes imputation, scaling, encoding, and outlier removal.
- [x] The **70:15:15 split** is the industry-standard for medium-sized datasets.
- [x] **Training Set** is for fitting parameters; **Validation Set** is for tuning hyperparameters; **Test Set** is for final unbiased evaluation.
- [x] **Min-Max Normalization** maps to $[0, 1]$; formula: $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$.
- [x] **Z-Score Standardization** maps to $\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$; formula: $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$.
- [x] **K-Fold Cross-Validation** with $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ is preferred over a single hold-out split for small datasets.
- [x] **Cross-validated accuracy** formula: $\text{CV}_K = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} M_i$.
- [x] **One-Hot Encoding** is for nominal features; **Label Encoding** is for ordinal features.
- [x] **Decision Trees and Random Forests do NOT require feature scaling**, but SVM, KNN, Logistic Regression, and Neural Networks **DO**.
- [x] **Always fit the scaler/encoder on the training set only** to prevent **data leakage**.
- [x] **Accuracy** alone is misleading for imbalanced datasets; prefer **F1-Score, Precision, Recall**.
- [x] **Overfitting** = high training accuracy, low test accuracy. **Underfitting** = low accuracy on both. Remedy: regularization, more data, simpler model.
- [x] In the KTU viva: be ready to justify the **choice of metric** and the **splitting strategy** for the dataset size.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — The Machine Learning Pipeline, Data Preprocessing, Idea of Training, Testing, Validation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Gradient Descent Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Review of Gradient Descent Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gradient Descent (GD)** is a first-order, iterative optimization algorithm used to find the **local minimum** of a differentiable function $J(\theta)$. In the context of Machine Learning, it is the canonical procedure for minimizing the **cost (loss) function** $J(\theta)$ by updating the model parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^n$ in the direction opposite to the gradient of the function at the current point.

Mathematically, the canonical update rule is expressed as:

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$$

where:
- $\theta_t$ is the parameter vector at iteration $t$.
- $\eta$ (eta) is the **learning rate**, a positive scalar hyperparameter.
- $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ is the gradient of the cost function evaluated at $\theta_t$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a hiker stranded on a fog-covered mountain at night.** The hiker cannot see the bottom of the valley but can feel the **slope of the ground beneath their feet**. To reach the lowest point (the valley floor = minimum cost), the hiker takes small steps **in the direction where the ground descends most steeply**.

- The **mountain** represents the cost function $J(\theta)$.
- The **steepness of the slope** is the gradient $\nabla J(\theta)$.
- The **size of each step** is governed by the learning rate $\eta$.
- The **valley floor** is the optimum $\theta^{*}$.

This analogy makes two truths immediately obvious:
1. The hiker always steps **downhill** (negative gradient direction).
2. If the steps are too large, the hiker may **overshoot** the valley or oscillate wildly.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Cost / Loss Function | $J(\theta)$ | Function to be minimized (e.g., MSE, Cross-Entropy) |
| Gradient | $\nabla J(\theta)$ | Vector of partial derivatives w.r.t. each parameter |
| Learning Rate | $\eta$ | Step-size hyperparameter (typically $10^{-4} \le \eta \le 10^{-1}$) |
| Iteration / Epoch | $t$ | One full parameter update |
| Convergence | $\| \nabla J(\theta) \| \rightarrow 0$ | Algorithm halts when the slope becomes negligible |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Gradient Descent algorithm proceeds as a deterministic, sequential logic pipeline:

1. **Initialize** parameters $\theta_0$ randomly (or to zeros).
2. **Compute** the gradient $\nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)$ at the current location.
3. **Update** parameters using the update rule $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$.
4. **Evaluate** convergence: check if $\| J(\theta_{t+1}) - J(\theta_t) \| < \epsilon$ or if $\| \nabla J(\theta_t) \| < \epsilon$.
5. **Repeat** steps 2–4 until convergence criteria are satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the negative gradient point to a minimum?**
> The gradient $\nabla J$ always points in the direction of **steepest ascent** of $J$. By taking a step in the **opposite** direction ($-\nabla J$), we guarantee a local decrease in the function value, provided $\eta$ is small enough.

## 2.2 The Three Variants of Gradient Descent

| Variant | Data Used per Update | Update Frequency | Noise Level | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Batch GD** | Entire training set | 1 per epoch | Smooth, deterministic | High ($O(n)$) |
| **Stochastic GD (SGD)** | One sample | $n$ per epoch | High variance | Low ($O(1)$) |
| **Mini-Batch GD** | Batch of size $b$ | $n/b$ per epoch | Moderate | Moderate ($O(b)$) |

Where $n$ is the total number of training samples and $b$ is the mini-batch size (commonly $32, 64, 128, 256$).

## 2.3 Effect of Learning Rate — The Critical Hyperparameter

> [!WARNING]
> **The learning rate $\eta$ is the single most influential hyperparameter in GD.** Mis-tuning it causes three classic failure modes, all of which are favorite KTU viva questions.

- **Too small ($\eta \ll \eta_{\text{opt}}$):** Convergence is excruciatingly slow; algorithm may appear "stuck" due to floating-point precision.
- **Too large ($\eta \gg \eta_{\text{opt}}$):** Cost function oscillates or **diverges** (NaN values).
- **Optimal ($\eta = \eta_{\text{opt}}$):** Fast, monotonic decrease in $J(\theta)$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation | Meaning | Units (if applicable) |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$ | Canonical GD update rule | dimensionless |
| $J(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} L\bigl(f(x_i;\theta), y_i\bigr)$ | Empirical risk / cost function | loss units |
| $\nabla_{\theta} J = \dfrac{2}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (f(x_i) - y_i)\, x_i$ | Gradient for MSE on linear model | gradient units |
| $\theta_{\text{new}} \leftarrow \theta_{\text{old}} - \eta \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Component-wise update for parameter $\theta_j$ | parameter units |
| $\sum_{t=1}^{T} \eta_t = \infty,\ \sum_{t=1}^{T} \eta_t^2 < \infty$ | Robbins-Monro conditions for SGD convergence | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gradient descent is not merely a textbook abstraction. It is the **engine** driving:
- **Deep Learning:** Backpropagation is simply GD applied via the chain rule.
- **Robotics:** Inverse kinematics solvers use GD to find joint angles.
- **Signal Processing:** Adaptive filters (LMS algorithm) are real-time GD.
- **Control Systems:** Model Predictive Control (MPC) uses GD for trajectory optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gradient Descent Update Rule (from Taylor Series)

We motivate the update rule rigorously using a **first-order Taylor series expansion**.

Consider the cost function $J(\theta)$. We wish to find a small perturbation $\Delta \theta$ such that $J(\theta + \Delta \theta) < J(\theta)$.

**Step 1 — Truncate the Taylor series to first order** (valid when $\vert \Delta \theta \vert$ is small):

$$
J(\theta + \Delta \theta) \approx J(\theta) + \nabla J(\theta)^{\top} \Delta \theta
$$

**Step 2 — Enforce the descent condition.** We require:

$$
J(\theta + \Delta \theta) - J(\theta) = \nabla J(\theta)^{\top} \Delta \theta < 0
$$

**Step 3 — Choose the optimal direction.** By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the dot product is minimized (most negative) when $\Delta \theta$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla J$. Setting the direction:

$$
\Delta \theta = -\eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta), \quad \eta > 0
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the update rule:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla J(\theta_t)
$$

This is the **fundamental GD update equation**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x - 3)^2$ using GD starting from $x_0 = 0$ with learning rate $\eta = 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient:**

$$
f'(x) = 2(x - 3)
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize and iterate.** Apply $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot f'(\theta_t)$:

$$
x_1 = 0 - 0.1 \cdot 2(0 - 3) = 0 - 0.1(-6) = 0.600
$$

$$
x_2 = 0.6 - 0.1 \cdot 2(0.6 - 3) = 0.6 - 0.1(-4.8) = 1.080
$$

$$
x_3 = 1.08 - 0.1 \cdot 2(1.08 - 3) = 1.08 - 0.1(-3.84) = 1.464
$$

$$
x_4 = 1.464 - 0.1 \cdot 2(1.464 - 3) = 1.464 - 0.1(-3.072) = 1.771
$$

$$
x_5 = 1.771 - 0.1 \cdot 2(1.771 - 3) = 1.771 - 0.1(-2.458) = 2.017
$$

$$
x_6 = 2.017 - 0.1 \cdot 2(2.017 - 3) = 2.017 - 0.1(-1.966) = 2.214
$$

$$
x_7 = 2.214 - 0.1 \cdot 2(2.214 - 3) = 2.214 - 0.1(-1.572) = 2.371
$$

$$
x_8 = 2.371 - 0.1 \cdot 2(2.371 - 3) = 2.371 - 0.1(-1.258) = 2.497
$$

$$
x_9 = 2.497 - 0.1 \cdot 2(2.497 - 3) = 2.497 - 0.1(-1.006) = 2.598
$$

$$
x_{10} = 2.598 - 0.1 \cdot 2(2.598 - 3) = 2.598 - 0.1(-0.804) = 2.678
$$

The sequence $x_t \to 3$, which is the **true minimum**, confirming convergence.

## 3.3 Analytical Trace Matrix

| Iteration $t$ | $x_t$ | $f'(x_t)$ | $x_{t+1}$ | $f(x_{t+1})$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | -6.000 | 0.600 | 5.760 |
| 1 | 0.600 | -4.800 | 1.080 | 3.686 |
| 2 | 1.080 | -3.840 | 1.464 | 2.359 |
| 3 | 1.464 | -3.072 | 1.771 | 1.510 |
| 4 | 1.771 | -2.458 | 2.017 | 0.967 |
| 5 | 2.017 | -1.966 | 2.214 | 0.619 |
| 6 | 2.214 | -1.572 | 2.371 | 0.396 |
| 7 | 2.371 | -1.258 | 2.497 | 0.253 |
| 8 | 2.497 | -1.006 | 2.598 | 0.162 |
| 9 | 2.598 | -0.804 | 2.678 | 0.104 |
| 10 | 2.678 | -0.643 | 2.743 | 0.066 |

## 3.4 Convergence Rate (Quadratic Bowl)

For a strictly convex quadratic $f(x) = \frac{1}{2}ax^2 + bx + c$ with $a > 0$, the convergence rate can be expressed analytically. The error $e_t = x_t - x^{*}$ evolves as:

$$
e_{t+1} = (1 - \eta a) e_t
$$

**Geometric convergence** is achieved iff $\vert 1 - \eta a \vert < 1$, which simplifies to the stability condition:

$$
0 < \eta < \frac{2}{a}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Gradient Descent

The diagram below represents the complete control-flow of the canonical GD algorithm, with explicit decision points for convergence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize parameters theta0 randomly]
    B --> C[Set learning rate eta and tolerance epsilon]
    C --> D[Compute cost J theta_t]
    D --> E[Compute gradient grad J theta_t]
    E --> F[Update parameters theta_t plus 1 equals theta_t minus eta times grad J]
    F --> G{Difference in J below epsilon?}
    G -- No --> H[Set theta_t equals theta_t plus 1]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I[Return optimal theta_star]
    I --> J([End])

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1A3A6E,color:#FFFFFF
    style J fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1A3A6E,color:#FFFFFF
    style G fill:#F5A623,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style I fill:#7ED321,stroke:#3A6E0F,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 Variant Comparison Block Architecture

The three GD variants differ only in how they feed data into the gradient computation block. The architecture below clarifies the trade-off structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Data Source Layer]
        FULL[Full Training Set n samples]
        SINGLE[Single Sample]
        BATCH[Mini Batch b samples]
    end

    SRC --> PIPE[Gradient Computation Engine]
    PIPE --> UPD[Parameter Update Block]
    UPD --> COST[Cost Function Evaluator]
    COST --> CONV{Convergence Met?}
    CONV -- Yes --> STOP[Optimal Parameters]
    CONV -- No --> PIPE

    FULL -.-> L1[Label BATCH GD]
    SINGLE -.-> L2[Label SGD]
    BATCH -.-> L3[Label MINI BATCH GD]

    style SRC fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1A3A6E,color:#000000
    style PIPE fill:#F5A623,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style UPD fill:#F5A623,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style CONV fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style STOP fill:#7ED321,stroke:#3A6E0F,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 Learning Rate Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LR[Learning Rate Selection] --> Q{Is eta optimal?}
    Q -- Too Small --> SLOW[Slow Convergence. Stuck in plateau. High epoch cost.]
    Q -- Too Large --> OSC[Oscillation or Divergence. NaN cost. Model collapse.]
    Q -- Optimal --> FAST[Monotonic cost decrease. Quadratic convergence on convex bowl.]

    style LR fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1A3A6E,color:#FFFFFF
    style Q fill:#F5A623,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style SLOW fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style OSC fill:#D0021B,stroke:#7A0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style FAST fill:#7ED321,stroke:#3A6E0F,color:#FFFFFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the Gradient Descent algorithm. Write its update rule and explain the role of the learning rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gradient Descent is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm that minimizes a differentiable cost function $J(\theta)$ by repeatedly updating parameters in the direction of steepest descent.

**Update Rule (1 mark):**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta_t)
$$

**Role of Learning Rate (2 marks):** The learning rate $\eta$ controls the size of the step taken in the direction of the negative gradient. A small $\eta$ yields slow but stable convergence; a large $\eta$ risks overshooting the minimum or causing divergence. It is the most critical hyperparameter, typically tuned via grid search or learning rate schedules.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Batch Gradient Descent and Stochastic Gradient Descent.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Batch GD | Stochastic GD |
|---|---|---|
| Data per update | Entire dataset ($n$) | Single sample |
| Updates per epoch | 1 | $n$ |
| Computational cost per update | High ($O(n)$) | Low ($O(1)$) |
| Noise in gradient | Smooth | High variance |
| Convergence path | Smooth, deterministic | Noisy, oscillatory |
| Suitable for | Small datasets, offline | Large datasets, online |

**Key trade-off:** SGD converges faster per epoch but with high variance; Batch GD is stable but computationally expensive for large $n$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the Gradient Descent algorithm with its step-by-step procedure. Discuss the convergence criteria and the role of the learning rate in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the cost function $J(\theta) = \theta^2 - 4\theta + 5$. Apply three iterations of Gradient Descent starting from $\theta_0 = 0$ with learning rate $\eta = 0.2$. Show all calculations and state whether the algorithm is converging. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Formal statement (1 mark):** GD iteratively updates parameters to minimize $J(\theta)$:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)
$$

**Step 2 — Algorithm procedure (3 marks):**

1. Initialize $\theta_0$ (randomly or to zeros).
2. Compute gradient $\nabla J(\theta_t)$.
3. Update: $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$.
4. Check convergence: $\vert J(\theta_{t+1}) - J(\theta_t) \vert < \epsilon$ OR $\Vert \nabla J(\theta_t) \Vert < \epsilon$.
5. If not converged, set $t \leftarrow t+1$ and return to step 2.

**Step 3 — Convergence criteria (1 mark):** The algorithm halts when the change in cost falls below a tolerance $\epsilon$, or when the gradient norm is near zero (i.e., a stationary point is reached).

**Step 4 — Learning rate role (2 marks):** $\eta$ determines step magnitude. Optimal $\eta$ ensures monotonic descent; poor $\eta$ causes divergence or stagnation. Adaptive methods (Adam, RMSProp) adjust $\eta$ per-parameter.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient (1 mark):**

$$
J'(\theta) = 2\theta - 4
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize (0.5 mark):** $\theta_0 = 0$, $\eta = 0.2$.

**Step 3 — Iterations (5 marks):**

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\theta_1 = 0 - 0.2 \cdot (2 \cdot 0 - 4) = 0 - 0.2(-4) = 0.800
$$

$$
J(\theta_1) = (0.8)^2 - 4(0.8) + 5 = 0.64 - 3.2 + 5 = 2.440
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\theta_2 = 0.8 - 0.2 \cdot (2 \cdot 0.8 - 4) = 0.8 - 0.2(-2.4) = 1.280
$$

$$
J(\theta_2) = (1.28)^2 - 4(1.28) + 5 = 1.6384 - 5.12 + 5 = 1.518
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\theta_3 = 1.28 - 0.2 \cdot (2 \cdot 1.28 - 4) = 1.28 - 0.2(-1.44) = 1.568
$$

$$
J(\theta_3) = (1.568)^2 - 4(1.568) + 5 = 2.4586 - 6.272 + 5 = 1.187
$$

**Step 4 — Convergence statement (0.5 mark):** The cost values $J(\theta_0) = 5 \to J(\theta_1) = 2.44 \to J(\theta_2) = 1.52 \to J(\theta_3) = 1.19$ are decreasing monotonically. The true minimum is at $\theta^* = 2$ with $J(\theta^*) = 1$. The sequence is converging correctly.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the Gradient Descent update rule from first principles using the Taylor series approximation. Explain why the negative gradient direction guarantees descent. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the three variants of Gradient Descent — Batch, Stochastic, and Mini-Batch — with respect to convergence speed, computational cost, and noise. State the Robbins-Monro conditions for guaranteed convergence of SGD. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Setup the Taylor expansion (2 marks):**

$$
J(\theta + \Delta \theta) \approx J(\theta) + \nabla J(\theta)^{\top} \Delta \theta
$$

This first-order approximation is valid for small $\vert \Delta \theta \vert$.

**Step 2 — Impose the descent requirement (1 mark):**

$$
\nabla J(\theta)^{\top} \Delta \theta < 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cauchy-Schwarz inequality (2 marks):** The dot product is bounded below by $-\Vert \nabla J(\theta) \Vert \cdot \Vert \Delta \theta \Vert$, achieved when $\Delta \theta = -\alpha \nabla J(\theta)$ for any scalar $\alpha > 0$.

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain the update rule (1 mark):** Setting $\alpha = \eta$:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)
$$

**Step 5 — Justification of negative gradient (1 mark):** Since $\nabla J$ points along the direction of steepest **ascent**, stepping in the $-\nabla J$ direction produces the largest possible local **decrease** in $J$, guaranteeing descent (modulo step-size limits).

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Batch GD characteristics (2 marks):** Uses all $n$ samples per update. Produces a smooth, deterministic trajectory but is computationally infeasible for large $n$. Memory cost is $O(n)$.

**Step 2 — SGD characteristics (2 marks):** Uses one sample per update. $n$ updates per epoch produce a noisy but rapidly progressing trajectory. Escapes shallow local minima due to noise. Memory cost is $O(1)$.

**Step 3 — Mini-batch GD characteristics (1.5 marks):** Uses $b$ samples (e.g., $b = 32$). Balances variance reduction with computational efficiency. The de facto standard in modern deep learning.

**Step 4 — Robbins-Monro convergence conditions (1.5 marks):** For SGD to converge almost surely to the optimum, the learning rate schedule $\{\eta_t\}$ must satisfy:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \eta_t^2 < \infty
$$

The first condition guarantees that the algorithm can reach any point; the second ensures the noise decays sufficiently fast.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Gradient Descent questions:**
> 1. **Sign error (–2 marks):** Forgetting the negative sign in the update rule; writing $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \eta \nabla J$ — this is **gradient ascent**, not descent.
> 2. **Skipping intermediate iterations (–2 marks):** KTU evaluators demand each iteration's gradient and update shown explicitly. Jumping to the final answer is penalized.
> 3. **Confusing batch size with epoch (–1 mark):** One epoch = one full pass through the data. In SGD, one epoch contains $n$ updates; in batch GD, one epoch = one update.
> 4. **Omitting convergence justification (–1 mark):** Always state whether the sequence is approaching the true optimum.
> 5. **No units / no formula recall (–1 mark):** Always write the governing equation before plugging in numbers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Equation (memorize verbatim):** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla J(\theta_t)$.
- **Three Variants:** Batch (smooth, expensive), Stochastic (noisy, cheap), Mini-Batch (balanced, industry standard).
- **Learning Rate Rules of Thumb:** Start with $\eta = 0.1$ or $\eta = 0.01$. Use **learning rate decay** for fine-tuning near minima.
- **Convergence Conditions:** Robbins-Monro: $\sum \eta_t = \infty$ and $\sum \eta_t^2 < \infty$.
- **Quadratic Convergence Speed:** For convex $J(\theta)$, error shrinks geometrically with ratio $(1 - \eta a)$ per iteration.
- **Three Failure Modes:** Too small $\eta$ (slow), too large $\eta$ (oscillation/divergence), saddle points (slow plateau).
- **Always** compute the gradient first, then plug into the update rule — never reverse the order.
- **Mini-batch size** is typically a power of 2: $32, 64, 128, 256$ (GPU memory alignment).
- **Feature scaling** (standardization) is a prerequisite for stable GD on multi-feature datasets.
- **GD is local:** it finds a local (not necessarily global) minimum for non-convex functions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Regression** is a **supervised machine learning algorithm** belonging to the family of **regression models** that establishes a **linear relationship** between one or more independent variables (predictors/features) and a continuous dependent variable (target/output) by fitting a linear equation to the observed data.

Mathematically, for a dataset with $n$ observations and $p$ features, the general form is expressed as:

$$
\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_p x_p + \varepsilon
$$

Where:
- $\hat{y}$ = predicted (estimated) value of the dependent variable
- $\beta_0$ = **intercept** (value of $\hat{y}$ when all $x_i = 0$)
- $\beta_i$ = **regression coefficients** (weights/parameters)
- $x_i$ = independent variables (features)
- $\varepsilon$ = **error term** (residual/random noise)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Best Fit Line" Through Scattered Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cricket Coach's Prediction**

Imagine a cricket coach plotting the relationship between **hours of practice** (independent variable, $x$) and **runs scored in a match** (dependent variable, $y$) for 50 players. The data points are scattered all over the graph because many other factors (fitness, mental state, pitch conditions) influence performance.

The coach's job is to draw **a single straight line** through this scattered cloud of points such that the line:
1. **Passes as close as possible to ALL the points simultaneously**
2. **Captures the general trend** (more practice → more runs, generally)
3. **Minimizes the gap** between the predicted and actual runs for every player

This line is precisely the **Linear Regression model**! Each data point's vertical distance from the line is an "error" (residual), and the line is positioned where the **sum of squared errors is minimized** — this is the foundational **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** principle.

### 1.3 Types of Linear Regression in the KTU Syllabus

| **Variant** | **Description** | **Use Case Context** |
|-------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Simple Linear Regression (SLR)** | One independent variable → One dependent variable | Predicting salary from years of experience |
| **Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)** | Multiple independent variables → One dependent variable | House price prediction (area, rooms, location) |
| **Polynomial Regression** | Non-linear relationship captured via polynomial terms | Population growth, epidemic curves |
| **Ridge / Lasso Regression** | Regularized versions to prevent overfitting | High-dimensional datasets |

### 1.4 Key Terminology Quick-Reference

> [!TIP]
> - **Independent Variable (Regressor):** The input feature(s), denoted as $X$.
> - **Dependent Variable (Response):** The output to be predicted, denoted as $Y$.
> - **Hypothesis Function:** $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$ (alternative notation used in some textbooks).
> - **Residual:** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ — the unexplained error.
> - **Fitted Line:** The final regression line after optimal coefficient estimation.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Workflow of Linear Regression (Step-by-Step Logic)

The linear regression process follows a **6-stage operational pipeline**:

1. **Data Acquisition & Preprocessing**
   - Collect dataset $(X, Y)$ with $n$ samples and $p$ features.
   - Handle **missing values** (imputation) and **outliers** (removal/capping).
   - Perform **feature scaling** (Standardization: $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ or Min-Max normalization) for gradient-based optimization.

2. **Hypothesis Formulation**
   - Define a linear hypothesis: $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \dots + \theta_p x_p$.
   - In matrix form: $h_\theta(X) = X\theta$ where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$ (augmented with bias column of 1's).

3. **Cost Function Definition**
   - **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** is the standard cost function $J(\theta)$:
   $$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$
   - The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience to cancel the exponent during differentiation.

4. **Parameter Optimization (The Core Learning Step)**
   - **Method A — Closed-Form (Normal Equation):**
     $$\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$
   - **Method B — Iterative (Gradient Descent):**
     $$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} J(\theta)$$
   - where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** hyperparameter.

5. **Model Validation & Assumption Checking**
   - Test for linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, and normality of residuals.
   - Detect **multicollinearity** using Variance Inflation Factor (VIF).

6. **Performance Evaluation**
   - Compute $R^2$, Adjusted $R^2$, RMSE, MAE on test set.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table is the Most Frequently Asked Formulas in KTU Board Exams. Memorize These.**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Units** |
|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Simple Linear Regression Model | $\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted value (unitless/same as $y$) |
| Slope (Coefficient) $\beta_1$ | $\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ = means of $x$ and $y$ |
| Intercept $\beta_0$ | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | Dimensionless (intercept point on y-axis) |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Squared units of $y$ |
| Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{n}}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| Mean Absolute Error (MAE) | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Same units as $y$ |
| Sum of Squared Residuals (SSR/SSRes) | $\text{SSR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Explained by residuals |
| Total Sum of Squares (SST) | $\text{SST} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Total variance in $y$ |
| Regression Sum of Squares (SSR) | $\text{SSR} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Variance explained by model |
| Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$) | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}} = \dfrac{\text{SS}_{\text{reg}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$, dimensionless |
| Adjusted $R^2$ | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - (1-R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | $p$ = number of predictors, $n$ = samples |
| Normal Equation (Matrix Form) | $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ | Requires $X^T X$ to be invertible |
| Gradient Descent Update Rule | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ | $\alpha$ = learning rate (hyperparameter) |
| Gradient (Partial Derivative) | $\dfrac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$ | Vectorized as $\nabla J = \frac{1}{n} X^T(X\theta - Y)$ |
| Covariance between $X$ and $Y$ | $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{n-1}$ | Units: product of $X$ and $Y$ units |
| Variance of $X$ | $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}$ | Squared units of $X$ |
| Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) | $\text{VIF}_i = \dfrac{1}{1 - R_i^2}$ | $R_i^2$ = $R^2$ of $X_i$ regressed on other $X$'s |
| Standard Error of Estimate | $S_e = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{n-2}}$ (for SLR) | Same units as $y$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Use Cases Aligned with KTU Application-Based Questions:**
>
> - **Civil Engineering:** Predicting concrete compressive strength from cement, water, and aggregate ratios.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Estimating tool wear based on cutting speed and feed rate.
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Modeling power consumption as a function of voltage and current.
> - **Economics & Finance:** Forecasting GDP growth from interest rate, inflation, and unemployment.
> - **Healthcare:** Predicting patient blood pressure from age, weight, and cholesterol levels.
> - **Marketing:** Estimating sales based on advertising spend across TV, radio, and digital channels.

### 2.4 Critical Assumptions of Linear Regression (Gauss-Markov Theorem)

For the OLS estimators to be the **Best Linear Unbiased Estimators (BLUE)**, the following assumptions **MUST** hold:

1. **Linearity:** Relationship between $X$ and $Y$ is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence of Errors:** Residuals $e_i$ are uncorrelated (no autocorrelation).
3. **Homoscedasticity:** Constant variance of residuals: $\text{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$.
4. **Normality of Errors:** $e_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (required for inference, not estimation).
5. **No Multicollinearity:** Independent variables should not be highly correlated.
6. **No Measurement Error in $X$:** Predictors are measured without error.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Derivation of Slope ($\beta_1$) and Intercept ($\beta_0$) via Ordinary Least Squares

**Problem Setup:** We are given $n$ data points $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$. We want to find a straight line $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ that **minimizes the sum of squared residuals** $\text{SS}_{\text{res}}$.

**Step 1: Define the Sum of Squared Residuals (SSR) Objective Function**

$$
\text{SS}_{\text{res}}(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i) \right)^2
$$

**Step 2: To minimize $\text{SS}_{\text{res}}$, we take the partial derivative with respect to $\beta_0$ and set it to zero.**

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the equation from Step 2 by dividing both sides by 2 and changing sign:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - n\beta_0 - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 0
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\beta_0$:**

$$
n\beta_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

$$
\beta_0 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n} - \beta_1 \cdot \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n} = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

**This proves that the regression line ALWAYS passes through the point $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$ — a key result students must remember.**

**Step 5: Take the partial derivative of $\text{SS}_{\text{res}}$ with respect to $\beta_1$ and set it to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial \, \text{SS}_{\text{res}}}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

**Step 6: Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ into the equation from Step 5:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \left( y_i - (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

**Step 7: Expand the parentheses and simplify:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \bar{y} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + \beta_1 \bar{x} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 = 0
$$

**Step 8: Since $\sum x_i = n\bar{x}$, the second and third terms cancel out:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y} + \beta_1 n\bar{x}^2 - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 = 0
$$

**Step 9: Group the $\beta_1$ terms on one side:**

$$
\beta_1 \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2 \right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}
$$

**Step 10: Recognize the algebraic identity $\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2 = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$, and similarly for $y$. Solve for $\beta_1$:**

$$
\boxed{\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X, Y)}{\text{Var}(X)}}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Derivation of the Normal Equation in Matrix Form

**Problem Setup:** We want to find $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{p+1}$ that minimizes $J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n}(X\theta - Y)^T(X\theta - Y)$ for design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (p+1)}$ and target vector $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

**Step 1: Expand the cost function $J(\theta)$:**

$$
J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \left( X\theta - Y \right)^T \left( X\theta - Y \right) = \frac{1}{2n} \left( \theta^T X^T X \theta - 2 \theta^T X^T Y + Y^T Y \right)
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate $J(\theta)$ with respect to $\theta$ using matrix calculus rules** (specifically, $\frac{\partial \theta^T A \theta}{\partial \theta} = 2A\theta$ for symmetric $A$, and $\frac{\partial \theta^T b}{\partial \theta} = b$):

$$
\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2n} \left( 2 X^T X \theta - 2 X^T Y \right) = \frac{1}{n} \left( X^T X \theta - X^T Y \right)
$$

**Step 3: Set the gradient equal to zero (stationary point condition for minimum):**

$$
X^T X \theta - X^T Y = 0
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\theta$ by left-multiplying both sides by $(X^T X)^{-1}$ (assuming invertibility):**

$$
\boxed{\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y}
$$

This is the **Normal Equation**. It provides a **closed-form (one-shot) solution** without iteration.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Gradient Descent Update Rule Derivation

**Problem Setup:** Iteratively minimize $J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$ where $h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x$.

**Step 1: Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $\theta_0$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the partial derivative of $J$ with respect to $\theta_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the gradient descent update rule simultaneously for both parameters:**

$$
\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4: Convergence Criterion — Gradient Descent is repeated until either:**

- $J(\theta)$ changes by less than a tolerance $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$).
- A maximum number of iterations is reached.
- $\vert \nabla J(\theta) \vert < \epsilon$ (gradient is approximately zero).

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Computation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem [KTU University Exam Style]:**
> Given the data points $(1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 5), (4, 4), (5, 5)$, find the regression line $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$.

**Step 1: Compute the means:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+4+5+4+5}{5} = 4
$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerator (covariance numerator) $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) &= (1-3)(2-4) + (2-3)(4-4) + (3-3)(5-4) + (4-3)(4-4) + (5-3)(5-4) \\
&= (-2)(-2) + (-1)(0) + (0)(1) + (1)(0) + (2)(1) \\
&= 4 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator (variance of $x$) $\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 &= (1-3)^2 + (2-3)^2 + (3-3)^2 + (4-3)^2 + (5-3)^2 \\
&= 4 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 4 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate $\beta_1$ and $\beta_0$:**

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 4 - (0.6)(3) = 4 - 1.8 = 2.2
$$

**Step 5: Final Regression Line:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = 2.2 + 0.6x}
$$

**Step 6: Compute $R^2$ for validation:**

$$
\hat{y}_i = 2.2 + 0.6x_i: \quad \hat{y}_1=2.8, \hat{y}_2=3.4, \hat{y}_3=4.0, \hat{y}_4=4.6, \hat{y}_5=5.2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SS}_{\text{res}} &= (2-2.8)^2 + (4-3.4)^2 + (5-4.0)^2 + (4-4.6)^2 + (5-5.2)^2 \\
&= 0.64 + 0.36 + 1.00 + 0.36 + 0.04 = 2.40
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{SS}_{\text{tot}} = (2-4)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (5-4)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 4 + 0 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 6
$$

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{2.40}{6} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

**Interpretation:** 60% of the variance in $y$ is explained by the linear relationship with $x$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Linear Regression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawData[Raw Dataset X Y] --> preprocess[Step 1 Data Preprocessing]
    preprocess -->|Missing Values Imputation| cleanData
    preprocess -->|Outlier Removal| cleanData
    preprocess -->|Feature Scaling| cleanData
    cleanData[Step 2 Cleaned Data Matrix X nxd] --> split[Step 3 Train Test Split]
    split --> trainSet[Training Set 80 percent]
    split --> testSet[Testing Set 20 percent]
    trainSet --> hypothesis[Step 4 Hypothesis Function h theta x equals theta0 plus theta1x]
    hypothesis --> costFn[Step 5 Cost Function MSE Calculation]
    costFn --> optimizer[Step 6 Optimization Engine]
    optimizer -->|Normal Equation Closed Form| normalEq[theta equals XTX inverse XTY]
    optimizer -->|Gradient Descent Iterative| gradDesc[Repeat until convergence theta j minus alpha dJ dtheta j]
    normalEq --> coefficients[Step 7 Optimized Coefficients theta0 theta1]
    gradDesc --> coefficients
    coefficients --> predict[Step 8 Prediction on Test Set yhat equals Xtest theta]
    predict --> evaluate[Step 9 Model Evaluation]
    evaluate --> r2[R2 Adjusted R2]
    evaluate --> rmse[RMSE MAE]
    evaluate --> residual[Residual Plot Analysis]
    r2 --> valid[Step 10 Validity Check Gauss Markov Assumptions]
    rmse --> valid
    residual --> valid
    valid -->|Assumptions Satisfied| deploy[Step 11 Deployment Ready Model]
    valid -->|Assumptions Violated| fix[Step 12 Remediation Feature Engineering Ridge Lasso]
    fix --> retrain[Retrain Model]
    retrain --> hypothesis
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Choosing Between Normal Equation and Gradient Descent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Choosing Optimizer] --> checkN{Dataset size n is small less than 10000}
    checkN -->|Yes small n| checkInv{X transpose X is invertible}
    checkInv -->|Yes invertible| useNormal[Use Normal Equation]
    checkInv -->|No singular| useGD[Use Gradient Descent or Pseudoinverse]
    checkN -->|No large n| checkFeat{Number of features p is small less than 1000}
    checkFeat -->|Yes small p| useGD[Use Gradient Descent]
    checkFeat -->|No large p| checkReg{Is regularization needed}
    checkReg -->|Yes Ridge Lasso| useGD[Use Gradient Descent]
    checkReg -->|No standard OLS| useNormal[Use Normal Equation with caution]
    useNormal --> note1[Note Closed form One shot solution O of n cubed complexity]
    useGD --> note2[Note Iterative Requires learning rate tuning Scales to large n]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initTheta[Initialize theta0 theta1 randomly or as zeros] --> computeGrad[Compute Gradient of J theta]

    checkConv -->|Not yet Maximum iterations not reached| computeGrad
    checkConv -->|Yes Converged| finalTheta[Final Optimal Parameters theta star]
    noteLR[Learning rate alpha is critical] -.-> updateTheta
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Linear Regression. List any four assumptions of linear regression. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Linear Regression is a supervised machine learning technique that models the relationship between a scalar dependent variable $Y$ and one or more independent variables $X$ by fitting a linear equation to the observed data. The general form is $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_p x_p + \varepsilon$.

**Four Assumptions of Linear Regression:**

1. **Linearity:** The relationship between independent variables and the dependent variable is linear in parameters.
2. **Independence of Errors:** The residuals (errors) are statistically independent of each other (no autocorrelation).
3. **Homoscedasticity:** The variance of the error terms is constant across all levels of the independent variables: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$.
4. **Normality of Errors:** The error terms are normally distributed: $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Additionally, **no multicollinearity** between independent variables is also accepted as the fourth assumption.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the Cost Function in Linear Regression? Write the formula for Mean Squared Error (MSE) and explain its role. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Function Definition:** The cost function in linear regression quantifies the **average error** between the predicted values $\hat{y}_i$ and the actual observed values $y_i$ across the entire training dataset. It is the objective function that we minimize to find the optimal regression coefficients.

**Formula for Mean Squared Error (MSE):**

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
$$

**Role of MSE:**

- It measures the **average of the squared differences** between predicted and actual values, penalizing larger errors more heavily due to the squaring operation.
- It provides a **smooth, convex, and differentiable** function which makes it suitable for optimization via gradient descent.
- The goal of training a linear regression model is to find $\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_p$ such that MSE is **minimized** (this is the Ordinary Least Squares principle).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the slope $\beta_1$ and intercept $\beta_0$ of a simple linear regression line $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ using the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method. State clearly the two normal equations obtained. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following data for the number of hours studied ($x$) and the marks scored ($y$) by 6 students, find the regression equation of $y$ on $x$. Also predict the marks of a student who studied for 7 hours. **(7 Marks)**

| Hours ($x$) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | 12 |
|-------------|---|---|---|---|----|----|
| Marks ($y$) | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

> **Step 1: Define the Residual Sum of Squares (RSS) Function:**
> The OLS method aims to minimize the sum of squared residuals between the observed values $y_i$ and the predicted values $\hat{y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$.

$$
S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)^2
$$

> **Step 2: Apply the First-Order Necessary Condition for Minimum:**
> To minimize $S$, take the partial derivative with respect to $\beta_0$ and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0
$$

> **Step 3: Simplify the First Normal Equation:**

$$
\sum y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad n\beta_0 = \sum y_i - \beta_1 \sum x_i
$$

> **Step 4: Take the partial derivative with respect to $\beta_1$ and set it to zero:**

$$
\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0
$$

> **Step 5: Simplify the Second Normal Equation:**

$$
\sum x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2
$$

> **Step 6: Solve the System of Two Linear Equations:**
> The two normal equations are:
> $$\sum y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i$$
> $$\sum x_i y_i = \beta_0 \sum x_i + \beta_1 \sum x_i^2$$
> Solving simultaneously (e.g., via substitution or Cramer's rule):

$$
\boxed{\beta_1 = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = \frac{\sum y_i}{n} - \beta_1 \frac{\sum x_i}{n}}
$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

> **Step 1: Construct the Computation Table:**

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i^2$ | $x_i y_i$ |
|-------|-------|---------|-----------|
| 2     | 10    | 4       | 20        |
| 4     | 20    | 16      | 80        |
| 6     | 30    | 36      | 180       |
| 8     | 40    | 64      | 320       |
| 10    | 50    | 100     | 500       |
| 12    | 60    | 144     | 720       |
| $\sum x_i = 42$ | $\sum y_i = 210$ | $\sum x_i^2 = 364$ | $\sum x_i y_i = 1820$ |

> **Step 2: Calculate the necessary quantities:**

$$
n = 6, \quad \bar{x} = \frac{42}{6} = 7, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{210}{6} = 35
$$

> **Step 3: Compute $\beta_1$ using the derived formula:**

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{6(1820) - (42)(210)}{6(364) - (42)^2} = \frac{10920 - 8820}{2184 - 1764} = \frac{2100}{420} = 5
$$

> **Step 4: Compute $\beta_0$:**

$$
\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 35 - 5(7) = 35 - 35 = 0
$$

> **Step 5: Final Regression Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{y} = 5x}
$$

> **Step 6: Prediction for $x = 7$ hours:**

$$
\hat{y} = 5(7) = 35 \text{ marks}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The model predicts that a student who studies for 7 hours will score approximately **35 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Gradient Descent algorithm** for linear regression. Derive the update rules for parameters $\theta_0$ and $\theta_1$ step by step. What is the role of the learning rate $\alpha$? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Multicollinearity** in multiple linear regression? Explain the **Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** with formula. How does multicollinearity affect the model's performance, and what are the remedies? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

> **Step 1: Conceptual Overview:**
> Gradient Descent is an **iterative first-order optimization algorithm** used to minimize the cost function $J(\theta_0, \theta_1)$ by updating the parameters in the direction of the **steepest descent** (negative gradient). It is preferred over the Normal Equation when the dataset is large or when $X^T X$ is non-invertible.

> **Step 2: Define the Cost Function:**

$$
J(\theta_0, \theta_1) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2, \quad h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x
$$

> **Step 3: Compute the Partial Derivatives (Gradients):**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_0} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}
$$

> **Step 4: Apply the Update Rule Simultaneously:**

$$
\theta_0 := \theta_0 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 := \theta_1 - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x^{(i)}
$$

> **Step 5: Iterative Procedure:**
> The update is repeated for a fixed number of iterations or until $J(\theta)$ converges (change is negligible). The parameters approach the global minimum since $J(\theta)$ is **convex**.

> **Step 6: Role of Learning Rate $\alpha$:**
> - **$\alpha$ too small** → Slow convergence (many iterations needed).
> - **$\alpha$ too large** → May overshoot the minimum, causing oscillation or divergence.
> - **$\alpha$ optimal** → Fast and stable convergence to global minimum.
> - Typical values: $0.001, 0.01, 0.1$ (tuned via experimentation).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

> **Step 1: Define Multicollinearity:**
> Multicollinearity is a phenomenon in multiple linear regression where **two or more independent variables are highly linearly correlated** with each other. This makes it difficult to isolate the individual effect of each predictor on the dependent variable.

> **Step 2: Detection Using VIF:**
> The **Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)** quantifies the severity of multicollinearity:

$$
\text{VIF}_i = \frac{1}{1 - R_i^2}
$$

> Where $R_i^2$ is the coefficient of determination obtained when variable $X_i$ is regressed on all other independent variables.

> **Step 3: Interpretation:**
> - $\text{VIF} = 1$: No correlation between $X_i$ and other predictors (ideal).
> - $1 < \text{VIF} < 5$: Moderate correlation (acceptable).
> - $\text{VIF} \geq 5$ or $10$: Severe multicollinearity (problematic; needs remediation).

> **Step 4: Effects on Model Performance:**
> 1. **Inflated standard errors** of regression coefficients → reduces statistical significance.
> 2. **Unstable coefficient estimates** → small data changes cause large coefficient swings.
> 3. **Reduced interpretability** — difficult to determine the true contribution of each feature.
> 4. **Counterintuitive signs** of coefficients (e.g., positive $X$ predicting negative $Y$).

> **Step 5: Remedies:**
> 1. **Remove highly correlated variables** (drop one of the correlated pair).
> 2. **Use regularization:** Apply **Ridge Regression** ($L_2$ penalty) or **Lasso Regression** ($L_1$ penalty).
> 3. **Principal Component Analysis (PCA):** Transform correlated variables into uncorrelated principal components.
> 4. **Combine correlated variables** into a single composite feature.
> 5. **Collect more data** to reduce variance of estimates.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT CAUSE MARK DEDUCTION IN KTU BOARD EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Linearity Assumption (–1 Mark):** Many students state only 2-3 assumptions; KTU examiners expect a minimum of **4-5 assumptions** for full credit.
>
> 2. **Confusing $R^2$ Interpretation (–1 to –2 Marks):** $R^2$ does NOT mean "the model is 95% accurate." Correct phrasing: **"95% of the variance in the dependent variable is explained by the independent variable(s)."**
>
> 3. **Not Simplifying the Cost Function (–1 Mark):** The factor $\frac{1}{2n}$ in MSE is intentionally chosen — examiners often look for explicit mention of why it is there (convenience for derivative cancellation).
>
> 4. **Sign Error in Gradient Descent (–2 Marks):** The update rule is $\theta := \theta - \alpha \nabla J$, NOT $\theta := \theta + \alpha \nabla J$. A sign flip changes the algorithm from minimization to maximization — a critical error.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Calculation Table (–1 Mark):** Always tabulate $\sum x, \sum y, \sum x^2, \sum xy$ before plugging into the formula. Examiners reward systematic presentation.
>
> 6. **Not Mentioning the Intercept Equation (–1 Mark):** When deriving $\beta_1$ using OLS, you MUST state $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ alongside, even if not explicitly asked.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Linear Regression (Module 2)**
>
> **Core Equation (SLR):** $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$
> **Core Equation (MLR):** $\hat{y} = X\theta$ in matrix form.
>
> **Closed-Form Solution:** $\theta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ — Best for small $n$, requires $X^T X$ to be invertible.
>
> **Iterative Solution:** Gradient Descent — Scales to large $n$, requires learning rate tuning.
>
> **Cost Function:** $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ — Always non-negative, convex, and differentiable.
>
> **Key Coefficients:**
> - $\beta_1 = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\text{Var}(X)} = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$
> - $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$
> - The regression line **always passes through** $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.
>
> **Performance Metrics:**
> - $R^2 \in [0,1]$: Higher is better; $R^2 = 0.8$ means 80% variance explained.
> - Adjusted $R^2$ penalizes adding non-useful predictors.
> - RMSE is in the same units as $y$ (most interpretable).
>
> **Five Gaussians-Markov Assumptions:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No Multicollinearity.
>
> **Multicollinearity Detection:** VIF $> 5$ is problematic; remedies include Ridge/Lasso regularization and PCA.
>
> **Gradient Descent Update:** $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ — Simultaneous update, sign is **NEGATIVE**.
>
