## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the PCA Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end PCA workflow** as implemented in a standard engineering data-pipeline. It documents the **functional flow** between the *data ingestion*, *statistical preprocessing*, *linear-algebra core*, and *output consumer* stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Matrix X n by p] --> B[Compute Feature Means mu]
    B --> C[Mean Centering X minus mu]
    C --> D{Features On Different Scales?}
    D -- YES --> E[Standardization Z score Normalization]
    D -- NO --> F[Skip Standardization]
    E --> G[Build Sample Covariance Matrix C]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Solve Eigenequation C v equals lambda v]
    H --> I[Sort Eigenvalues in Descending Order]
    I --> J[Choose Number of Components k using Scree Plot]
    J --> K[Form Projection Matrix W of size p by k]
    K --> L[Project Z equals X centered times W]
    L --> M[Lower Dimensional Representation Z n by k]
    M --> N[Reconstruction Optional X hat equals Z times W transpose]
    N --> O[Compute Reconstruction Error SSE equals sum lambda discarded]
    O --> P[Downstream Tasks Visualization Clustering Classification Anomaly Detection]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    style P fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style M fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow-shaded block $\mathbf{C}$ and the eigensolver represent the **linear-algebra core** of PCA. The pink-shaded block $\mathbf{Z}$ is the **information bottleneck** — all downstream learning algorithms consume only this compressed representation.

---

### 4.2 Scree-Plot Decision Diagram

Choosing $k$ is a frequent KTU exam topic. The decision logic for selecting the number of components is summarized below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute All p Eigenvalues lambda 1 to lambda p] --> B[Plot Scree Plot lambda i vs Component Index i]
    B --> C{Decision Rule}
    C -- KAISER RULE --> D[Keep Components with lambda greater than 1]
    C -- CUMULATIVE VARIANCE --> E[Keep Smallest k Such That Cumulative Variance greater than 95 percent]
    C -- ELBOW RULE --> F[Locate Sharp Bend in Scree Plot]
    D --> G[Final k Value Selected]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Form Projection Matrix W of size p by k]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    style G fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    style H fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-fact:** The **Kaiser criterion** (keep $\lambda_i > 1$) is only meaningful when data has been **standardized** to unit variance — otherwise eigenvalues are not directly comparable to 1.

---

### 4.3 Geometric Interpretation Schematic (Variance Maximization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Data Cloud Ellipsoid in p D] --> B[Center Cloud at Origin]
    B --> C[Find Longest Principal Axis PC1]
    B --> D[Find Orthogonal Second Longest PC2]
    C --> E[Project Onto PC1 axis]
    D --> F[Project Onto PC2 axis]
    E --> G[Reduced 1 D Representation]
    F --> H[Full 2 D Preserved Representation]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    style G fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    style H fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> The data cloud is an **ellipsoid** in $p$-space. The principal axes of this ellipsoid are *exactly* the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, and the *squared semi-axis lengths* are proportional to the eigenvalues. PCA finds the natural orientation of the data.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
> **Q:** *What is the geometric interpretation of Principal Component Analysis? State the relationship between eigenvalues and the variance captured.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Geometrically, PCA rotates the original coordinate axes to align with the **principal axes of the data ellipsoid**. The original data points are projected onto these new orthogonal axes.*
> *Mathematically, the principal components are the **eigenvectors** of the sample covariance matrix $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$.*
> *The variance of the data along the $i$-th principal component equals the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda_i$. The fraction of total variance retained by PC$_i$ is $\eta_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}$ (explained variance ratio).*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Q:** *Why is it mandatory to mean-center (and sometimes standardize) data before applying PCA? What happens if this step is skipped?*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *PCA is a **variance-based** transformation. Mean-centering shifts the data cloud to the origin so that the computed variance reflects spread about the centroid, not bias due to offset. Without centering, the first principal component would be biased toward the direction of the data's mean rather than the direction of maximum variance.*
> *Standardization (z-score scaling) is required when features are measured in **incompatible units** (e.g., age vs salary); without it, features with larger numerical range dominate the principal components, producing a misleading result.*
> *In short: skipping these steps leads to PCs that **misrepresent the true underlying structure** of the data.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – 14-Mark Question A]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q:** *(a)* Derive mathematically that **the principal components of a dataset are the eigenvectors of its sample covariance matrix** and that the variance captured by each component equals its corresponding eigenvalue. State the optimization objective and the role of the Lagrange multiplier. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* For the dataset $\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 3 \\ 5 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$, perform PCA and reduce it to a **single dimension**. Show all calculations: mean-centering, covariance matrix, eigenvalues, eigenvectors, explained variance ratio, and the projected 1-D representation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Theoretical Derivation (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Formulate the objective:* Given centered data $\mathbf{X}_c$, find unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes projected variance.

$$\max_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1$$

*Step 2 — Build the Lagrangian:* Introduce multiplier $\lambda$ for the unit-norm constraint.

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

*Step 3 — Set gradient to zero:*

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\mathcal{L} = 2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathbf{C}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$$

*Step 4 — Conclusion:* This is the **eigenequation** of $\mathbf{C}$. Therefore, the direction of maximum variance $\mathbf{w}^*$ must be an eigenvector. Substituting back:

$$\mathbf{w}^{*\top}\mathbf{C}\mathbf{w}^{*} = \mathbf{w}^{*\top}\lambda\mathbf{w}^{*} = \lambda$$

Hence variance captured = eigenvalue $\lambda$. Selecting the largest $\lambda$ gives PC1; inductively the next orthogonal eigenvector gives PC2, and so on. ✓ (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical PCA (7 marks)**

*Step 1 — Means:*
$\mu_1 = \frac{2+3+4+5}{4} = 3.5$, $\mu_2 = \frac{1+2+3+4}{4} = 2.5$.

*Step 2 — Centered data:*
$$\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -1.5 \\ -0.5 & -0.5 \\ \;0.5 & \;0.5 \\ \;1.5 & \;1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 3 — Covariance matrix:* Using $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{3}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$:

$$\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 & 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 \\ \text{sym} & 2.25+0.25+0.25+2.25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 & 5.0 \\ 5.0 & 5.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 5.0 & 5.0 \\ 5.0 & 5.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.667 & 1.667 \\ 1.667 & 1.667 \end{bmatrix}$$

*Step 4 — Eigendecomposition:* $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda\mathbf{I}) = (1.667 - \lambda)^2 - 1.667^2 = 0 \Rightarrow (1.667-\lambda)^2 = 2.778 \Rightarrow 1.667 - \lambda = \pm 1.667$

So $\lambda_1 = 0$, $\lambda_2 = 3.333$ (i.e., $10/3$).

*Step 5 — Eigenvectors:*
For $\lambda_1 = 3.333$: $(1.667 - 3.333)v_1 + 1.667 v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_1 = v_2 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, 1)^{\top}$.
For $\lambda_2 = 0$: $1.667 v_1 + 1.667 v_2 = 0 \Rightarrow v_2 = -v_1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1, -1)^{\top}$.

*Step 6 — Explained variance:* Total $= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 3.333$. $\eta_1 = 3.333/3.333 = 100\%$.

*Step 7 — Projection:* $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{v}_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} -3 \\ -1 \\ \;1 \\ \;3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.121 \\ -0.707 \\ \;0.707 \\ \;2.121 \end{bmatrix}$.

Since PC1 captures **100%** of the variance, the data is perfectly collinear and the 1-D representation is lossless. ✓ (7 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – 14-Mark Question B (Internal Choice)]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the **Scree plot** and **Kaiser criterion** as methods for choosing the optimal number of principal components $k$. Also, derive the formula for **cumulative explained variance** and explain its role in PCA. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A standardized dataset has 4 features and yields the following eigenvalues after PCA: $\lambda_1 = 2.5$, $\lambda_2 = 1.0$, $\lambda_3 = 0.4$, $\lambda_4 = 0.1$. Using the **Kaiser criterion** and the **95% cumulative variance** rule, determine the optimal number of principal components. Justify your answer with calculations. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Choosing $k$ (7 marks)**

The **Scree plot** is a line-plot of eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ against component index $i$ (in descending order). The "elbow" — the point where the curve transitions from steep descent to a flat tail — marks the optimal $k$. Components to the *left* of the elbow are retained; those to the *right* are discarded (treated as noise).

The **Kaiser criterion** (Kaiser, 1960) retains only those principal components whose eigenvalue exceeds **1**. This rule is meaningful only when data has been standardized — since each standardized feature has variance 1, eigenvalues less than 1 capture less variance than any single original feature, and are deemed uninformative.

The **cumulative explained variance** is defined as:

$$\Sigma_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \eta_i = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}$$

It quantifies the **fraction of total information retained** when using $k$ components. A common engineering threshold is $\Sigma_k \geq 0.95$ (i.e., 95% variance preserved). This rule is preferred when downstream tasks (e.g., regression) are sensitive to information loss, while the Kaiser rule is preferred for *interpretability* and *parsimony*. ✓ (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Determining $k$ (7 marks)**

Given eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 2.5, \lambda_2 = 1.0, \lambda_3 = 0.4, \lambda_4 = 0.1$. Total variance $= 2.5 + 1.0 + 0.4 + 0.1 = 4.0$.

| Component $i$ | $\lambda_i$ | $\eta_i$ | Cumulative $\Sigma_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.5 | 0.625 (62.5%) | 62.5% |
| 2 | 1.0 | 0.250 (25.0%) | 87.5% |
| 3 | 0.4 | 0.100 (10.0%) | 97.5% ✓ |
| 4 | 0.1 | 0.025 (2.5%) | 100.0% |

**By Kaiser criterion:** Retain $\lambda_i > 1$ $\Rightarrow$ $\lambda_1 = 2.5$ and $\lambda_2 = 1.0$ are *borderline*. Strictly $> 1$ keeps only PC1; allowing $\geq 1$ keeps PC1 and PC2. So Kaiser gives $k = 1$ (strict) or $k = 2$ (inclusive).

**By 95% cumulative variance rule:** $\Sigma_2 = 87.5\% < 95\%$, but $\Sigma_3 = 97.5\% \geq 95\%$. So $k = 3$.

**Optimal recommendation:** Since the 95% rule is the stricter, more information-preserving criterion, the **optimal choice is $k = 3$**, retaining 97.5% of the variance. ✓ (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mean-center** before computing the covariance matrix → lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Using $\frac{1}{n}$ instead of $\frac{1}{n-1}$** (Bessel's correction) — state which one you are using.
> 3. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** to unit length — leads to incorrect projections.
> 4. **Confusing "principal components" with "principal axes"** — PCs are the *projected coordinates*, not the eigenvectors themselves.
> 5. **Skipping the Lagrange multiplier step** in the derivation — full marks for the derivation require showing the constrained optimization explicitly.
> 6. **Citing Kaiser criterion without standardization** — Kaiser rule is invalid for un-standardized data.
> 7. **Arithmetic errors in $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda\mathbf{I})$** — practice small $2 \times 2$ and $3 \times 3$ determinant expansions before the exam.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — PCA (Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

* [ ] PCA is an **unsupervised, linear, deterministic** dimensionality-reduction method.
* [ ] It maximizes **projected variance** under the constraint that the projection direction is a **unit vector**.
* [ ] **Always mean-center** before computing the covariance matrix; **standardize** when units differ.
* [ ] Covariance matrix $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}_c^{\top}\mathbf{X}_c$ is **symmetric, positive semi-definite**, with size $p \times p$.
* [ ] Principal components = **eigenvectors** of $\mathbf{C}$; **eigenvalues** = variance along each PC.
* [ ] **Total variance = trace($\mathbf{C}$) = sum of all eigenvalues** — an invariant under orthogonal transformations.
* [ ] **Explained variance ratio:** $\eta_i = \lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$.
* [ ] **Reconstruction error** = sum of discarded eigenvalues = $E_k = \sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \lambda_i$.
* [ ] Three rules for choosing $k$: **Scree elbow**, **Kaiser ($\lambda_i > 1$)**, and **95% cumulative variance**.
* [ ] PCA assumes **linearity**, **large variance = importance**, and **orthogonal PCs** — invalid for non-linear manifolds.
* [ ] Alternative methods for non-linear data: **Kernel PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders**.
* [ ] Real-world applications: *Eigenfaces*, *gene-expression analysis*, *LSA in NLP*, *anomaly detection*, *image compression*.
* [ ] Equivalence: PCA on $\mathbf{X}_c$ ≈ SVD on $\mathbf{X}_c$ (with $\sigma_i^2 = (n-1)\lambda_i$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K-Means Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** K-Means Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — K-Means Clustering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**K-Means Clustering** is an unsupervised, iterative, centroid-based partitioning algorithm that divides a dataset of $n$ observations into $k$ pre-defined, non-overlapping subgroups (clusters), where each observation belongs to the cluster with the **nearest centroid (mean)**. The algorithm's objective is to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the **inertia** or distortion function.

Mathematically, it solves the following optimization problem:

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} \lVert x_i^{(j)} - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

where $x_i^{(j)}$ is the $i^{th}$ data point assigned to cluster $j$, $\mu_j$ is the centroid of cluster $j$, and $n_j$ is the number of points in cluster $j$. The goal is to find the partition that **minimizes** $J$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** K-Means is a *prototype-based* (centroid-based), *flat* (non-hierarchical) clustering technique. It belongs to the family of **unsupervised learning** algorithms because it operates on unlabeled data and discovers intrinsic group structure without external supervision.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Organization

Imagine you are a librarian given 1,000 unsorted books and told to arrange them into **3 sections** (let's say Fiction, Science, History) without any prior labels. You would:

1. **Pick 3 random books** as initial reference points (one per section).
2. **Place every other book** next to the reference book whose topic it resembles most.
3. **Recompute the "ideal" book** for each section by averaging the topics of all books currently placed there.
4. **Re-sort** all books based on the new ideal books.
5. **Repeat** until no book changes sections.

This is precisely how **K-Means Clustering** operates on numerical data — picking initial centroids, assigning points, recomputing centroids, and iterating until convergence.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

- **Centroid ($\mu_j$):** The arithmetic mean of all data points currently belonging to cluster $j$.
- **Inertia (WCSS):** The sum of squared distances of every point to its cluster centroid. A **lower inertia** indicates tighter, more compact clusters.
- **Distance Metric:** Most commonly **Euclidean distance**; can also use Manhattan or Cosine depending on data type.
- **Convergence:** The algorithm terminates when centroid positions stabilize (do not change between iterations) or when the maximum iteration count is reached.
- **k:** A **user-defined hyperparameter** — the number of clusters. The "K" in K-Means.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **k** is not learned by the algorithm; it must be specified *before* execution. The **Elbow Method** and **Silhouette Score** are standard techniques used to choose an optimal $k$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — The "Why" and "How"

K-Means operates through a **Lloyd's Algorithm** loop, which alternates between two complementary phases until stabilization:

#### **Phase 1 — Assignment Step (Expectation)**
For every data point $x_i$, compute its distance to each existing centroid $\mu_j$ and assign the point to the cluster whose centroid is closest.

$$c_i = \arg\min_{j} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

*Why?* This step minimizes the intra-cluster distance by ensuring each point sits in its locally optimal cluster.

#### **Phase 2 — Update Step (Maximization)**
Recompute each centroid $\mu_j$ as the **mean of all points** currently assigned to cluster $j$.

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{n_j} \sum_{i: c_i = j} x_i$$

*Why?* The arithmetic mean is the point that minimizes the sum of squared distances to all members of the cluster — making it the *true* optimal centroid for that assignment.

#### **Phase 3 — Convergence Check**
Compare the new centroids with the previous iteration's centroids. If the shift is below a tolerance threshold ($\epsilon$) OR the cluster assignments no longer change, **terminate**. Otherwise, return to Phase 1.

*Why?* Repeatedly assigning-then-updating monotonically decreases the objective $J$, guaranteeing convergence to a (local) minimum.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x_i, \mu_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_{id} - \mu_{jd})^2}$ | Measures similarity between a point and a centroid |
| 2 | Objective Function (WCSS / Inertia) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{k}\sum_{i \in C_j} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | Quantifies total cluster compactness; to be **minimized** |
| 3 | Centroid Update Rule | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ | Computes new cluster mean after re-assignment |
| 4 | Cluster Assignment Rule | $c_i = \arg\min_{j \in \{1,\ldots,k\}} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | Assigns each point to its nearest centroid |
| 5 | Convergence Criterion | $\lVert \mu_j^{(t+1)} - \mu_j^{(t)} \rVert < \epsilon$ | Stops the iteration loop |
| 6 | Elbow Method (heuristic) | Plot $J$ vs $k$; choose $k$ at the "elbow" | Selects the optimal number of clusters |
| 7 | Silhouette Coefficient | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Validates clustering quality ($s \in [-1, 1]$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX NOTE:** In LaTeX rendered inside markdown tables, use `\lVert` and `\Vert` for norm symbols and `\vert C_j \vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking the table pipe (`|`) parser.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Customer Segmentation:** Marketing teams cluster buyers by purchasing behavior to design targeted campaigns.
- **Image Compression (Vector Quantization):** Reducing color palette to $k$ representative colors.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Points far from any centroid are flagged as outliers (e.g., network intrusion, manufacturing defects).
- **Document Classification:** Grouping news articles by topic similarity (NLP preprocessing).
- **Sensor Data Analysis:** IoT and smart-city systems cluster sensor readings to detect environmental patterns.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression profiling groups genes with similar expression patterns.

### 2.4 Strengths, Limitations & Variants

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Strengths** | Simple, scalable ($O(nki)$), easily interpretable, fast convergence |
| **Limitations** | Must pre-specify $k$; sensitive to initial centroid placement; assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters; sensitive to outliers |
| **Variants** | **K-Means++** (smart initialization), **Mini-Batch K-Means** (scalable to big data), **Fuzzy C-Means** (soft assignments) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Centroid Update Rule

We want to find the point $\mu_j$ that **minimizes** the sum of squared distances from itself to all points in cluster $C_j$:

$$J_j = \sum_{i \in C_j} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

Take the partial derivative of $J_j$ with respect to $\mu_j$ and set it to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$\frac{\partial J_j}{\partial \mu_j} = -2 \sum_{i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j) = 0$$

Solving for $\mu_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j) &= 0 \\
\sum_{i \in C_j} x_i - \sum_{i \in C_j} \mu_j &= 0 \\
\sum_{i \in C_j} x_i - n_j \mu_j &= 0 \\
\mu_j &= \frac{1}{n_j} \sum_{i \in C_j} x_i
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal centroid of a cluster is the **arithmetic mean** of its members. This is the formal proof of *why* K-Means recomputes centroids as averages.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Given the following 2-D dataset with **$k = 2$** and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 8)$. Execute **one complete iteration** of K-Means and compute the updated centroids.

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 2 | 10 |
| $B$ | 2 | 5 |
| $C$ | 8 | 4 |
| $D$ | 5 | 8 |
| $E$ | 7 | 5 |
| $F$ | 6 | 4 |

#### **Step 1: Compute Euclidean distance from each point to each centroid**

The Euclidean distance formula is:

$$d(P, \mu) = \sqrt{(x_1 - \mu_1)^2 + (x_2 - \mu_2)^2}$$

For **Point A = (2, 10)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(A, \mu_1) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2} = \sqrt{0+0} = 0.00 \\
d(A, \mu_2) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61
\end{aligned}$$

Since $0.00 < 3.61$, **Point A → Cluster 1**.

For **Point B = (2, 5)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(B, \mu_1) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (5-10)^2} = \sqrt{0+25} = 5.00 \\
d(B, \mu_2) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24
\end{aligned}$$

Since $4.24 < 5.00$, **Point B → Cluster 2**.

For **Point C = (8, 4)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(C, \mu_1) &= \sqrt{(8-2)^2 + (4-10)^2} = \sqrt{36+36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49 \\
d(C, \mu_2) &= \sqrt{(8-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00
\end{aligned}$$

Since $5.00 < 8.49$, **Point C → Cluster 2**.

For **Point D = (5, 8)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(D, \mu_1) &= \sqrt{(5-2)^2 + (8-10)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61 \\
d(D, \mu_2) &= \sqrt{(5-5)^2 + (8-8)^2} = \sqrt{0+0} = 0.00
\end{aligned}$$

Since $0.00 < 3.61$, **Point D → Cluster 1**.

For **Point E = (7, 5)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(E, \mu_1) &= \sqrt{(7-2)^2 + (5-10)^2} = \sqrt{25+25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07 \\
d(E, \mu_2) &= \sqrt{(7-5)^2 + (5-8)^2} = \sqrt{4+9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61
\end{aligned}$$

Since $3.61 < 7.07$, **Point E → Cluster 2**.

For **Point F = (6, 4)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
d(F, \mu_1) &= \sqrt{(6-2)^2 + (4-10)^2} = \sqrt{16+36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21 \\
d(F, \mu_2) &= \sqrt{(6-5)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{1+16} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12
\end{aligned}$$

Since $4.12 < 7.21$, **Point F → Cluster 2**.

#### **Step 2: Tabulate cluster assignments after Iteration 1**

| Point | Coordinates | $d(\mu_1)$ | $d(\mu_2)$ | Assigned Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | (2, 10) | 0.00 | 3.61 | **C1** |
| B | (2, 5) | 5.00 | 4.24 | **C2** |
| C | (8, 4) | 8.49 | 5.00 | **C2** |
| D | (5, 8) | 3.61 | 0.00 | **C1** |
| E | (7, 5) | 7.07 | 3.61 | **C2** |
| F | (6, 4) | 7.21 | 4.12 | **C2** |

**Cluster 1 = {A, D}** and **Cluster 2 = {B, C, E, F}**.

#### **Step 3: Recompute (update) the centroids**

**New centroid of Cluster 1** (mean of A and D):

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{\text{new}} &= \left( \frac{2+5}{2}, \frac{10+8}{2} \right) \\
&= \left( \frac{7}{2}, \frac{18}{2} \right) \\
&= (3.5, 9.0)
\end{aligned}$$

**New centroid of Cluster 2** (mean of B, C, E, F):

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_2^{\text{new}} &= \left( \frac{2+8+7+6}{4}, \frac{5+4+5+4}{4} \right) \\
&= \left( \frac{23}{4}, \frac{18}{4} \right) \\
&= (5.75, 4.5)
\end{aligned}$$

#### **Step 4: Compute the new WCSS (Inertia)**

For **Cluster 1** with centroid $(3.5, 9.0)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{WCSS}_1 &= (2-3.5)^2 + (10-9.0)^2 + (5-3.5)^2 + (8-9.0)^2 \\
&= 2.25 + 1.00 + 2.25 + 1.00 \\
&= 6.50
\end{aligned}$$

For **Cluster 2** with centroid $(5.75, 4.5)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{WCSS}_2 &= (2-5.75)^2 + (5-4.5)^2 + (8-5.75)^2 + (4-4.5)^2 \\
&\quad + (7-5.75)^2 + (5-4.5)^2 + (6-5.75)^2 + (4-4.5)^2 \\
&= 14.0625 + 0.25 + 5.0625 + 0.25 + 1.5625 + 0.25 + 0.0625 + 0.25 \\
&= 21.75
\end{aligned}$$

**Total WCSS after Iteration 1:**

$$J^{(1)} = 6.50 + 21.75 = 28.25$$

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm would now check if $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ have changed significantly from the previous iteration. Since both centroids shifted, the algorithm proceeds to **Iteration 2**, repeating Steps 1–3 with the new centroids until convergence.

### 3.3 Convergence Guarantee Proof (Intuitive Sketch)

Let $J^{(t)}$ be the objective value at iteration $t$. Lloyd's algorithm ensures:

$$J^{(t+1)} \leq J^{(t)}$$

This is because (a) the assignment step keeps each point in its closest centroid (no worse than before), and (b) the update step computes the globally optimal centroid for the new assignment. Since $J$ is bounded below by **0**, the algorithm **must converge** in finite steps. However, convergence is to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global optimum — hence the importance of smart initialization (K-Means++).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 K-Means Algorithm Flowchart (Lloyd's Iteration Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start K-Means Algorithm]) --> inputA[/Input: Dataset X and k value/]
    inputA --> initA[Initialize k centroids using K-Means Plus Plus]
    initA --> assignA[Assignment Step: Compute Euclidean distance from each point to every centroid]
    assignA --> clusterA[Assign each point to cluster with nearest centroid]
    clusterA --> updateA[Update Step: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points]
    updateA --> shiftA[Compute centroid shift: norm of new minus old centroid]
    shiftA --> checkA{Is shift less than epsilon OR max iterations reached?}
    checkA -->|No| assignA
    checkA -->|Yes| outputA[/Output: Final cluster labels and centroids/]
    outputA --> stopA([End])
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of K-Means

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dataStore[Dataset X: n samples with D features] --> preProc[Preprocessing: Feature scaling and normalization]
    preProc --> kSelect[Module: K Selection via Elbow or Silhouette]
    kSelect --> initMod[Initialization Module: K-Means Plus Plus seeding]
    initMod --> assignMod[Assignment Engine: Nearest centroid labeling]
    assignMod --> updateMod[Centroid Update Engine: Mean recomputation]
    updateMod --> convMod[Convergence Monitor: Shift and inertia tracking]
    convMod --> dec{Convergence reached?}
    dec -->|No| assignMod
    dec -->|Yes| resultOut[Output: Cluster labels, centroids, WCSS]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Cluster Formation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    raw[Raw Unlabeled Data Points] --> step1[Step 1: Random Centroid Selection]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Distance Based Assignment]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Centroid Recomputation]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Reassignment]
    step4 --> convCheck{Converged?}
    convCheck -->|No| step2
    convCheck -->|Yes| finalC[Final Compact Clusters Formed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked to "explain with a diagram" in the exam, always annotate the **input → process → output** flow and clearly mark the **two alternating phases** (Assignment and Update). Examiners award partial marks for well-labeled flow diagrams even when the math is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define K-Means clustering. List any two limitations of the algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** K-Means is an unsupervised, centroid-based partitioning algorithm that divides $n$ unlabeled data points into $k$ pre-defined clusters by minimizing the Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS). Each point is assigned to the cluster with the nearest centroid, and centroids are iteratively updated as cluster means.
- **Limitations (1 mark — list any two):**
  1. The number of clusters $k$ must be specified in advance.
  2. Sensitive to initial centroid placement (may converge to local minima).
  3. Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters; performs poorly on elongated or irregular shapes.
  4. Highly sensitive to outliers since they distort the mean.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is the role of the Elbow Method in K-Means clustering?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The **Elbow Method** is a heuristic used to determine the **optimal number of clusters $k$** before running K-Means.
- The procedure involves running K-Means for a range of $k$ values (e.g., $k = 1$ to $10$) and plotting the resulting **WCSS (inertia)** against $k$.
- The plot typically decreases sharply at first and then levels off, forming an "elbow" shape. The $k$ value at the **bend (elbow point)** is chosen as optimal — it represents the point where adding more clusters yields diminishing returns in reducing WCSS.
- (1 mark for procedure, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark for stating the chosen $k$ point.)

---

### **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)**

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** with sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the working of the K-Means clustering algorithm with a suitable flowchart. Discuss the role of Euclidean distance in cluster assignment. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Working (5 marks):**

1. **Input:** Choose the number of clusters $k$ and initialize $k$ centroids randomly (or via K-Means++).
2. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $x_i$, compute its Euclidean distance to every centroid $\mu_j$ and assign it to the cluster with the minimum distance:

$$c_i = \arg\min_{j} \sqrt{\sum_{d=1}^{D}(x_{id} - \mu_{jd})^2}$$

3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster:

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{n_j} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$

4. **Convergence Check:** Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids stabilize (movement $<\epsilon$) or the iteration limit is reached.
5. **Output:** Final cluster labels and centroid positions.

**Role of Euclidean Distance (2 marks):**
- Euclidean distance serves as the **similarity measure** in K-Means.
- It determines cluster membership by quantifying "closeness" in feature space.
- A smaller Euclidean distance implies greater similarity, so each point is grouped with its nearest centroid.
- (Limitation: it treats all features equally and assumes isotropic clusters, making it unsuitable for high-dimensional or correlated data.)

**(b)** Consider the following 2-D data points: $A(1,1)$, $B(1,2)$, $C(2,1)$, $D(4,3)$, $E(5,4)$, $F(6,5)$. Take $k=2$ with initial centroids $\mu_1 = (1,1)$ and $\mu_2 = (6,5)$. Perform **two complete iterations** of K-Means and compute the final WCSS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Iteration 1 — Assignment Step:**
Compute Euclidean distances:

| Point | $d$ to $\mu_1$ | $d$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(1,1) | 0.00 | 6.40 | **C1** |
| B(1,2) | 1.00 | 6.40 | **C1** |
| C(2,1) | 1.00 | 6.40 | **C1** |
| D(4,3) | 3.61 | 2.83 | **C2** |
| E(5,4) | 5.00 | 1.41 | **C2** |
| F(6,5) | 6.40 | 0.00 | **C2** |

**C1 = {A, B, C}**, **C2 = {D, E, F}**.

**Iteration 1 — Update Step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{\text{new}} &= \left( \frac{1+1+2}{3}, \frac{1+2+1}{3} \right) = \left( \frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3} \right) \approx (1.33, 1.33) \\
\mu_2^{\text{new}} &= \left( \frac{4+5+6}{3}, \frac{3+4+5}{3} \right) = (5.0, 4.0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Iteration 2 — Assignment Step (using new centroids):**

| Point | $d$ to $\mu_1$ | $d$ to $\mu_2$ | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|
| A(1,1) | 0.47 | 5.00 | **C1** |
| B(1,2) | 0.75 | 4.47 | **C1** |
| C(2,1) | 0.75 | 4.24 | **C1** |
| D(4,3) | 3.00 | 1.41 | **C2** |
| E(5,4) | 4.67 | 0.00 | **C2** |
| F(6,5) | 6.00 | 1.41 | **C2** |

No point changed cluster → **Algorithm has converged** in 2 iterations.

**Final WCSS Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{WCSS}_1 &= (1-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2 + (2-1.33)^2 + (1-1.33)^2 \\
&= 0.11 + 0.11 + 0.11 + 0.45 + 0.11 = 0.89 \\
\text{WCSS}_2 &= (4-5)^2 + (3-4)^2 + (5-5)^2 + (4-4)^2 + (6-5)^2 + (5-4)^2 \\
&= 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 4.00 \\
J^{\text{final}} &= 0.89 + 4.00 = 4.89
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** Clusters C1 = {A, B, C} and C2 = {D, E, F} with centroids $(1.33, 1.33)$ and $(5.0, 4.0)$ and WCSS $\approx$ **4.89**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how the K-Means++ initialization improves over random initialization. Why is initialization important in K-Means? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Importance of Initialization (3 marks):**
- K-Means converges to a **local minimum**, not the global minimum.
- Poor initialization can lead to:
  - **Empty clusters** (centroid with no assigned points).
  - **Suboptimal clustering** (points mis-grouped).
  - **Slow convergence** (more iterations needed).
- Two random initializations can yield drastically different final clusters and WCSS values.

**K-Means++ Algorithm (4 marks):**

1. Choose the first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random from the data points.
2. For each remaining data point $x_i$, compute its squared distance $D(x_i)^2$ to the **nearest already-chosen centroid**.
3. Select the next centroid $\mu_j$ with probability proportional to $D(x_i)^2$ — i.e., points **far from existing centroids** are more likely to be chosen.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 until $k$ centroids are selected.
5. Proceed with standard Lloyd's K-Means.

**Why K-Means++ Works:**
- It ensures initial centroids are **well-separated**, covering the data spread.
- Empirically, K-Means++ produces clusters with WCSS closer to the global optimum.
- Theoretical guarantee: expected WCSS is **$O(\ln k)$** competitive with the optimal solution.

**(b)** Discuss the **Elbow Method** and **Silhouette Coefficient** as techniques for selecting the optimal value of $k$ in K-Means. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Elbow Method (3.5 marks):**
- Run K-Means for a range of $k$ values (typically $k = 1$ to $k = 10$ or higher).
- Compute the **WCSS (inertia)** for each $k$.
- Plot $J$ vs $k$ — the curve decreases monotonically.
- The optimal $k$ is the point where the **rate of decrease sharply changes** (the "elbow").
- **Limitation:** The elbow is sometimes visually ambiguous, especially for $k > 5$.

**Silhouette Coefficient (3.5 marks):**
For each point $i$:
- $a(i)$ = average distance to all other points in the **same cluster** (intra-cluster cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = average distance to all points in the **nearest other cluster** (inter-cluster separation).

The Silhouette score is:

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

- $s(i) \in [-1, 1]$.
- $s(i) \approx 1$ → point is well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$ → point lies on cluster boundary.
- $s(i) \approx -1$ → point may be misassigned.
- The **average silhouette score** across all points is plotted against $k$; the $k$ yielding the **highest average silhouette** is selected as optimal.

**Comparison:**
| Method | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| Elbow | Simple, visual | Ambiguous elbow |
| Silhouette | More rigorous, bounded metric | Computationally expensive |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the convergence check ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** Always state that K-Means *terminates* when centroids stabilize or max iterations are reached.
> 2. **Confusing WCSS formula with assignment rule:** WCSS uses *squared* distances summed over all points; assignment uses the *argmin* of distance.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in centroid recomputation:** Show the sum and division *explicitly* — examiners penalize skipped steps.
> 4. **Not labeling flowchart components:** A flowchart without input/output labels and decision diamonds loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Memorizing but not explaining "why":** KTU 2024 emphasizes *understanding* — always pair a formula with its purpose and a real-world example.
> 6. **Confusing K-Means (flat) with Hierarchical clustering:** K-Means requires pre-specified $k$; hierarchical methods build a dendrogram and do not.
> 7. **Ignoring feature scaling:** Real exam questions often deduct marks for not mentioning that K-Means requires feature standardization (e.g., StandardScaler) since it uses Euclidean distance.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** *(Rapid-Revision Checklist)*

- **K-Means** is an **unsupervised**, **centroid-based**, **flat (non-hierarchical)** clustering algorithm.
- It minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / Inertia):** $J = \sum_j \sum_i \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$.
- **Two-step iteration:** (1) Assign each point to the nearest centroid using **Euclidean distance**, (2) Update each centroid as the **arithmetic mean** of its cluster members.
- **Convergence** is guaranteed but only to a **local minimum** — hence the importance of **K-Means++** initialization.
- **Optimal $k$** selection: **Elbow Method** (visual heuristic) and **Silhouette Coefficient** (rigorous metric).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot k \cdot i \cdot D)$ where $n$ = samples, $k$ = clusters, $i$ = iterations, $D$ = dimensions.
- **Preprocessing is mandatory:** Apply **feature scaling** (Standardization or Min-Max Normalization) before running K-Means since Euclidean distance is feature-scale sensitive.
- **Algorithm family:** Lloyd's Algorithm (the standard iterative K-Means procedure).
- **Key Variants:** K-Means++ (smart seeding), Mini-Batch K-Means (scalable), Fuzzy C-Means (probabilistic membership).
- **Real-world uses:** Customer segmentation, image compression, document clustering, anomaly detection, gene expression analysis.
- **Hard constraints to mention in answers:** Pre-specification of $k$, sensitivity to outliers, assumption of spherical clusters.
- **Examiner's mantra:** Always show the *distance calculation*, the *centroid update*, and the *convergence statement* in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Selection and Regularization: Underfitting, Overfitting, L1 and L2 Regularization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model Selection and Regularization: Underfitting, Overfitting, L1 and L2 Regularization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Model Selection — The Formal Definition

**Model Selection** is the systematic process of identifying the most appropriate machine learning model (including its hyperparameters) from a finite set of candidate models by balancing **goodness-of-fit** against **generalization performance** on unseen data. In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework, model selection directly maps to the Course Outcome (CO) emphasizing the design of robust AI pipelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization Error** (also called expected prediction error) is the model's error on *new, unseen* data drawn from the same distribution. The entire goal of model selection is to minimize this quantity, not the training error.

## 1.2 Underfitting and Overfitting — Formal Definitions

**Underfitting** occurs when a model is **too simple** to capture the underlying structure of the data. It exhibits **high bias** and **low variance**, performing poorly on both training and test sets.

**Overfitting** occurs when a model is **too complex** and memorizes noise/idiosyncrasies in the training data. It exhibits **low bias** and **high variance**, performing extremely well on training data but poorly on unseen test data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Bias** = model makes strong, oversimplified assumptions (underfitting).  
> **High Variance** = model is overly sensitive to fluctuations in training data (overfitting).  
> The irreducible error (Bayes error / noise floor) is a lower bound we cannot eliminate.

## 1.3 Regularization — The Formal Definition

**Regularization** is a class of techniques that introduce a **penalty term** to the model's loss function to discourage excessive complexity. By constraining the magnitude of model parameters, regularization reduces overfitting and improves generalization on unseen data.

The general form of a regularized objective function is:

$$J_{\text{reg}}(w) = J(w) + \lambda \, \Omega(w)$$

where:
- $J(w)$ is the original loss (e.g., Mean Squared Error).
- $\Omega(w)$ is the regularization penalty.
- $\lambda \geq 0$ is the **regularization hyperparameter** controlling the strength of the penalty.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Student Cramming" Story

Imagine three students preparing for an exam:

| Student Type | Behavior | Performance on Exam |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Underfit Student** | Memorizes only a single formula and applies it to every problem, ignoring context | Fails both practice and real exam |
| **Balanced Student** | Understands core concepts and generalizes them to solve novel questions | Performs well on both practice and real exam |
| **Overfit Student** | Memorizes every past-year question word-for-word, including the typos in the question paper | Scores 100% on past papers but fails novel questions |

Regularization is like a wise **teacher** who tells the cramming student: *"Don't try to memorize everything — focus on the most important concepts and ignore the noise."* The penalty term $\lambda$ is the strength of the teacher's advice.

## 1.5 Why This Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the **Bias-Variance Tradeoff** as a 7-mark Part B sub-question, often paired with a numerical example of computing training vs. validation error. Always relate your answer to **generalization**, not memorization.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bias-Variance Decomposition (The Heart of the Topic)

For a regression problem with true function $f(x)$ and prediction $\hat{f}(x)$ at a point $x$, the **expected prediction error** decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{Squared bias}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible noise}}$$

- **Bias²** — error from erroneous model assumptions. Decreases as model complexity increases.
- **Variance** — error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations. Increases as complexity increases.
- **$\sigma^2$** — irreducible noise inherent in the data.

### Step-by-step Intuition
1. As we increase model complexity (e.g., polynomial degree), **bias drops** because the model can fit more patterns.
2. Simultaneously, **variance rises** because the model becomes sensitive to every training point.
3. The total error has a **U-shape**; the sweet spot is the model complexity that minimizes the sum.

## 2.2 L1 Regularization (Lasso — Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator)

The L1 regularized cost function is:

$$J_{\text{L1}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert w_j \vert$$

**Key Property:** L1 regularization produces **sparse models** — it drives many weights to *exactly zero*, effectively performing **automatic feature selection**.

**Geometric Intuition:** The L1 constraint region $\sum \vert w_j \vert \leq t$ is a **diamond** in weight space. The loss contours first touch the diamond at its **corners** (where one or more coordinates are zero).

## 2.3 L2 Regularization (Ridge Regression / Weight Decay)

The L2 regularized cost function is:

$$J_{\text{L2}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}w_j^{\,2}$$

**Key Property:** L2 regularization **shrinks weights** smoothly toward zero but rarely makes them exactly zero. All features contribute, but with smaller magnitudes.

**Geometric Intuition:** The L2 constraint region $\sum w_j^{\,2} \leq t$ is a **circle** in weight space. The loss contours typically touch the circle at points where all weights are non-zero.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **high-yield formula sheet** for this topic. Master these — they are board-exam favorites.

| Concept | Formula | Key Parameter | Units (if applicable) |
|---------|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | $m$ = number of samples | squared units of $y$ |
| Bias² | $\text{Bias}^2 = \left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2$ | None | squared units of $y$ |
| Variance | $\text{Var} = \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$ | None | squared units of $y$ |
| Total Expected Error | $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | None | squared units of $y$ |
| L1 Penalty (Lasso) | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert w_j \vert$ | $\lambda \geq 0$ | unitless weight sum |
| L2 Penalty (Ridge) | $\lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}w_j^{\,2}$ | $\lambda \geq 0$ | unitless weight sum |
| Elastic Net Penalty | $\lambda_1 \sum \vert w_j \vert + \lambda_2 \sum w_j^{\,2}$ | $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \geq 0$ | unitless |
| Ridge Closed-Form | $w = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^\top y$ | $I$ = identity matrix | weight vector |
| Gradient (L2) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum (h_w - y)x_j + 2\lambda w_j$ | None | weight gradient |
| Gradient (L1) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum (h_w - y)x_j + \lambda \, \text{sign}(w_j)$ | $\text{sign}(0) \in [-1, 1]$ | weight gradient |
| Sub-gradient (L1 at 0) | $\frac{\partial \vert w \vert}{\partial w} = \begin{cases} +1 & w > 0 \\ -1 & w < 0 \\ \in[-1,1] & w = 0 \end{cases}$ | None | sub-gradient |
| AIC (Akaike) | $2k - 2\ln(\hat{L})$ | $k$ = #parameters | unitless |
| BIC (Bayesian) | $k \ln(m) - 2\ln(\hat{L})$ | $m$ = #samples | unitless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"L1 is **L**asso, makes weights **L**ittle to zero. L2 is **R**idge, makes weights **R**ound and small."*

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Civil Engineering:** Ridge regression regularizes structural load models to avoid fitting to sensor noise.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Lasso selects a handful of relevant biomarkers from thousands of gene expressions.
- **Computer Vision:** Weight decay (L2) is universally applied in deep CNNs and transformers to prevent overfitting on small image datasets.
- **Natural Language Processing:** L1 + L2 (Elastic Net) is used in spam classifiers with millions of word features.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update for L2 Regularization (Ridge)

We begin with the L2 regularized cost:

$$J_{\text{L2}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}w_j^{\,2}$$

**Step 1 —** Compute the partial derivative with respect to a single weight $w_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{L2}}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + 2\lambda w_j$$

*Logic:* The first term is the standard MSE gradient. The second term is the derivative of $\lambda w_j^2$, which is $2\lambda w_j$.

**Step 2 —** Write the gradient descent update rule:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J_{\text{L2}}}{\partial w_j}$$

**Step 3 —** Substitute the partial derivative:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + 2\lambda w_j\right]$$

**Step 4 —** Distribute and rearrange:

$$w_j := w_j\left(1 - 2\alpha\lambda\right) - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)}$$

*Logic:* The factor $(1 - 2\alpha\lambda)$ shows that on every iteration, the weight is *decayed* by a small fraction before the usual gradient step. This is why L2 regularization is also called **weight decay**.

> [!TIP]
> The decay factor $(1 - 2\alpha\lambda)$ must remain positive to prevent divergence. For numerical stability, choose $\alpha\lambda < 0.5$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Closed-Form Solution for Ridge Regression

**Step 1 —** Express the L2 regularized cost in matrix form (for linear regression $h_w(x) = w^\top x$):

$$J_{\text{L2}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}(Xw - y)^\top(Xw - y) + \lambda w^\top w$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the gradient with respect to $w$:

$$\nabla_w J_{\text{L2}} = \frac{1}{m}X^\top(Xw - y) + 2\lambda w$$

**Step 3 —** Set the gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition):

$$X^\top(Xw - y) + 2m\lambda w = 0$$

**Step 4 —** Expand and collect terms in $w$:

$$X^\top X w - X^\top y + 2m\lambda w = 0$$

**Step 5 —** Factor out $w$:

$$(X^\top X + 2m\lambda I) w = X^\top y$$

**Step 6 —** Solve for $w$ (assuming the matrix is invertible):

$$w = (X^\top X + 2m\lambda I)^{-1} X^\top y$$

*Logic:* The term $2m\lambda I$ added to $X^\top X$ **guarantees invertibility** even when $X^\top X$ is singular (e.g., when features outnumber samples, $n > m$). This is a critical practical advantage of Ridge regression.

## 3.3 Derivation: L1 Gradient and Sparsity

**Step 1 —** The L1 penalty is $\lambda \sum_{j} \vert w_j \vert$. Its derivative (sub-gradient) is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} \vert w_j \vert = \text{sign}(w_j) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } w_j > 0 \\ -1 & \text{if } w_j < 0 \\ \text{any value in } [-1, 1] & \text{if } w_j = 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 —** Gradient descent update for L1:

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + \lambda \, \text{sign}(w_j)\right]$$

**Step 3 —** Demonstrating sparsity (1D case): Suppose $w_j > 0$ and the data-driven gradient is small, say $\frac{1}{m}\sum(\cdots) = \epsilon > 0$. Update becomes:

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j - \alpha(\epsilon + \lambda)$$

If $\alpha(\epsilon + \lambda) \geq w_j$, the weight crosses zero. After that, the sign function may keep it pinned at zero (with a sub-gradient that matches the data term).

*Logic:* This is the **soft-thresholding** behavior of Lasso — small weights are forced exactly to zero, removing irrelevant features.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | Underfitting | Good Fit | Overfitting |
|----------|--------------|----------|-------------|
| **Training Error** | High | Low | Very Low (often ~0) |
| **Validation Error** | High | Low | High |
| **Bias** | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Variance** | Low | Low | High |
| **Model Complexity** | Too simple | Balanced | Too complex |
| **Remedy** | Add features, increase complexity, reduce regularization | None needed | Reduce features, increase regularization, gather more data |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state *"overfitting means high bias."* This is **wrong**. Overfitting means **low bias** but **high variance**. Memorize the inverted-U diagram of the bias-variance tradeoff.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Low Complexity"] --> nodeB["Underfitting"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["High Bias, Low Variance"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["High Training Error"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["High Test Error"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Sweet Spot"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Balanced Bias and Variance"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Low Training Error"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Low Test Error"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["High Complexity"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Overfitting"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Low Bias, High Variance"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Very Low Training Error"]
    nodeL --> nodeN["High Test Error"]
```

## 4.2 L1 vs L2 Regularization Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Loss Function Contours"] --> nodeB["Concentric Ellipses around optimum"]
    nodeC["L1 Constraint: Sum of absolute values of w less than t"] --> nodeD["Diamond shape in weight space"]
    nodeE["L2 Constraint: Sum of squares of w less than t"] --> nodeF["Circle shape in weight space"]
    nodeB --> nodeG{"Which constraint touches the contour first?"}
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -->|L1 diamond| nodeH["Intersection at corner: weight equals zero, sparse solution"]
    nodeG -->|L2 circle| nodeI["Intersection on edge: all weights nonzero, smooth shrinkage"]
```

## 4.3 Regularized Linear Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Raw Data X, y"] --> nodeB["Train / Validation / Test Split"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Initialize weights w and bias b"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Loop: Gradient Descent"}
    nodeD --> nodeE["Compute Predictions h of w x"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute Regularized Loss: MSE plus lambda times penalty"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Compute Gradient and update w"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Converged?"}
    nodeH -->|No| nodeD
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI["Evaluate on Validation Set"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{"Validation Error Acceptable?"}
    nodeJ -->|No, try new lambda| nodeK["Tune Regularization Hyperparameter lambda"]
    nodeK --> nodeC
    nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeL["Final Test Set Evaluation"]
```

## 4.4 Model Selection Workflow (KTU Standard Answer Format)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Step 1: Define candidate models with different complexity"] --> nodeB["Step 2: Choose evaluation metric: MSE, Accuracy, F1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 3: Apply k-fold Cross Validation"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 4: Compute average validation score per model"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 5: Select model with best CV score and lowest variance"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 6: Retrain on full training set"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 7: Report final test performance"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definitions)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define underfitting and overfitting. How do they affect model generalization?**

**Model Answer:**

Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern in data, leading to **high bias** and poor performance on both training and test sets. Overfitting occurs when a model is too complex and memorizes noise in training data, leading to **low bias but high variance** — excellent training performance but poor generalization to unseen data. Both degrade model performance; the goal is to find a complexity level that balances bias and variance (the bias-variance tradeoff). Regularization techniques like L1 and L2 are used to combat overfitting by penalizing large weights.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Write the cost function for Ridge and Lasso regression. State one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**

Ridge (L2) regression cost function:

$$J_{\text{Ridge}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}w_j^{\,2}$$

Lasso (L1) regression cost function:

$$J_{\text{Lasso}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(h_w(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert w_j \vert$$

**Key Difference:** Ridge regression **shrinks** all weights smoothly toward zero but rarely makes any weight exactly zero, while Lasso regression can drive many weights to **exactly zero**, producing sparse models and performing automatic feature selection.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**  
**(a)** Explain the **bias-variance tradeoff** in supervised learning with a neatly labeled diagram. Discuss how it relates to underfitting and overfitting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a regression problem with $m = 100$ training samples, the following errors are observed for polynomial models of different degrees:

| Polynomial Degree | Training MSE | Validation MSE |
|-------------------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 (Linear)        | 240          | 245            |
| 3 (Cubic)         | 80           | 95             |
| 7 (Degree 7)      | 10           | 180            |
| 12 (Degree 12)    | 1            | 320            |

Identify which model is underfitting, optimally fit, and overfitting. Justify your answer using the bias-variance framework. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Bias-Variance Decomposition:**

The expected prediction error at point $x$ for a regression model $\hat{f}(x)$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \left(\text{Bias}[\hat{f}(x)]\right)^2 + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2$$

**Definitions:**
- **Bias²** = $\left(\mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2$ — measures how far the *average* model prediction is from the true function.
- **Variance** = $\mathbb{E}_D\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}_D[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$ — measures how much $\hat{f}$ fluctuates across different training sets $D$.
- **$\sigma^2$** = irreducible noise.

**Relationship with Underfitting/Overfitting:**

| Condition | Bias | Variance | Symptom |
|-----------|------|----------|---------|
| Underfitting | High | Low | Both train and test error high |
| Good Fit | Moderate | Moderate | Both errors low and close |
| Overfitting | Low | High | Train error very low, test error high |

As model complexity increases:
- **Bias monotonically decreases** (more flexible models fit data better on average).
- **Variance monotonically increases** (more flexible models are more sensitive to training data).
- The sum has a **U-shape** with a minimum at the optimal complexity.

**Diagram Description (text-based for KTU answer sheet):**

```
Test Error
    |        Variance
    |       /    \
    |      /      \
    |     /  Total \
    |    /   Error  \
    |   /     |      \
    |  /      | Optimum\
    | / Bias² |        \
    |/________|_________\____
              Model Complexity →
        Underfit  Optimum  Overfit
```

**Conclusion:** To achieve the best generalization, we must choose a model complexity that minimizes the sum of bias² and variance. Regularization, cross-validation, and gathering more training data are the standard remedies.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 —** Analyze each model:

| Degree | Training MSE | Validation MSE | Gap (Val − Train) | Diagnosis |
|--------|--------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | 240 | 245 | 5 | **Underfitting** — both errors high, small gap |
| 3 | 80 | 95 | 15 | **Optimal Fit** — low errors, small gap |
| 7 | 10 | 180 | 170 | **Overfitting** — training low, validation high |
| 12 | 1 | 320 | 319 | **Severe Overfitting** — training ≈ 0, validation huge |

**Step 2 —** Bias-Variance Interpretation:
- **Degree 1:** High bias (model cannot capture curvature), low variance → underfitting.
- **Degree 3:** Balanced bias and variance → optimal generalization.
- **Degree 7:** Low bias, high variance (sensitive to noise) → overfitting.
- **Degree 12:** Near-zero bias (fits every point), extreme variance → severe overfitting.

**Step 3 —** Recommendation: Select the **degree 3 polynomial**, as it minimizes the validation MSE (95) and exhibits the smallest generalization gap. Alternatively, keep degree 7 or 12 and apply L2 regularization to reduce variance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**  
**(a)** Derive the gradient descent update rule for **L2 regularized linear regression**. Show explicitly how weight decay arises from the regularization term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear regression model has cost function $J(w) = 100$ at iteration 0. After one L2-regularized gradient descent update with learning rate $\alpha = 0.01$ and $\lambda = 1$, the cost becomes $J = 85$. For the next iteration, you switch to **L1 regularization** with the same $\lambda$. State the new gradient update rule and explain **why L1 can produce sparse models while L2 cannot.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 —** Define the L2 regularized cost function for linear regression:

$$J_{\text{L2}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}w_j^{\,2}$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the partial derivative with respect to a single weight $w_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{L2}}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + 2\lambda w_j$$

*Explanation:* The first term is the standard MSE gradient. The second term comes from $\frac{\partial}{\partial w_j}(\lambda w_j^2) = 2\lambda w_j$.

**Step 3 —** Write the gradient descent update:

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J_{\text{L2}}}{\partial w_j}$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute and expand:

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j - \alpha \left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + 2\lambda w_j\right]$$

**Step 5 —** Distribute $\alpha$:

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} - 2\alpha\lambda w_j$$

**Step 6 —** Factor out $w_j$:

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j \underbrace{(1 - 2\alpha\lambda)}_{\text{weight decay factor}} - \alpha \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 7 —** Interpret weight decay:
- The factor $(1 - 2\alpha\lambda) < 1$ multiplies the current weight *before* the standard gradient step.
- Geometrically, every iteration **shrinks** the weight by a small fraction, hence the name **weight decay**.
- Practical constraint: $2\alpha\lambda < 1$ (i.e., $\alpha\lambda < 0.5$) to ensure the weight decays rather than explodes.

**Conclusion:** L2 regularization is mathematically equivalent to multiplying each weight by a decay constant on every iteration, in addition to the standard gradient step. This keeps weights small and bounded, which reduces variance and combats overfitting.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 —** State the L1 regularized cost function:

$$J_{\text{L1}}(w) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{n}\vert w_j \vert$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the sub-gradient (note: $\vert \cdot \vert$ is non-differentiable at 0):

$$\frac{\partial J_{\text{L1}}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + \lambda \, \text{sign}(w_j)$$

where:

$$\text{sign}(w_j) = \begin{cases} +1, & w_j > 0 \\ -1, & w_j < 0 \\ \in[-1, 1], & w_j = 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 —** Write the gradient descent update:

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j - \alpha \left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(w^\top x^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right) x_j^{(i)} + \lambda \, \text{sign}(w_j)\right]$$

**Step 4 —** Explain the sparsity mechanism:
- The L1 penalty is **constant in magnitude** (always $\pm \lambda$, regardless of $|w_j|$).
- The L2 penalty is **proportional to $|w_j|$** (small weights receive a small penalty).
- When $w_j$ is small and the data-driven gradient is also small, L2's penalty $\lambda w_j$ is too weak to push $w_j$ to zero, but L1's penalty $\lambda \, \text{sign}(w_j)$ is **strong enough** to force it across zero.
- After crossing zero, the sub-gradient $\in [-1, 1]$ can match the data term exactly, **pinning $w_j$ at zero** for all future iterations.

**Step 5 —** Geometric Reinforcement:
- L2 constraint region: **circle** — optimum is on the smooth edge → all $w_j$ nonzero.
- L1 constraint region: **diamond** — optimum is at the **corner** → some $w_j = 0$.

**Conclusion:** L1 regularization is preferred when interpretability and feature selection are required (e.g., high-dimensional datasets like genomics or text). L2 is preferred when all features are believed to be informative and we simply want to reduce variance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse bias and variance direction.** Underfitting = *high bias, low variance*. Overfitting = *low bias, high variance*. A wrong direction costs full marks.
> 2. **Always write the full regularization equation** with both MSE and the penalty term, not just the penalty. Examiners specifically look for the complete cost.
> 3. **For the sub-gradient of L1 at $w = 0$**, write the full sub-gradient set $[-1, 1]$ — saying "undefined" is technically incorrect and loses 1 mark.
> 4. **State units and assumptions** (e.g., "$\lambda \geq 0$", "$\alpha$ is the learning rate") even if not asked; this signals strong understanding to the examiner.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as a **rapid revision checklist** the night before the KTU exam.

- **Model Selection Goal:** Minimize expected prediction error on unseen data (generalization), not training error.
- **Underfitting:** Model too simple → high training error, high test error, high bias, low variance.
- **Overfitting:** Model too complex → low training error, high test error, low bias, high variance.
- **Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** Total error = Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$; as complexity ↑, bias ↓ but variance ↑.
- **Bias-Variance Decomposition Formula:** $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- **L1 (Lasso) Penalty:** $\lambda \sum \vert w_j \vert$ — promotes **sparsity** (feature selection).
- **L2 (Ridge) Penalty:** $\lambda \sum w_j^2$ — promotes **smooth shrinkage**.
- **L2 Gradient Descent Update:** $w_j := w_j(1 - 2\alpha\lambda) - \alpha \cdot \text{(data gradient)}$ — **weight decay** factor.
- **L1 Gradient Descent Update:** $w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \text{sign}(w_j) \lambda - \alpha \cdot \text{(data gradient)}$ — **soft thresholding**.
- **Ridge Closed-Form Solution:** $w = (X^\top X + 2m\lambda I)^{-1} X^\top y$ — always invertible.
- **L1 vs L2 Geometry:** L1 diamond (sparse corners) vs L2 circle (smooth edges).
- **Higher $\lambda$** → more regularization → simpler model (risk of underfitting).
- **Lower $\lambda$** → less regularization → more complex model (risk of overfitting).
- **Cross-Validation:** Use k-fold CV to tune $\lambda$ and select the best model.
- **Elastic Net:** Combines L1 and L2; useful when features are correlated.
- **Irreducible Error ($\sigma^2$):** Lower bound on prediction error; cannot be removed by any model.
- **Sub-gradient at 0 for L1:** Belongs to $[-1, 1]$, not undefined.
- **Unit of regularization hyperparameter $\lambda$:** Dimensionless; controls penalty strength.
- **Engineering Applications:** Ridge in structural/health-monitoring models; Lasso in genomics/text feature selection; Elastic Net in spam filtering.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Model Selection and Regularization**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation Measures: Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination, Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Evaluation Measures: Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination, Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Why Do We Need Evaluation Measures? After a Machine Learning model is trained, we must **quantify how good its predictions are**. Evaluation measures (also called **performance metrics** or **scoring functions**) are mathematical instruments that compare predicted values against the ground-truth values from a held-out test set. They serve three primary purposes: 1. **Model Selection** — comparing competing algorithms on the same dataset. 2. **Hyper-parameter Tuning** — selecting optimal learning rates, depths, regularisation strengths, etc. 3. **Deployment Readiness** — certifying that a model satisfies a business/engineering accuracy threshold. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Evaluation measures are split into two families based on the **target variable type**: > - **Regression metrics** → MSE, MAE, RMSE, R² (continuous target) > - **Classification metrics** → Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure (categorical target) > > Mixing them up is one of the top reasons students lose marks in Module 2. --- ## 1.2 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of Classification Metrics Before defining Precision/Recall/Accuracy/F-Measure, we must understand the **Confusion Matrix**, a 2×2 table that records the *agreement* and *disagreement* between the predicted class and the actual class for a binary classifier. | Predicted → / Actual ↓ | **Predicted Positive (P̂)** | **Predicted Negative (N̂)** | |---|---|---| | **Actual Positive (P)** | **TP** — True Positive | **FN** — False Negative (Type II Error) | | **Actual Negative (N)** | **FP** — False Positive (Type I Error) | **TN** — True Negative | Where: - **TP** = model correctly predicted the positive class. - **TN** = model correctly predicted the negative class. - **FP** = model predicted positive, but it was actually negative (false alarm). - **FN** = model predicted negative, but it was actually positive (missed detection). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Smoke Detector:** Imagine a smoke detector (the classifier) and a house fire (the positive class). **TP** = alarm rings AND there is a fire (correct). **FP** = alarm rings but no fire (false alarm — burnt toast). **FN** = no alarm but there IS a fire (disaster). **TN** = no alarm and no fire (peaceful day). Every classification metric is just a different way of summarising these four counts. --- ## 1.3 Regression Metrics — Formal Definitions Let $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ be the test set, where $y_i$ is the true value and $\hat{y}_i$ is the model's prediction. Let $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ be the mean of actual values. ### Mean Squared Error (MSE) The **arithmetic mean of the squared differences** between predicted and actual values. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Penalty Box:** Squaring penalises large errors *much more* than small ones. If a model misses by 2, the penalty is 4; if it misses by 10, the penalty is 100. MSE is therefore strict — it is most suitable when **large errors are particularly undesirable** (e.g., predicting structural stress in a beam). ### Mean Absolute Error (MAE) The **arithmetic mean of the absolute differences** between predicted and actual values. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Honest Average:** MAE treats every unit of error equally. A 5-unit error contributes 5, regardless of whether it occurred alongside a 100-unit error. MAE is **robust to outliers** and is expressed in the *same units* as the target variable. ### Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) The **square root of the MSE**. Expressed in the *same units* as the target variable. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — Bringing the Penalty Back to Reality:** MSE is in *squared* units (e.g., if you predict temperature in °C, MSE is in °C² — which is meaningless to humans). Taking the square root converts it back to °C, so RMSE is interpretable while retaining the heavy-penalty property of squaring. ### R² (Coefficient of Determination) The **proportion of variance in the dependent variable that is explained by the model**. It compares the model's performance against a naive baseline that simply predicts $\bar{y}$ for every sample. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Explainer's Score:** Imagine your friend claims to "explain" exam scores using a regression model. R² = 0.85 means the model explains 85% of the variability in scores; the remaining 15% is unexplained noise. R² = 0 means the model is no better than always guessing the mean. R² < 0 means the model is *worse* than that naive baseline. --- ## 1.4 Classification Metrics — Formal Definitions Using the TP, FP, TN, FN notation from the confusion matrix above: ### Accuracy The **fraction of all predictions that are correct**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Overall Hit Rate:** Out of 100 predictions, if 92 are correct, Accuracy = 0.92. **Critical caveat:** Accuracy is misleading on *imbalanced* datasets. If 99% of emails are non-spam, a dumb model that always predicts "non-spam" achieves 99% accuracy but catches zero spam. ### Precision (Positive Predictive Value) Out of all samples the model **labelled positive**, the fraction that are **truly positive**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Quality of Positive Calls:** "When the model raises an alarm, how often is it right?" High precision = few false alarms. Important in **spam detection** (you don't want legitimate emails in the spam folder). ### Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate) Out of all samples that are **truly positive**, the fraction the model **successfully retrieved**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Coverage of Positives:** "Of all the actual positives, how many did the model catch?" High recall = few missed detections. Important in **cancer diagnosis** (you cannot afford to miss a tumour). ### F-Measure (F1-Score) The **harmonic mean of Precision and Recall**, balancing both concerns into a single number. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition — The Fair Judge:** Arithmetic mean treats (P=1, R=0) and (P=0.5, R=0.5) similarly. Harmonic mean *punishes* the extreme imbalance — F1 = 0 when either P or R is 0, regardless of the other being 1. It is the *de facto* single-number summary for imbalanced classification. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Residual Concept All regression metrics revolve around the **residual** (or *error term*): $$e_i = \hat{y}_i - y_i$$ A positive residual means the model **over-predicted**; a negative residual means it **under-predicted**. Each metric simply aggregates the vector $(e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n)$ using a different *aggregation operator*: | Metric | Aggregation Operator | Outlier Sensitivity | Same Units as Target? | |---|---|---|---| | MSE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2$ | **Very High** | No (squared) | | MAE | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_i \vert$ | **Low** | Yes | | RMSE | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum e_i^2}$ | **Very High** | Yes | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Regression) | # | Metric | Formula | Range | Ideal Value | Units Match Target? | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **MSE** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - y_i)^2$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **0** | No | | 2 | **MAE** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert \hat{y}_i - y_i \vert$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **0** | Yes | | 3 | **RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | $[0, \infty)$ | **0** | Yes | | 4 | **R²** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | **1** | Dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Frequently Confused Pair:** RMSE is **always ≥ MAE**. Equality holds only if every residual has the same magnitude. Proof: by Jensen's inequality, $\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[X^2]} \geq \mathbb{E}[\vert X \vert]$ with equality iff $X$ is constant. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question in every ESE. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Classification) | # | Metric | Formula | Range | Ideal Value | Depends on | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 5 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | All four cells | | 6 | **Precision (P)** | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | TP, FP | | 7 | **Recall (R)** | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | TP, FN | | 8 | **F-Measure (F1)** | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | TP, FP, FN | | 9 | **Fβ Generalised** | $F_\beta = (1+\beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | **1** | TP, FP, FN, β | ### 2.3.1 The Precision–Recall Trade-off - $\beta = 1 \Rightarrow F_1$ (equal weight to P and R) - $\beta < 1 \Rightarrow$ weights **Precision** more (e.g., $F_{0.5}$) - $\beta > 1 \Rightarrow$ weights **Recall** more (e.g., $F_2$) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** In medical diagnosis (KTU biomedical applications), $\beta = 2$ is common because missing a disease (low recall) is far costlier than a false alarm (low precision). In spam filtering, $\beta = 0.5$ is common because users tolerate a missed spam far more than a wrongly-blocked important email. ## 2.4 Why Harmonic Mean? — Mathematical Justification For two positive numbers $P, R \in (0, 1]$: $$\text{HM}(P, R) = \frac{2PR}{P+R} \leq \text{AM}(P, R) = \frac{P+R}{2}$$ Equality holds iff $P = R$. The harmonic mean: - Approaches 0 when *either* $P$ or $R$ approaches 0. - Is bounded above by the arithmetic mean. - Ensures that a model must perform well on **both** precision and recall to score highly on F1. ## 2.5 Why R² Uses the Mean as a Baseline? The denominator $\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ is the **Total Sum of Squares (SS_tot)**, which is the residual sum of squares of the *worst reasonable model* — one that always predicts $\bar{y}$. If our model cannot beat that, its $R^2$ becomes negative.
