For max pooling with window $f \times f$ and stride $s$: $$ y_{i,j,k} = \max_{0 \le u < f,\; 0 \le v < f} x_{i \cdot s + u,\; j \cdot s + v,\; k} $$ Average pooling replaces the max with the arithmetic mean. **Why Parameter Sharing is Efficient:** - A dense layer treating the input as a flat vector of size $H_{in} \cdot W_{in} \cdot C_{in}$ would require $H_{in} \cdot W_{in} \cdot C_{in} \cdot F$ weights. - A CONV layer with the same effective output requires only $f \cdot f \cdot C_{in} \cdot F$ weights. - The ratio is $\frac{H_{in} \cdot W_{in}}{f^{2}}$, which is **thousands** for typical image sizes. - Furthermore, the same filter is *shared* across all spatial locations, which encodes the prior that **useful features (e.g., edges) can occur anywhere in the image**. *(2 marks convolution math, 2 marks pooling math, 1 mark parameter sharing derivation, 2 marks intuition.)* **(b) Five Real-World Applications of CNNs (7 marks):** | # | Application | Input Data | Typical CNN Architecture | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Medical Imaging (Tumor Detection)** | 2D MRI / CT slices | U-Net, ResNet-50 (transfer-learned) | | 2 | **Autonomous Driving** | Dashcam video frames | YOLO, Faster R-CNN (object detection) | | 3 | **Facial Recognition** | Face images | FaceNet, DeepFace, VGG-Face | | 4 | **Satellite Image Analysis** | Multispectral satellite tiles | ResNet, Inception-v3 | | 5 | **Handwritten Digit Recognition** | MNIST $28 \times 28$ grayscale | LeNet-5, simple 2-layer CNN | | 6 (bonus) | **Video Action Recognition** | Sequence of frames | I3D, C3D, Two-Stream CNN | | 7 (bonus) | **Style Transfer / Art** | Natural images | VGG-19 (frozen feature extractor) | *(5 applications × 1.4 marks = 7 marks; each requires: name of application, input type, and architecture name.)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (CNNs):** - [ ] A CNN is built for **grid-structured data** (images) and uses the **convolution operation** as its core building block. - [ ] Three pillars of CNN design: **Local Receptive Fields, Shared Weights, Pooling**. - [ ] A **CONV layer** is parameterized by 4-D weight tensor of shape $f \times f \times C_{in} \times F$. - [ ] **Activation function** (ReLU) is applied **after** every CONV layer — never forget this in diagrams. - [ ] **Pooling layers have ZERO learnable parameters** — classic viva question. - [ ] Output dimension formula: $H_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H_{in} + 2p - f}{s} \right\rfloor + 1$ (use `\vert` or `\mid` when writing this inline, never `|`). - [ ] Parameter count for CONV: $P_{conv} = (f \cdot f \cdot C_{in} + 1) \cdot F$ (the $+1$ accounts for the bias per filter). - [ ] **Stride $> 1$** sub-samples (no need for a separate pooling layer if stride-convolution is used). - [ ] **Padding $p$** preserves the border; $p = \frac{f-1}{2}$ with stride 1 gives "same" output. - [ ] **Translation Invariance** comes from weight sharing + pooling; **Translation Equivariance** is the property of the convolution operation itself. - [ ] Hierarchical feature learning: edges $\rightarrow$ textures $\rightarrow$ parts $\rightarrow$ objects. - [ ] Popular legacy architectures for the KTU exam: **LeNet-5, AlexNet, VGG-16, ResNet** (memorize approximate layer counts). - [ ] **Softmax + Cross-Entropy** is the standard output layer + loss for multi-class CNN classifiers. - [ ] Always explicitly state: **filter size, number of filters, stride, padding** when computing output dimensions in numerical problems. > [!NOTE] > **Final Examiner's Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 3 carries **15% of the course marks** (typically one 14-mark Part B question). Numerical problems on output dimension and parameter count are guaranteed. Diagram-based theory questions on architecture and applications are also highly probable. Practice both.

topics.title: Case Study: Application of ML for Phishing Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Study: Application of ML for Phishing Detection # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Phishing** is a form of social engineering cyber-attack in which an attacker masquerades as a legitimate and trustworthy entity (such as a bank, e-commerce portal, or government agency) to deceive users into revealing sensitive information such as usernames, passwords, credit card numbers, or personally identifiable information (PII). Phishing attacks are typically delivered through email, SMS (smishing), voice calls (vishing), or fraudulent websites. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 3, Case Study):** > *Machine Learning (ML)-based Phishing Detection is a cybersecurity application of Artificial Intelligence in which computational models are trained on labeled datasets of legitimate and phishing instances to automatically classify, with high precision, whether a given URL, email, or webpage is malicious — without relying on static blacklists alone.* ## 1.2 The ML Paradigm in Phishing Detection Traditional phishing defense relied on **blacklist-based filtering** (blocking known malicious URLs). This approach failed because: - It could not detect **zero-day phishing attacks**. - Attackers easily generated new URLs using obfuscation techniques. - Manual rule maintenance could not scale with the explosive growth of the internet. **ML-based detection** solves this by learning *patterns* rather than memorizing URLs. The model generalizes from features extracted across millions of samples and flags previously unseen attacks. > [!NOTE] > **Core Syllabus Highlight:** > Phishing detection is a classic example of a **binary classification problem** in supervised learning, where the input is a feature vector representing a URL/email/webpage, and the output label $y \in \{0, 1\}$ where $y=1$ denotes "phishing" and $y=0$ denotes "legitimate". ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Post Office Inspector Imagine a postal inspector at a busy sorting facility. The inspector does not personally know every fraudster, but over years of experience has learned to **spot subtle cues** in envelopes: | Inspector's Experience | ML Equivalent | |---|---| | Notices misspelled return addresses | Lexical features of URL (e.g., `paypa1.com`) | | Detects fake official logos on letters | Visual features of webpage (logo similarity) | | Flags unusual sender country | Host-based features (geolocation, WHOIS) | | Sorts mail into "Trusted" / "Suspicious" | Binary classifier output: Legitimate / Phishing | Just as the inspector improves with more letters seen, an ML model **improves with more training data** — this is *learning from experience*, the central tenet of ML. ## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Phishing Detection The following metrics are used universally in evaluating any phishing detection system (and in the KTU 2024 syllabus): - **True Positive (TP)**: Phishing URL correctly identified as phishing. - **True Negative (TN)**: Legitimate URL correctly identified as legitimate. - **False Positive (FP)**: Legitimate URL wrongly flagged as phishing (Type I Error). - **False Negative (FN)**: Phishing URL missed (Type II Error) — the most dangerous error in cybersecurity. - **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**, **ROC-AUC**. > [!TIP] > **Why Recall matters most in cybersecurity:** A False Negative means a phishing site slipped through and a user gets compromised. Hence phishing detectors are optimized for **high Recall** (also called *True Positive Rate* or *Sensitivity*). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ML Pipeline for Phishing Detection A typical end-to-end ML pipeline for phishing detection consists of **six operational stages**: ### Stage 1 — Data Collection - **Sources:** PhishTank, OpenPhish, UCI ML Repository (PhiData, Phishing Websites dataset), Alexa Top 1M (legitimate URLs), Common Crawl. - **Volume:** Modern datasets contain **100,000+ URL samples** with balanced or imbalanced class distributions. - **Labeling:** Each URL is assigned a ground-truth label $y \in \{0, 1\}$. ### Stage 2 — Feature Engineering (The Most Critical Step) Features are extracted along **three orthogonal dimensions** that together build a high-dimensional fingerprint of the URL: **A. Lexical (URL String) Features** — 30+ features typically - Length of URL, length of hostname, number of dots, presence of `@` symbol, IP address usage, suspicious characters (`-`, `_`), use of HTTPS, number of subdomains, presence of suspicious words ("login", "verify", "secure", "bank"). **B. Host-Based Features** — 15+ features - Age of domain (WHOIS), country of registration, authoritative DNS records, SSL certificate issuer, domain registration length. **C. Content / HTML-Based Features** — 20+ features - Number of iframes, presence of onmouseover, anchor href mismatch, SFH (Server Form Handler) legitimacy, request URLs to external domains, abnormal CSS/font usage, page rank. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Feature engineering is worth **at least 2–3 marks** in a 14-mark KTU question. Always mention at least two categories with 2–3 example features each. ### Stage 3 — Data Preprocessing - **Normalization:** Min-Max scaling of numerical features to $[0, 1]$. - **Encoding:** Categorical features (e.g., TLD type) one-hot encoded. - **Balancing:** SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) applied if dataset is skewed (e.g., 80% legitimate vs 20% phishing). - **Train-Test Split:** Typically **70% training / 30% testing**, or 80/20 with cross-validation. ### Stage 4 — Model Selection & Training Common algorithms used in the case study: | Algorithm | Type | Strength in Phishing Context | |---|---|---| | **Logistic Regression** | Linear | Interpretable baseline; fast training | | **Decision Tree (C4.5/J48)** | Tree-based | Rule-based explanations; handles mixed features | | **Random Forest** | Ensemble | High accuracy, robust to overfitting | | **XGBoost / Gradient Boosting** | Ensemble | State-of-the-art on tabular phishing data | | **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** | Kernel-based | Effective in high-dimensional feature space | | **Deep Neural Networks (DNN)** | Non-linear | Captures complex non-linear patterns | | **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** | Deep Learning | Used for visual phishing detection (screenshot classification) | ### Stage 5 — Model Evaluation Performance is measured on the **held-out test set** using classification metrics (formulas in §2.2). ### Stage 6 — Deployment & Real-Time Inference The trained model is deployed as a **browser extension, email gateway filter, or web proxy** that scores every visited URL in real-time. New attacks are fed back for **periodic retraining** (online/incremental learning). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU 2024 Board Examinations. Memorize the formula and the meaning of every variable. | Metric | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of all correct predictions | | **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of URLs flagged phishing, how many truly were | | **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual phishing URLs, how many were caught | | **Specificity (TNR)** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of legitimate URLs, how many were correctly passed | | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - \text{Spec}$ | Probability of false alarm | | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall | | **Error Rate** | $\text{ERR} = \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = 1 - \text{Acc}$ | Fraction of all wrong predictions | | **Logistic (Sigmoid) Function** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Squashes real-valued score to $[0,1]$ probability | | **Linear Score (Logistic Reg.)** | $z = w^T x + b = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j + b$ | Weighted sum of features | | **Cross-Entropy Loss (Log Loss)** | $L = -\dfrac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$ | Loss minimized during training | > [!WARNING] > In the markdown formula table above, vertical bars in formulas have been written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown pipe `|` used for column separators. KTU students frequently make the mistake of writing `|x|` in a table row, which corrupts the table. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Browser Security (e.g., Google Chrome Safe Browsing, Mozilla Firefox Phishing Protection):** Use ML models trained on billions of URLs to warn users in real time. - **Email Gateways (e.g., Microsoft Defender for Office 365):** Apply ML to email headers, body, and embedded URLs to quarantine phishing emails. - **Banking & FinTech (e.g., RBI's fraud monitoring, PayPal):** Block phishing transactions at the payment layer. - **Enterprise SOCs (Security Operations Centers):** Automate triage of reported phishing incidents using NLP-based email classifiers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Logistic Regression Decision Function Logistic Regression is the most commonly used baseline model in the KTU 2024 case study. We derive its mathematical form from first principles. **Step 1 — Linear Combination (Score)** We start with a weighted linear combination of all $n$ features for a single URL: $$ z = w^T x + b = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j + b $$ **Step 2 — Problem with the Linear Score** The variable $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$, but we need a probability $P(y=1 \mid x) \in [0, 1]$. A linear function is unbounded — so it cannot directly represent a probability. **Step 3 — Apply the Sigmoid (Logistic) Function** We pass $z$ through the **sigmoid** function $\sigma$: $$ \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} $$ This maps any real number to the open interval $(0, 1)$, satisfying the probability requirement. **Step 4 — Final Model Output** The probability that the URL is phishing is: $$ \hat{y} = P(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}} $$ The probability that it is legitimate is: $$ P(y = 0 \mid x) = 1 - \hat{y} $$ **Step 5 — Decision Rule** At test time, a threshold $\tau = 0.5$ is applied: $$ \text{Predicted Class} = \begin{cases} 1 \; (\text{Phishing}) & \text{if } \hat{y} \geq \tau \\ 0 \; (\text{Legitimate}) & \text{if } \hat{y} < \tau \end{cases} $$ **Step 6 — Cost Function (Cross-Entropy / Log Loss)** The model parameters $\mathbf{w}, b$ are learned by minimizing the average log loss over $m$ training samples: $$ L(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \Big[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \Big] $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why log loss?** It is convex in $\mathbf{w}$, so gradient descent converges to a global minimum. Squared error is not used for classification because it yields non-convex loss surfaces here. ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Confusion Matrix → All Metrics The following worked example is **directly aligned with KTU Board exam style** (often asked for 3–4 marks in Part A and reused in Part B). > **Problem Statement:** > A phishing detection model is evaluated on a test set of **200 URLs**. Out of these, **120 are actual phishing URLs** and **80 are legitimate**. The model's predictions produce the following confusion matrix: > - True Positives (TP) = 108 > - False Negatives (FN) = 12 > - True Negatives (TN) = 72 > - False Positives (FP) = 8 > - Total = $108 + 12 + 72 + 8 = 200$ ✓ **Step 1 — Verify Counts** - Phishing: TP + FN = $108 + 12 = 120$ ✓ - Legitimate: TN + FP = $72 + 8 = 80$ ✓ **Step 2 — Accuracy** $$ \text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{108 + 72}{200} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 $$ **Step 3 — Precision (PPV — Positive Predictive Value)** $$ \text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{108}{108 + 8} = \frac{108}{116} = 0.9310 $$ **Step 4 — Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** $$ \text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{108}{108 + 12} = \frac{108}{120} = 0.9000 $$ **Step 5 — Specificity (TNR)** $$ \text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{72}{72 + 8} = \frac{72}{80} = 0.9000 $$ **Step 6 — F1-Score** $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9310 \times 0.9000}{0.9310 + 0.9000} $$ $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8379}{1.8310} = 2 \cdot 0.4577 = 0.9154 $$ **Step 7 — Error Rate** $$ \text{ERR} = \frac{FP + FN}{Total} = \frac{8 + 12}{200} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10 $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Note:** Notice that Accuracy (0.90) equals Error Rate (0.10) only when totals sum to 1, which is the case here. This is a classic KTU trick question. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of ML Models on a Standard Phishing Dataset The table below summarizes the typical results reported in published phishing-detection case studies (e.g., Mohammad et al., 2014; Sahingoz et al., 2019). It is frequently expected in KTU descriptive answers. | ML Algorithm | Accuracy | Precision | Recall | F1-Score | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Logistic Regression | 0.92 – 0.94 | 0.92 | 0.91 | 0.91 | Simple, interpretable baseline | | Decision Tree (J48) | 0.95 – 0.96 | 0.95 | 0.95 | 0.95 | Easy to convert to IF-THEN rules | | Random Forest | 0.97 – 0.98 | 0.97 | 0.97 | 0.97 | Ensemble of decision trees, low variance | | XGBoost | 0.98 – 0.99 | 0.98 | 0.98 | 0.98 | Top performer on tabular phishing data | | SVM (RBF Kernel) | 0.95 – 0.97 | 0.96 | 0.95 | 0.95 | Good for high-dimensional feature vectors | | Deep Neural Network | 0.97 – 0.99 | 0.98 | 0.98 | 0.98 | Best when trained on millions of samples | | CNN (visual phishing) | 0.99+ | 0.99 | 0.99 | 0.99 | Used for screenshot-based detection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** When asked *"Which ML algorithm is best for phishing detection?"* the textbook-correct answer is **Random Forest or XGBoost** for tabular URL data, due to a strong trade-off between **accuracy, training time, and interpretability**. Pure deep learning wins only at extreme scale (millions of samples). ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Forward Pass of a Single URL through Logistic Regression Let us work a complete numerical example with **3 features** for a URL `http://paypa1-login.com/verify`: | Feature $x_j$ | Description | Value | |---|---|---| | $x_1$ | URL length $\geq 54$ chars (1=yes, 0=no) | $x_1 = 1$ | | $x_2$ | Has IP address (1=yes, 0=no) | $x_2 = 0$ | | $x_3$ | Suspicious word "login" present (1=yes, 0=no) | $x_3 = 1$ | | $w_1, w_2, w_3$ | Trained weights | $1.2, \; 0.8, \; 0.5$ | | $b$ | Bias | $-0.9$ | **Step 1 — Compute Linear Score** $$ z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b = (1.2)(1) + (0.8)(0) + (0.5)(1) + (-0.9) = 1.2 + 0 + 0.5 - 0.9 = 0.8 $$ **Step 2 — Apply Sigmoid** $$ \hat{y} = \sigma(0.8) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.8}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.4493} = \frac{1}{1.4493} = 0.6900 $$ **Step 3 — Decision at Threshold $\tau = 0.5$** $$ \hat{y} = 0.69 \geq 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Class = Phishing (1)} \;\;\checkmark $$ > [!NOTE] > **Takeaway:** The sigmoid output $0.69$ means the model is **69% confident** that this URL is phishing. This probability is what security teams use to rank alerts in a Security Operations Center (SOC) dashboard. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End ML Pipeline for Phishing Detection The diagram below maps the entire operational workflow of an ML-based phishing detector — from raw data ingestion to deployed model — in a board-exam-friendly block-level architecture. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw URL Data Sources
PhishTank OpenPhish Alexa] --> B[Data Cleaning
Deduplication and Label Validation] B --> C[Feature Extraction Module] C --> C1[Lexical Features
URL String Pattern] C --> C2[Host Based Features
WHOIS DNS SSL] C --> C3[Content Features
HTML DOM Heuristics] C1 --> D[Feature Vector x of size 60 plus] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> E[Preprocessing
Normalization and SMOTE] E --> F[Train Validation Test Split
70 percent 15 percent 15 percent] F --> G[Model Training
Logistic Regression RF XGBoost DNN] G --> H[Hyperparameter Tuning
Grid Search CV] H --> I[Model Evaluation
Accuracy Precision Recall F1] I --> J{Performance Acceptable} J -- No --> G J -- Yes --> K[Deploy Model
Browser Extension or API] K --> L[Real Time Inference
Score New URLs] L --> M[Feedback Loop
Retrain with New Samples] M --> B ``` ## 4.2 Decision Boundary of the Logistic Regression Classifier The figure below illustrates how a binary classifier separates the feature space into "Phishing" and "Legitimate" regions. A new URL is projected onto this space and classified based on which side of the decision boundary it falls. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FeatureSpace["Feature Space x1 x2"] P1[Phishing URLs Cluster
Class Label y equals 1] -.->|Sigmoid boundary| Q1[Decision Boundary
wTx plus b equals 0] Q1 -.->|Linear separator| R1[Legitimate URLs Cluster
Class Label y equals 0] end NewURL[New URL x1 x2] -->|Score equals wx plus b| Q1 Q1 -->|Score greater than zero| PhishOut[Classified as Phishing] Q1 -->|Score less than zero| LegitOut[Classified as Legitimate] ``` ## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Visualization The figure below is the **standard KTU Board Diagram** for confusion matrix questions. Memorize this layout — examiners expect it verbatim in 7-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CM[Confusion Matrix 2 by 2] A1[Actual Positive row 1] --> B1[TP cell top left] A1 --> B2[FN cell top right] A2[Actual Negative row 2] --> C1[FP cell bottom left] A2 --> C2[TN cell bottom right] end B1 --> D1[Precision equals TP by TP plus FP] B1 --> D2[Recall equals TP by TP plus FN] C2 --> D3[Specificity equals TN by TN plus FP] D1 --> E1[F1 Score equals 2 Prec Rec by Prec plus Rec] D2 --> E1 D3 --> F1[Accuracy equals TP plus TN by Total] E1 --> F1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram matters:** Drawing the confusion matrix and marking TP/TN/FP/FN in the correct cells fetches **full 1 mark** for diagram alone in KTU 2024 Part B answers. Confusing the layout (placing FP in the top-right) is a top-3 marking error. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style] **Q: Define phishing. List any four lexical features used in ML-based phishing detection.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Phishing is a cyber-attack in which an attacker impersonates a trusted entity (such as a bank or e-commerce site) to fraudulently obtain sensitive information like usernames, passwords, and credit card details, typically through fake emails, SMS, or websites. **Four Lexical Features (2 Marks — ½ mark each):** 1. **Length of URL** — Phishing URLs are often longer than legitimate ones. 2. **Presence of IP address in URL** — Legitimate sites use domain names; raw IPs (e.g., `http://203.0.113.5/login`) are suspicious. 3. **Number of dots / subdomains** — Phishing URLs often have excessive subdomains (e.g., `paypal.com.secure-login.verify.ru`). 4. **Suspicious keywords in URL** — Words like `login`, `verify`, `secure`, `account`, `update`, `bank` appearing in the domain string. 5. *(Additional valid option)* Use of `@` symbol in URL — browser ignores everything before `@`, redirecting to a hidden address. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style] **Q: Differentiate between Precision and Recall. Why is Recall considered more critical in phishing detection?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Precision | Recall | |---|---|---| | Formula | $TP / (TP + FP)$ | $TP / (TP + FN)$ | | Question answered | *"Of all URLs flagged phishing, how many actually are?"* | *"Of all actual phishing URLs, how many did we catch?"* | | Focus | Reducing False Alarms (FP) | Reducing Misses (FN) | **Why Recall is critical (1 Mark):** In phishing detection, a *False Negative* (missing a real phishing URL) leads directly to **user compromise, financial loss, and data breach**. A *False Positive* (blocking a legitimate URL) is an inconvenience. Therefore, security systems **prioritize high Recall**, often accepting slightly lower Precision to minimize dangerous False Negatives. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Pattern:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following. Each question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**. ### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style] **Q: (a)** Explain the complete end-to-end Machine Learning pipeline for detecting phishing URLs. Include all stages from data collection to model deployment, with at least two example features in each feature category. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A phishing detection model is tested on **500 URLs**, of which **300 are phishing** and **200 are legitimate**. The model produces the following confusion matrix values: TP = 270, FN = 30, FP = 20, TN = 180. Calculate the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, and F1-Score** of the model. Comment on the model's performance. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution A(a) — ML Pipeline (7 Marks) The end-to-end ML pipeline for phishing URL detection consists of **six sequential stages**: **Stage 1 — Data Collection (1 Mark):** Gather labeled URLs from public repositories such as **PhishTank, OpenPhish, UCI Machine Learning Repository (Phishing Websites Dataset), and Alexa Top 1M** for legitimate URLs. Each sample is labeled $y=1$ (phishing) or $y=0$ (legitimate). A balanced dataset of ~100,000 URLs is typical for academic studies. **Stage 2 — Feature Engineering (2 Marks — the most important stage):** Three feature families are extracted: - **Lexical Features (URL String):** URL length, number of dots, presence of `@`, IP address usage, special characters, suspicious tokens (`login`, `verify`, `secure`), HTTPS usage. *Example:* `http://203.0.113.5/login.html` triggers IP + suspicious word features. - **Host-Based Features:** Domain age, WHOIS registration period, country of registration, SSL certificate authority. *Example:* A domain registered 2 days ago is highly suspicious. - **Content/HTML Features:** Number of iframes, anchor-href mismatch, external request URLs, presence of `onmouseover` to hide the real link. *Example:* Login form posting credentials to a different domain. **Stage 3 — Preprocessing (1 Mark):** Apply Min-Max normalization to bring all features to $[0,1]$. Apply **SMOTE** if the dataset is class-imbalanced. Split into 70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing. **Stage 4 — Model Training (1 Mark):** Train classifiers such as **Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, or Deep Neural Networks**. The model learns weights $\mathbf{w}$ that minimize a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy). **Stage 5 — Evaluation (1 Mark):** Measure performance using **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and ROC-AUC** on the held-out test set. Use **k-fold cross-validation** (typically $k=5$ or $k=10$) for robust estimates. **Stage 6 — Deployment & Retraining (1 Mark):** Deploy the model as a **browser extension, web proxy, or email gateway** for real-time scoring. New URLs flagged by users are added to the training set, and the model is **retrained periodically** to handle evolving phishing patterns. --- #### Solution A(b) — Numerical Metrics (7 Marks) **Given:** - Total = 500; Phishing = 300; Legitimate = 200 - TP = 270, FN = 30, FP = 20, TN = 180 **Verification:** $TP+FN = 270+30 = 300$ ✓ ; $FP+TN = 20+180 = 200$ ✓ **Step 1 — Accuracy (1.5 Marks):** $$ \text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{Total} = \frac{270 + 180}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 90\% $$ **Step 2 — Precision (1.5 Marks):** $$ \text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{270}{270 + 20} = \frac{270}{290} = 0.9310 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 93.10\% $$ **Step 3 — Recall (1.5 Marks):** $$ \text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{270}{270 + 30} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.9000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 90.00\% $$ **Step 4 — Specificity (1 Mark):** $$ \text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.9000 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 90.00\% $$ **Step 5 — F1-Score (1 Mark):** $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9310 \times 0.9000}{0.9310 + 0.9000} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8379}{1.8310} = 0.9154 $$ **Step 6 — Performance Comment (0.5 Mark):** The model achieves **90% accuracy with 93.10% Precision and 90% Recall**, indicating strong and balanced performance. The high F1-Score (0.9154) confirms the model is suitable for real-world deployment, though further tuning (e.g., threshold adjustment) could push Recall even higher to minimize dangerous False Negatives. --- ### Question B [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] **Q: (a)** Compare the following algorithms for phishing detection: **Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and Deep Neural Networks**. Discuss their relative strengths, weaknesses, and typical performance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is the **sigmoid (logistic) function**? Derive the **logistic regression prediction formula** from a linear score and explain how a decision threshold is applied to classify a URL as phishing or legitimate. Use the URL `https://accounts-secure-bank-verify.com/login` with weights $w_1 = 1.5$ (URL length flag), $w_2 = 0.7$ (suspicious words count), $b = -1.0$ to demonstrate. Assume $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 3$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution B(a) — Algorithm Comparison (7 Marks) | Aspect | Logistic Regression | Random Forest | Deep Neural Network | |---|---|---|---| | **Model Type** | Linear classifier | Ensemble of decision trees | Multi-layer non-linear network | | **Mathematical Basis** | Sigmoid on linear score $z = w^T x + b$ | Bagging + majority voting on $N$ trees | Composition of weighted non-linear activations | | **Training Time** | Very fast | Moderate | High (needs GPU for large data) | | **Interpretability** | High (weights show feature impact) | Medium (feature importance plots) | Low (black box) | | **Handling Non-linearity** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | | **Data Requirement** | Works on small datasets | Works on medium datasets | Requires large datasets (10k+ samples) | | **Typical Accuracy on Phishing** | 92% – 94% | 97% – 98% | 97% – 99% | | **Overfitting Risk** | Low | Low (bagging reduces variance) | High (needs regularization/dropout) | | **Best Use Case** | Quick baseline; small datasets | Production-grade tabular phishing data | Visual phishing, very large datasets | **Conclusion (1 Mark):** **Random Forest** offers the best trade-off between accuracy, training time, and interpretability for typical phishing detection tasks. **Deep Neural Networks** are preferred only when the dataset is extremely large or when visual (screenshot-based) features are used. **Logistic Regression** remains an indispensable baseline. --- #### Solution B(b) — Sigmoid and Logistic Regression Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Definition of the Sigmoid (1 Mark):** The sigmoid (or logistic) function is a real-valued, differentiable, monotonically increasing function that maps any real input $z \in \mathbb{R}$ to the open interval $(0, 1)$. It is defined as: $$ \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} $$ **Step 2 — Linear Score (1 Mark):** Given an input feature vector $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$, the linear score is computed as: $$ z = w^T x + b = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j + b $$ **Step 3 — Logistic Regression Prediction (1 Mark):** The probability that the input URL is phishing is obtained by applying the sigmoid: $$ \hat{y} = P(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}} $$ **Step 4 — Threshold-Based Decision (1 Mark):** A threshold $\tau$ (default 0.5) converts the probability to a class label: $$ \hat{y}_{\text{class}} = \begin{cases} 1 \; (\text{Phishing}) & \text{if } \hat{y} \geq \tau \\ 0 \; (\text{Legitimate}) & \text{if } \hat{y} < \tau \end{cases} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical Demonstration (2 Marks):** For the given URL with $w_1 = 1.5$, $w_2 = 0.7$, $b = -1.0$, $x_1 = 1$, $x_2 = 3$: $$ z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = (1.5)(1) + (0.7)(3) + (-1.0) = 1.5 + 2.1 - 1.0 = 2.6 $$ $$ \hat{y} = \sigma(2.6) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-2.6}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.0743} = \frac{1}{1.0743} = 0.9309 $$ **Step 6 — Final Decision (1 Mark):** Since $\hat{y} = 0.9309 \geq 0.5$, the URL is classified as **Phishing** ✓ with **93.09% confidence**. This is consistent with the visual cues: long URL, multiple suspicious words (`accounts`, `secure`, `bank`, `verify`), and a `.com` domain with a brand-impersonation pattern. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusion Matrix Layout Error (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently swap FP and FN positions, or write Precision formula as $TP/(TP+FN)$ instead of $TP/(TP+FP)$. Memorize the layout: **Top-Left = TP, Top-Right = FN, Bottom-Left = FP, Bottom-Right = TN.** > 2. **Forgetting to Scale Inputs (–1 mark):** When explaining Logistic Regression, students often skip normalization. Always state that features are Min-Max normalized to $[0,1]$ before training. > 3. **Using Accuracy as the Sole Metric (–0.5 to –1 mark):** In imbalanced phishing datasets, accuracy is misleading. Always quote **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** alongside accuracy. > 4. **Vague "Feature Engineering" Answers (–1 mark):** Writing *"features are extracted"* without naming 2–3 concrete features per category is a guaranteed partial-credit killer. Always give examples. > 5. **No Mention of Class Imbalance / SMOTE (–0.5 mark):** Real phishing datasets are skewed; mentioning SMOTE or class-weight balancing earns bonus marks. > 6. **Skipping the Decision Threshold (–1 mark):** In sigmoid derivations, the final threshold step $\hat{y} \geq 0.5 \Rightarrow$ Phishing is mandatory; omitting it loses 1 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Phishing** is a social-engineering cyber-attack; **ML-based detection** generalizes from features instead of using static blacklists. - The problem is a **supervised binary classification** with $y \in \{0, 1\}$. - The **ML pipeline** has 6 stages: Data Collection → Feature Engineering → Preprocessing → Model Training → Evaluation → Deployment + Retraining. - **Three feature families**: Lexical (URL string), Host-based (WHOIS/DNS/SSL), Content/HTML (DOM heuristics). Always name 2–3 examples per family. - **Algorithms**: Logistic Regression (baseline), Decision Tree, Random Forest (best trade-off), XGBoost, SVM, DNN, CNN. - **Evaluation Metrics** — memorize all formulas: - $\text{Acc} = (TP+TN)/N$ - $\text{Prec} = TP/(TP+FP)$ - $\text{Rec} = TP/(TP+FN)$ — **most critical for cybersecurity** - $\text{Spec} = TN/(TN+FP)$ - $F_1 = 2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec} / (\text{Prec} + \text{Rec})$ - **Sigmoid function**: $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$, used in Logistic Regression. - **Logistic Regression prediction**: $\hat{y} = \sigma(w^T x + b)$. - **Decision rule**: $\hat{y} \geq 0.5 \Rightarrow$ Phishing, else Legitimate. - **Confusion Matrix Layout** (top to bottom, left to right): [TP, FN; FP, TN] — memorize this verbatim. - **Real-world deployments**: Google Safe Browsing, Microsoft Defender, Mozilla Phishing Protection, banking fraud systems. - **Class imbalance** is a real challenge — mention **SMOTE** or class-weight balancing in your answers. - **Why Recall over Accuracy?** False Negatives (missed phishing) cause actual security breaches; False Positives cause user inconvenience. Therefore, optimize for high Recall. - **Cross-Entropy Loss** $L = -\frac{1}{m}\sum[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ is the cost function minimized during Logistic Regression training. - **Top KTU Pitfall**: Always show the **confusion matrix diagram** in your 14-mark answers; it fetches 1 free mark.

topics.title: Architecture and Applications (General Understanding of Natural Language Processing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Architecture and Applications (General Understanding of Natural Language Processing) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computational Linguistics that focuses on the interaction between computers and human (natural) languages. It involves designing algorithms, models, and systems that can **understand, interpret, generate, and respond to human language** in a manner that is both contextually and semantically meaningful. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECCT413 – Module 4)**, the study of NLP architecture requires a *general understanding* of the layered pipeline that converts unstructured textual data into structured representations that machine learning models can process. The core objective of NLP can be formally expressed as a mapping function: $$f: \mathcal{T}_{human} \rightarrow \mathcal{R}_{machine}$$ where $\mathcal{T}_{human}$ represents the space of natural language utterances (sentences, paragraphs, documents) and $\mathcal{R}_{machine}$ represents the structured representation (vectors, tags, intents, or actions) that a machine learning model can act upon. > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Terminology to Memorize:** > - **Corpus** – A large, structured collection of texts used for training NLP models. > - **Token** – The smallest discrete unit of text (word, sub-word, or character). > - **Lexicon** – A vocabulary or dictionary used by the system. > - **Syntax** – The grammatical structure of sentences. > - **Semantics** – The meaning conveyed by the text. > - **Pragmatics** – The intended meaning based on context. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **multilingual translator sitting in a glass booth at an international airport**. Passengers speak to you in English, Hindi, Malayalam, Tamil, and Japanese. Your job is to: 1. **Listen carefully** (Input acquisition) – convert sound waves into written text. 2. **Break the sentence into words** (Tokenization) – separate "Iwantaflight" into "I", "want", "a", "flight". 3. **Identify the grammar** (Syntactic Analysis) – understand that "I" is the subject, "want" is the verb, "flight" is the object. 4. **Understand the meaning** (Semantic Analysis) – know that the passenger wants to book an air ticket. 5. **Identify the intent** (Pragmatic Analysis) – recognize that the user is requesting a service. 6. **Generate a response** (Output Generation) – reply in the user's language with appropriate information. This is *exactly* what an NLP system does, except it works in **milliseconds**, handles **billions of sentences**, and learns from **massive datasets** rather than from a single human life of experience. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently use "Explain NLP with a real-world analogy" as a 3-mark question. The airport translator or "language decoder" analogy is a high-scoring, intuitive answer that demonstrates genuine understanding rather than rote memorization. ## 1.3 Why NLP is Critical in Modern AI Natural language is the **most natural interface for human-computer interaction**. According to industry reports, **over 80% of the world's data is unstructured text** — emails, chat logs, social media posts, medical records, legal documents, and scientific papers. Without NLP, this vast reservoir of information remains inaccessible to automated systems. The three foundational challenges of NLP, as classified by Jurafsky & Martin's classical textbook framework, are: | Challenge | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Ambiguity** | One sentence, many meanings | "I saw the man with the telescope." (Who has the telescope?) | | **Variability** | Same meaning, many expressions | "Good", "Awesome", "Fantastic", "Brilliant" | | **Sparsity** | Vast vocabulary, limited data per word | Rare technical jargon in legal/medical domains | | **Context Dependency** | Meaning changes with surrounding text | "Bank" (river bank vs. financial bank) | These four challenges form the *core motivation* behind the entire NLP architecture that we will study next. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Layered NLP Architecture The standard NLP architecture is organized as a **pipeline of processing stages**, where each stage transforms the text into a progressively richer representation. This is conceptually similar to the layers of an onion — each layer peels back a deeper level of linguistic understanding. > [!NOTE] > **The 7-Stage NLP Pipeline (KTU High-Yield):** > 1. **Sentence Segmentation** – Split document into individual sentences. > 2. **Tokenization** – Split sentences into words/sub-words. > 3. **Stop-word Removal** – Filter out common, low-information words (a, the, is, of). > 4. **Stemming / Lemmatization** – Reduce words to their root form. > 5. **Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** – Label each token with its grammatical role. > 6. **Named Entity Recognition (NER)** – Identify proper nouns (people, places, organizations). > 7. **Semantic / Sentiment Analysis** – Extract meaning, intent, or emotion. Let us now dissect each stage with mathematical and engineering rigor. ## 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown ### **Stage 1: Sentence Segmentation** A document $D$ composed of $n$ sentences is segmented as: $$D = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_n\}$$ The segmentation boundary is typically detected using **period (.), question mark (?), exclamation mark (!)**, or by using machine learning classifiers trained on punctuation patterns. ### **Stage 2: Tokenization** A sentence $s_i$ is tokenized into a sequence: $$s_i = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_k\}$$ Modern transformers (like BERT) use **sub-word tokenization** algorithms such as **WordPiece**, **Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)**, or **SentencePiece**. This solves the **Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV)** problem by breaking rare words into frequent sub-units. ### **Stage 3: Stop-word Removal** A filtered token list is created: $$T_{filtered} = \{t \in s_i : t \notin \mathcal{S}\}$$ where $\mathcal{S}$ is the **stop-word set** (e.g., {a, an, the, is, of, in, on}). > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT always remove stop-words blindly. For sentiment analysis and machine translation, stop-words often carry critical negation cues ("not good", "no problem"). Always justify the removal in your exam answer. ### **Stage 4: Stemming vs. Lemmatization** Both reduce words to their base form, but they differ in linguistic sophistication: | Aspect | Stemming | Lemmatization | |---|---|---| | **Method** | Rule-based suffix stripping | Dictionary + morphological analysis | | **Output** | Crude root (may not be a real word) | Linguistically valid lemma | | **Example** | "running" → "runn" | "running" → "run" | | **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (requires lookup) | | **Algorithm** | Porter Stemmer, Snowball | WordNet Lemmatizer, spaCy | The Porter Stemmer applies a cascade of rules. A simplified version of one rule is: $$\text{if } t \text{ ends in "ies"} \rightarrow \text{replace with "y"}$$ $$\text{e.g., "ponies" } \rightarrow \text{"poni"}$$ ### **Stage 5: Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging** Each token is assigned a grammatical tag from a predefined tagset $\mathcal{G}$: $$g: T \rightarrow \mathcal{G}, \quad g(t_i) = \text{POS tag}$$ Common tags include: **NN** (Noun), **VB** (Verb), **JJ** (Adjective), **RB** (Adverb), **PRP** (Pronoun), **DT** (Determiner). ### **Stage 6: Named Entity Recognition (NER)** NER identifies and classifies named entities into categories like **PERSON, LOCATION, ORGANIZATION, DATE, MONEY**. Mathematically, NER is a **sequence labeling problem** solved using Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) or BiLSTM-CRF models. ### **Stage 7: Semantic Analysis** This is the final and most abstract layer. It produces a meaning representation that can drive downstream applications. ## 2.3 Mathematical Foundations of Text Representation For KTU 2024, you must understand three core text representation techniques: ### **A. Bag of Words (BoW)** The BoW model represents a document as a vector of word counts. For a vocabulary $\mathcal{V}$ of size $V$: $$\vec{d} = [c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots, c_V]$$ where $c_i$ is the count of word $w_i$ in document $d$. ### **B. TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)** This is a **statistical measure** that evaluates how important a word is to a document in a collection (corpus). **Term Frequency (TF):** $$TF(t, d) = \frac{\text{Number of times term } t \text{ appears in document } d}{\text{Total number of terms in document } d}$$ **Inverse Document Frequency (IDF):** $$IDF(t, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{\vert \{d \in D : t \in d\} \vert}\right)$$ where $N$ is the total number of documents in the corpus $D$. **TF-IDF Score:** $$TF\text{-}IDF(t, d, D) = TF(t, d) \times IDF(t, D)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why TF-IDF works:** Common words like "the", "a", "is" have HIGH TF but LOW IDF (they appear in almost all documents), so their product is low. Rare, discriminative words like "photosynthesis" have LOW TF but HIGH IDF, so they get high overall scores. This is the genius of TF-IDF. ### **C. Word Embeddings (Dense Vector Representation)** Modern NLP uses **distributed representations** where each word is mapped to a dense vector of dimension $d$ (typically 50–300): $$\vec{w} = [e_1, e_2, \dots, e_d] \in \mathbb{R}^d$$ The most famous algorithm is **Word2Vec**, which uses two architectures: - **CBOW (Continuous Bag of Words):** Predicts target word from context. - **Skip-gram:** Predicts context words from a target word. The fundamental idea is captured by the **distributional hypothesis**: > *"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."* — J.R. Firth, 1957 This means words appearing in similar contexts have similar embeddings, and the famous equation holds: $$\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula / Concept** | **Equation / Definition** | **Purpose / Use Case** | |---|---|---| | Term Frequency | $TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Normalized word count in a document | | Inverse Document Frequency | $IDF(t,D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + df(t)}\right)$ | Penalizes common words across corpus | | TF-IDF Score | $TF\text{-}IDF = TF \times IDF$ | Weighs words by importance | | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert}$ | Measures similarity between two documents | | Bag of Words vector | $\vec{d} \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert \mathcal{V} \vert}$ | Sparse representation of document | | Word Embedding dim | $\vec{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, d \in [50, 300]$ | Dense semantic representation | | Perplexity (Language Model) | $PPL = 2^{H(p)} = 2^{-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \log_2 p(x_i)}$ | Evaluates language model quality (lower is better) | | BLEU Score (bilingual) | $BLEU = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right)$ | Evaluates machine translation quality | | Edit Distance | $D[i,j] = \min\{D[i-1,j]+1, D[i,j-1]+1, D[i-1,j-1]+c\}$ | Measures string similarity (Levenshtein) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** TF-IDF powers search engines (Elasticsearch, Solr). Word embeddings power chatbots, translation systems (Google Translate), and recommendation engines. Cosine similarity is used in document clustering, plagiarism detection, and information retrieval. ## 2.5 Modern Deep Learning Architectures in NLP The evolution of NLP architectures can be summarized as: 1. **Rule-Based Systems (1950s–1990s):** Hand-crafted grammar rules, regex patterns. Used in early chatbots like ELIZA. 2. **Statistical NLP (1990s–2010s):** HMMs, CRFs, n-gram language models, TF-IDF. 3. **Neural NLP (2013–2017):** Word2Vec, GloVe, RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs. 4. **Transformer Era (2017–Present):** Attention mechanism, BERT, GPT, T5, LLaMA. > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the architecture of an NLP system", always draw the **pipeline diagram** first, then briefly mention the **evolution from rule-based → statistical → neural → transformer-based** approaches. This shows depth and earns full marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: TF-IDF from First Principles Let us rigorously derive why TF-IDF works as an effective weighting scheme. Consider a corpus $D$ with $N = 1000$ documents, and we want to evaluate the importance of the word "**neural**" in a specific document $d_1$. ### **Step 1: Calculate Term Frequency (TF)** Suppose $d_1$ contains **200 words** in total, and the word "neural" appears **4 times** in $d_1$. $$TF(\text{"neural"}, d_1) = \frac{f_{\text{"neural"}, d_1}}{\sum_{t' \in d_1} f_{t', d_1}} = \frac{4}{200} = 0.02$$ *Explanation:* The raw count is normalized by the document length to prevent bias toward longer documents. ### **Step 2: Calculate Document Frequency (DF)** Suppose the word "neural" appears in **50 documents** out of the 1000: $$DF(\text{"neural"}) = 50$$ ### **Step 3: Calculate Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)** Using the smoothed IDF formula (with the +1 smoothing constant to avoid division by zero): $$IDF(\text{"neural"}, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + DF}\right) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{1 + 50}\right) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{51}\right)$$ $$IDF = \log(19.6078) \approx 1.2924$$ ### **Step 4: Calculate TF-IDF Score** $$TF\text{-}IDF(\text{"neural"}, d_1, D) = TF \times IDF = 0.02 \times 1.2924 \approx 0.0258$$ ### **Step 5: Compare with a Common Word** Now consider the word "**the**", which appears **20 times** in $d_1$ and in **950** documents out of 1000. $$TF(\text{"the"}, d_1) = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10$$ $$IDF(\text{"the"}, D) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{1 + 950}\right) = \log\left(\frac{1000}{951}\right) \approx 0.0217$$ $$TF\text{-}IDF(\text{"the"}) = 0.10 \times 0.0217 \approx 0.00217$$ ### **Step 6: Comparative Analysis** $$\frac{TF\text{-}IDF(\text{"neural"})}{TF\text{-}IDF(\text{"the"})} = \frac{0.0258}{0.00217} \approx 11.89$$ **Conclusion:** The word "neural" is weighted **~12 times higher** than "the", despite "the" appearing 5 times more frequently in $d_1$. This is exactly the desired behavior — discriminative, content-bearing words should be prioritized. $$\boxed{TF\text{-}IDF_{\text{"neural"}} \approx 11.89 \times TF\text{-}IDF_{\text{"the"}}}$$ ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Cosine Similarity Between Two Documents Let us compute the cosine similarity between two documents represented as BoW vectors. ### **Step 1: Define the Vocabulary** Consider a tiny vocabulary $\mathcal{V} = \{\text{"AI"}, \text{"ML"}, \text{"deep"}, \text{"learning"}\}$, so $V = 4$. ### **Step 2: Define Document Vectors** $$\vec{d_1} = [3, 2, 1, 2] \quad \text{(Document 1: "AI ML AI deep learning AI ML")}$$ $$\vec{d_2} = [2, 3, 2, 1] \quad \text{(Document 2: "AI ML deep ML AI learning")}$$ ### **Step 3: Compute the Dot Product** $$\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2} = (3)(2) + (2)(3) + (1)(2) + (2)(1) = 6 + 6 + 2 + 2 = 16$$ ### **Step 4: Compute the Magnitudes** $$\vert \vec{d_1} \vert = \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{9 + 4 + 1 + 4} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.2426$$ $$\vert \vec{d_2} \vert = \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.2426$$ ### **Step 5: Compute Cosine Similarity** $$\cos(\theta) = \frac{\vec{d_1} \cdot \vec{d_2}}{\vert \vec{d_1} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{d_2} \vert} = \frac{16}{4.2426 \times 4.2426} = \frac{16}{18} \approx 0.8889$$ **Interpretation:** A cosine similarity of **0.8889** indicates the two documents are highly similar in content (value of 1.0 means identical, 0.0 means orthogonal/disjoint). $$\boxed{\cos(\theta) \approx 0.89 \text{ (high similarity)}}$$ ## 3.3 The Attention Mechanism: Core Intuition The **Transformer architecture** (Vaswani et al., 2017) revolutionized NLP with the **self-attention mechanism**. Given a sequence of token embeddings $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, the attention output is computed as: $$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V$$ where: - $Q = X W_Q$ (Query matrix) - $K = X W_K$ (Key matrix) - $V = X W_V$ (Value matrix) - $d_k$ is the dimensionality of the keys (scaling factor) - $W_Q, W_K, W_V$ are learnable weight matrices > [!NOTE] > **Plain English:** Self-attention allows every word in a sentence to "look at" every other word and decide how much focus to place on it. For example, in "The animal didn't cross the street because **it** was tired", the attention mechanism learns that "it" refers to "animal", not "street". This contextual understanding is what made Transformers dominant in NLP. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of NLP Architectures | **Architecture** | **Year** | **Strength** | **Weakness** | **Best Use Case** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Bag of Words + Naive Bayes | Pre-2000 | Simple, fast, interpretable | Ignores word order, sparse | Spam detection, baseline classifiers | | TF-IDF + SVM | 2000s | Strong for classical tasks | No semantic understanding | Document classification, IR | | Word2Vec / GloVe | 2013 | Captures semantic similarity | One vector per word (no context) | Word similarity, analogy tasks | | RNN / LSTM | 2014–2016 | Handles sequences | Vanishing gradient, slow | Named Entity Recognition | | Seq2Seq + Attention | 2014–2017 | Variable-length input/output | Sequential bottleneck | Machine Translation | | Transformer (BERT/GPT) | 2017–Present | Parallel, contextual, scalable | Computationally expensive | Almost all modern NLP tasks | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Insight:** Module 4 of PECCT413 expects you to understand the *architectural evolution* of NLP, not just the final transformer model. Examiners often ask: *"Compare rule-based, statistical, and neural approaches to NLP"* — the matrix above is your ready-made answer framework. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Standard NLP Pipeline Architecture The following block-level architecture diagram illustrates the complete flow of data through a typical NLP system, from raw text input to a meaningful machine action. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Text Input
Document or Sentence] --> B[Sentence Segmentation
Split into sentences] B --> C[Tokenization
Split into words or subwords] C --> D[Text Normalization
Lowercasing and cleaning] D --> E[Stop Word Removal
Filter common words] E --> F[Stemming or Lemmatization
Reduce to root form] F --> G[POS Tagging
Assign grammatical roles] G --> H[Named Entity Recognition
Identify people places orgs] H --> I[Feature Extraction
TF-IDF or Word Embeddings] I --> J[Machine Learning Model
Classifier or Neural Network] J --> K[Semantic Analysis
Meaning intent sentiment] K --> L[Output Generation
Response or action] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#FFEBCD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style G fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style I fill:#DA70D6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style J fill:#BA55D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style K fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style L fill:#8A2BE2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 NLP Application Domains Map The following diagram categorizes the major application areas of NLP in modern industry, helping you recall them for essay-type questions. ```mermaid mindmap root((NLP
Applications)) Search and IR Google Search Elasticsearch Question Answering Conversational AI Chatbots Virtual Assistants Customer Support Healthcare Clinical NER Drug Discovery Medical Chatbots Finance Sentiment Analysis Fraud Detection News Analytics Education Grammar Checkers Auto Grading Language Learning Legal Contract Analysis E Discovery Compliance Monitoring Social Media Hate Speech Detection Trend Analysis Recommendation ``` ## 4.3 The Transformer Block Architecture (Simplified) For advanced KTU questions on modern NLP, here is a high-level view of the Transformer encoder block, the foundational unit of BERT and GPT-family models. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Embedding
plus Positional Encoding] --> B[Multi Head
Self Attention] B --> C[Add and Norm
Residual Connection] C --> D[Feed Forward
Neural Network] D --> E[Add and Norm
Residual Connection] E --> F[Output to Next
Encoder Block] style A fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#6495ED,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style D fill:#4169E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style F fill:#0066CC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram for exams:** The "Add and Norm" blocks are **residual connections + layer normalization** — a critical engineering trick that prevents vanishing gradients in deep networks. The **Multi-Head Self Attention** allows the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously. This is the *heart* of all modern LLMs (Large Language Models). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** > **Define Natural Language Processing (NLP). List any four major applications of NLP.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a branch of Artificial Intelligence that enables computers to **understand, interpret, and generate human language** in a meaningful and useful way. It combines computational linguistics with machine learning and deep learning techniques. **Four Applications (1.5 marks – 0.375 each):** 1. **Machine Translation** – Google Translate, which converts text from one language to another. 2. **Sentiment Analysis** – Analyzing customer reviews to determine positive/negative opinion (used by Amazon, Flipkart). 3. **Chatbots & Virtual Assistants** – Siri, Alexa, customer support bots. 4. **Speech Recognition** – Converting spoken language into text (used in medical transcription). > [!TIP] > **Scoring Tip:** Examiners allot **1.5 marks for the definition** and **1.5 marks for the list**. Always number your applications clearly. Mentioning one real-world product per application elevates the answer from "good" to "excellent". --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > **What is tokenization? Explain with an example. Why is sub-word tokenization used in modern NLP?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Tokenization (1 mark):** Tokenization is the process of breaking a continuous stream of text into smaller units called **tokens** (words, sub-words, or characters). It is the **first critical preprocessing step** in any NLP pipeline. **Example (1 mark):** Input sentence: *"I love programming in Python."* Tokens: `["I", "love", "programming", "in", "Python", "."]` **Why Sub-word Tokenization (1 mark):** Sub-word tokenization (used by BERT, GPT) splits rare words into frequent sub-units. For example, "unhappiness" → `["un", "happiness"]`. This solves the **Out-Of-Vocabulary (OOV) problem**, reduces vocabulary size, and allows the model to handle morphologically rich languages (like Malayalam) effectively. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]** > **(a)** Explain the complete architecture of a Natural Language Processing system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each stage in detail. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With suitable examples, explain **TF-IDF** (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency). Compute the TF-IDF score for the word "**artificial**" in the following data: **(7 marks)** > > - Total documents in corpus $N = 500$ > - Document $d_1$ has 100 words > - The word "artificial" appears 5 times in $d_1$ > - "artificial" appears in 25 documents out of 500 #### **Model Solution for (a) – 7 marks:** The NLP architecture is organized as a **7-stage pipeline**: | Stage | Process | Description | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sentence Segmentation** | Splits a paragraph into individual sentences using punctuation. | 1 | | 2 | **Tokenization** | Breaks sentences into words or sub-words. | 1 | | 3 | **Stop-word Removal** | Filters out high-frequency, low-content words like "the", "a". | 1 | | 4 | **Stemming/Lemmatization** | Reduces words to base form: "running" → "run". | 1 | | 5 | **POS Tagging** | Labels tokens as noun, verb, adjective, etc. | 1 | | 6 | **NER** | Identifies entities: persons, locations, organizations. | 1 | | 7 | **Semantic Analysis** | Extracts intent, meaning, sentiment. | 1 | **Conclusion (included in any one stage's marks):** Each stage progressively enriches the textual representation, transforming unstructured human text into structured data suitable for ML models. #### **Model Solution for (b) – 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute Term Frequency. $$TF(\text{"artificial"}, d_1) = \frac{5}{100} = 0.05$$ *Explanation:* The word "artificial" occurs 5 times in a document of 100 words, so its normalized frequency is 0.05. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify Document Frequency. $$DF(\text{"artificial"}) = 25$$ *Explanation:* The word appears in 25 out of 500 documents in the corpus. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute Inverse Document Frequency (with smoothing). $$IDF(\text{"artificial"}, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{1 + DF}\right) = \log\left(\frac{500}{1 + 25}\right) = \log\left(\frac{500}{26}\right)$$ $$IDF = \log(19.2308) \approx 2.956$$ *Explanation:* The log function (typically base 10 or natural log) dampens the IDF value. A higher IDF indicates the word is more discriminative. **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute TF-IDF Score. $$TF\text{-}IDF(\text{"artificial"}, d_1, D) = 0.05 \times 2.956 \approx 0.1478$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Interpretation. Since "artificial" appears in only 25 out of 500 documents (5% of the corpus), it is a **rare, topic-specific term**. Its high IDF (~2.96) reflects its discriminative power, making it an important keyword for $d_1$. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 – Alternative to Question A)** > **(a)** Compare and contrast **Bag of Words (BoW)** and **TF-IDF** representations. Discuss their limitations. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **Word2Vec** approach in NLP. Describe the **CBOW** and **Skip-gram** architectures with diagrams. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a) – 7 marks:** | Aspect | **Bag of Words (BoW)** | **TF-IDF** | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | **Definition** | Counts word occurrences in a document. | Weights words by TF × IDF, penalizing common words. | 1 | | **Vector** | Raw integer counts $[c_1, c_2, \dots, c_V]$. | Weighted real values $[w_1, w_2, \dots, w_V]$. | 1 | | **Common words** | Treated as important (high count). | Suppressed (low IDF). | 1 | | **Sparsity** | Very high ($\vert \mathcal{V} \vert$ can be 100,000+). | Same sparsity, but values are more meaningful. | 1 | | **Semantic understanding** | None — "good" and "excellent" are different. | None — same limitation. | 1 | | **Order information** | Lost entirely. | Lost entirely. | 1 | | **Use case** | Baseline text classification, Naive Bayes. | Search engines, information retrieval. | 1 | #### **Model Solution for (b) – 7 marks:** **Word2Vec (3 marks):** Word2Vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) is a **shallow neural network** that learns dense, low-dimensional vector representations of words such that semantically similar words are mapped to nearby points in the embedding space. It is based on the **distributional hypothesis** — words with similar contexts have similar meanings. **CBOW Architecture (2 marks):** In **Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW)**, the model predicts the **target (center) word** from a window of surrounding **context words**. Example: For the sentence "The cat sat on the mat" with window size 2, given context "The cat on the", the model predicts "sat". **Skip-gram Architecture (2 marks):** In **Skip-gram**, the operation is reversed — the model predicts the **surrounding context words** given a single **target (center) word**. Example: Given the target word "sat", the model predicts "The", "cat", "on", "the". | Aspect | CBOW | Skip-gram | |---|---|---| | **Speed** | Faster to train | Slower | | **Quality on rare words** | Worse | Better | | **Best for** | Large corpora | Small corpora, rare words | **Famous Result (Bonus 0 marks – for impressing examiner):** Word2Vec famously produces: $$\vec{king} - \vec{man} + \vec{woman} \approx \vec{queen}$$ This linear algebra works because the embedding space captures semantic relationships geometrically. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Diagram is mandatory (–2 marks if missing):** For 7-mark questions on CBOW/Skip-gram, you MUST draw the architecture diagram. A textual description alone is insufficient. > 2. **Don't confuse stemming with lemmatization (–1 mark):** Stemming is crude rule-based chopping; lemmatization uses a vocabulary and morphological analysis. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **TF-IDF base of logarithm (–0.5 mark):** State explicitly whether you are using $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$. Different bases give different numerical values. > 4. **Stop-word removal is not always good (–1 mark):** Always mention the exception case (negation handling, sentiment preservation). > 5. **BoW ≠ TF-IDF (–0.5 mark):** Many students write identical answers for both. The KEY difference is IDF weighting of common words. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 – Module 4** - [ ] **NLP Definition** = AI + Linguistics + ML, focused on understanding and generating human language. - [ ] **The 4 challenges of NLP** = Ambiguity, Variability, Sparsity, Context Dependency. - [ ] **The 7-stage NLP pipeline** = Segmentation → Tokenization → Stop-word Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → POS Tagging → NER → Semantic Analysis. - [ ] **Stemming** chops suffixes crudely (Porter Stemmer); **Lemmatization** uses dictionary + morphology (WordNet). - [ ] **Bag of Words** = sparse count vector, ignores word order and semantics. - [ ] **TF-IDF** = $TF \times IDF$, where $IDF = \log\left(\frac{N}{1+df}\right)$ — weighs words by discriminative importance. - [ ] **Word Embeddings** = dense vectors in $\mathbb{R}^d$ ($d = 50$ to $300$) that capture semantic meaning. - [ ] **Word2Vec** has two flavors: **CBOW** (context → target) and **Skip-gram** (target → context). - [ ] **Distributional Hypothesis** = "A word is known by the company it keeps" (Firth, 1957). - [ ] **Cosine Similarity** = $\frac{\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}}{\vert \vec{A} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{B} \vert}$, range $[-1, 1]$, higher means more similar. - [ ] **Evolution of NLP** = Rule-Based → Statistical (HMM, CRF) → Neural (RNN, LSTM) → Transformer (BERT, GPT). - [ ] **Transformer's core equation** = $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$. - [ ] **Top 6 NLP applications** = Machine Translation, Sentiment Analysis, Chatbots, Speech Recognition, Information Retrieval, Named Entity Recognition. - [ ] **KTU Formula Sheet** memorized: TF, IDF, TF-IDF, Cosine Similarity, Perplexity, BLEU, Edit Distance. - [ ] **Real-world deployment** = Google Search (TF-IDF), Google Translate (Transformer), Alexa (ASR + NLU), Grammarly (POS + grammar), spam filters (Naive Bayes + BoW). > [!TIP] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** When asked an NLP architecture question, ALWAYS structure your answer as: (1) Definition, (2) Block diagram, (3) Stage-by-stage explanation, (4) Real-world application, (5) Limitations. This 5-part structure guarantees a complete answer and maximizes marks under KTU's revised Bloom's Taxonomy evaluation rubric.

topics.title: Ethical Considerations in AI and ML: Bias, Fairness, Explainability, Societal Impact
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethical Considerations in AI and ML: Bias, Fairness, Explainability, Societal Impact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Ethics" in the context of AI/ML?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **AI Ethics** is a branch of applied ethics that systematically studies the moral behaviour, design principles, deployment constraints, and societal consequences of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning systems. It operationalises abstract human values (fairness, accountability, transparency, privacy, beneficence, non-maleficence) into measurable engineering specifications, algorithmic constraints, regulatory frameworks, and auditable governance pipelines. In the KTU 2024 outcome-based curriculum, AI Ethics is positioned as a *socio-technical control system* that ensures ML pipelines do not amplify historical inequities, do not violate individual autonomy, and remain interpretable to both domain experts and affected end-users.

In simpler terms, AI Ethics is the "**moral compass**" wired into intelligent systems. Just as a civil engineer must ensure a bridge does not collapse and does not harm pedestrians, an ML engineer must ensure that a credit-scoring model does not silently reject applicants based on race, gender, caste, or religion. Ethics in AI is **not a soft, philosophical add-on** — under the KTU 2024 scheme, it is treated as a *first-class engineering requirement* equivalent to accuracy, latency, or memory.

## 1.2 The Four Pillars of AI Ethics (Module 4 Focus)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PECCT413 Module 4) explicitly identifies **four interlocking pillars** that any responsible AI/ML system must address:

| Pillar | One-Line Definition | Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Bias** | Systematic errors in data or model that produce skewed, prejudiced outcomes | The *noise / error term* in a learning system |
| **Fairness** | The mathematical and procedural guarantee that model decisions do not unjustly discriminate across protected groups | The *loss function constraint* |
| **Explainability (XAI)** | The ability of a model to communicate *why* it produced a particular prediction in human-understandable terms | The *system documentation* |
| **Societal Impact** | The aggregate effect of AI deployment on employment, privacy, autonomy, democratic processes, and human rights | The *environmental & social impact assessment* |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Self-Driving Car" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump — Think of an AI system as an autonomous vehicle navigating society:**
> - **Bias** is the misaligned sensor (a camera that under-detects dark-skinned pedestrians) — the car *thinks* the road is clear when it is not.
> - **Fairness** is the steering algorithm that guarantees the car stops equally reliably for *every* pedestrian, regardless of age, gender, or clothing.
> - **Explainability** is the black-box flight recorder — when an accident occurs, investigators must reconstruct *why* the car braked or failed to brake.
> - **Societal Impact** is the broader effect on traffic, insurance premiums, employment of taxi drivers, urban planning, and pollution.
>
> Just as we would never certify a self-driving car without crash tests, pedestrian-fairness audits, and event-data recorders, the KTU 2024 scheme requires ML systems to pass analogous *algorithmic impact assessments* before deployment.

## 1.4 The KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Mapping

| Course Outcome (CO) | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Linkage to This Module |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** — Identify ethical, societal, and legal implications of AI/ML solutions | Apply / Analyse | Direct — this entire module |
| **CO5** — Recommend appropriate governance frameworks for responsible AI deployment | Evaluate | Indirect — frameworks are part of broader course |
| **PO12** — Life-long learning in the context of AI ethics | Understand | Indirect — reading contemporary case law |

## 1.5 Why This Module Matters in 2024–2025 (Industry Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As of 2024, the **EU AI Act** (entered into force August 2024) classifies AI systems into *unacceptable, high, limited, and minimal risk* tiers. High-risk systems (e.g., recruitment AI, credit scoring, biometric identification) **must** demonstrate bias audits, fairness metrics, explainability reports, and human oversight. The KTU 2024 syllabus has been deliberately updated to align with this regulatory shift, making this module a *board-examination high-weightage area*.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PILLAR 1 — BIAS IN AI/ML

### 2.1.1 Formal Definition

> **Bias** in ML is a *systematic deviation* of a model's predictions, or of the data it is trained on, from the *true underlying distribution* in a way that *disadvantageously affects* specific subgroups of the population.

Mathematically, bias is any component of model error that is **correlated with a sensitive attribute** $A$ (e.g., gender, caste, religion, age, disability status).

### 2.1.2 The Taxonomy of Bias (Suresh & Guttag, 2021 framework adopted by KTU 2024)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognises **seven distinct stages** at which bias can enter an ML pipeline:

1. **Historical Bias** — Exists in the world *before* any data is collected. Example: Historically, women were denied credit; the past data *correctly* records this discrimination, and the model learns it.
2. **Representation Bias** — Occurs when the *sampling process* under-represents certain groups. Example: Facial recognition datasets historically over-represented lighter-skinned males.
3. **Measurement Bias** — Arises when *proxies* for the true target variable are systematically noisier for some groups. Example: Arrest records as a proxy for "crime" — over-policed neighbourhoods generate more arrests *per actual crime*.
4. **Aggregation Bias** — Occurs when a *single model* is fit to diverse sub-populations whose true relationships differ. Example: A one-size-fits-all diabetes model that under-performs for Asian populations.
5. **Learning Bias** — Introduced by modelling choices — loss functions, regularisation, hyper-parameters — that amplify disparities. Example: Asymmetric loss where false negatives for one group are penalised less.
6. **Evaluation Bias** — Occurs when benchmark datasets do not represent the deployment population. Example: Evaluating a Hindi-speech recogniser only on Tamil speakers.
7. **Deployment Bias** — Arises when a model is used *for a purpose different* from its design intent. Example: Using a criminal-risk predictor for sentencing instead of pre-trial bail.

### 2.1.3 Why Bias is a *Performance* Issue, Not Just a *Social* Issue

> [!NOTE]
> Bias is not merely an ethical concern — it is a *statistical generalisation failure*. A biased model fails the standard ML assumption of **Independent and Identically Distributed (i.i.d.)** training and test data, because the deployment population differs systematically from the training distribution along protected attributes.

---

## 2.2 PILLAR 2 — FAIRNESS

### 2.2.1 Formal Definition

> **Fairness** in ML is the property that a model's decisions are *independent* of (or appropriately *conditioned* on) sensitive attributes $A$, in a mathematically and ethically defensible way.

### 2.2.2 Three Families of Fairness Notions

| Family | Core Idea | Mathematical Condition | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Group Fairness** | Outcomes should be *statistically equal* across demographic groups | Demographic Parity, Equalised Odds | Can hide intra-group inequality |
| **Individual Fairness** | *Similar individuals* should receive *similar outcomes* | $\| \hat{Y}_i - \hat{Y}_j \| \leq L \cdot d(x_i, x_j)$ for similar $x_i, x_j$ | Requires a *task-specific similarity metric* — hard to define |
| **Counterfactual Fairness** | The outcome should be the *same* in a counterfactual world where $A$ were flipped | $\hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a} = \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'}$ for all $a, a'$ | Requires a causal model of the world |

### 2.2.3 The Impossibility Theorem (Chouldechova, 2017; Kleinberg et al., 2016)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examination Favourite Result:**
> Under standard assumptions, it is **mathematically impossible** to simultaneously satisfy all three of the following fairness criteria *unless* the base rates $P(Y=1 \mid A=a)$ are exactly equal across groups OR the predictor is *perfect*:
> 1. **Calibration** within groups
> 2. **Equalised Odds** (equal TPR *and* FPR across groups)
> 3. **Predictive Parity** (equal PPV across groups)
>
> This means engineers must make an **explicit normative choice** about *which* fairness notion to optimise. There is no "default fair" model.

### 2.2.4 Fairness Through Unawareness vs. Fairness Through Awareness

- **Fairness Through Unawareness (FTU)**: Simply *remove* the sensitive attribute $A$ from the feature set. **This does NOT work** because of *redundant encodings* (e.g., zip code correlates with race).
- **Fairness Through Awareness (FTA)**: Define a *similarity metric* over individuals and enforce Lipschitz-style outcome constraints.

---

## 2.3 PILLAR 3 — EXPLAINABILITY (XAI)

### 2.3.1 Formal Definition

> **Explainability** is the degree to which a human can *understand and reason about* the cause of a model's output. The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes between:
> - **Interpretability** — the model is *inherently* transparent (e.g., linear regression, decision trees of bounded depth).
> - **Explainability (Post-hoc)** — a separate procedure generates a *human-understandable explanation* for an otherwise opaque model (e.g., SHAP applied to a deep network).

### 2.3.2 The Global vs. Local Explanation Duality

| Scope | Question Answered | Example Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **Global** | "How does the model behave *overall*?" | Partial Dependence Plots (PDP), Accumulated Local Effects (ALE), Feature Importance ranking |
| **Local** | "Why did the model predict *this specific instance* this way?" | LIME, SHAP, Counterfactual Explanations, Anchors |

### 2.3.3 Taxonomy of XAI Methods (Adadi & Berrada, 2018; adopted by KTU)

```
XAI
├── Intrinsic (Model-Specific)
│   ├── Linear / Logistic Regression (coefficients)
│   ├── Decision Trees / Rule Lists
│   ├── Attention Mechanisms (transformers)
│   └── Generalised Additive Models (GAMs)
└── Post-hoc (Model-Agnostic)
    ├── Local Surrogates → LIME
    ├── Shapley Value Based → SHAP (KernelSHAP, TreeSHAP, DeepSHAP)
    ├── Counterfactual → DiCE, Wachter et al.
    └── Example-Based → Prototypes, Criticisms, Influence Functions
```

### 2.3.4 The SHAP Value — Mathematical Essence

For a model $f$ and an instance $x$, the **Shapley value** of feature $i$ is the *average marginal contribution* of feature $i$ across all possible feature coalitions:

$$\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \cdot (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert!} \left[ f_{S \cup \{i\}}(x_{S \cup \{i\}}) - f_S(x_S) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the gold standard:** The Shapley value is the *unique* attribution method that satisfies **Efficiency** (sum of attributions = total prediction), **Symmetry** (equally-contributing features get equal credit), **Dummy** (non-contributing features get zero), and **Linearity** (attributions add across models).
