This is why SHAP appears in the KTU 2024 question bank.

---

## 2.4 PILLAR 4 — SOCIETAL IMPACT

### 2.4.1 Formal Definition

> **Societal Impact** of AI is the *aggregate, second-order effect* of AI/ML deployment on human institutions, labour markets, civil liberties, and the distribution of power.

### 2.4.2 The Five Domains of Societal Impact (KTU 2024 Module 4.4)

1. **Labour & Employment** — Task automation, skill displacement, gig-economy algorithmic management.
2. **Privacy & Surveillance** — Facial recognition, predictive policing, data-broker ecosystems.
3. **Autonomy & Human Agency** — Recommendation algorithms shaping attention, Nudger-AI, dark patterns.
4. **Democracy & Information Integrity** — Deepfakes, generative misinformation, micro-targeted political advertising.
5. **Environmental Sustainability** — Energy cost of large-scale model training, e-waste from GPU infrastructure.

### 2.4.3 The KTU High-Yield Governance Frameworks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 board examiners expect students to be able to *name, compare, and contrast* at least three governance frameworks:

| Framework | Origin | Key Principle | Binding Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EU AI Act (2024)** | European Union | Risk-tiered regulation (unacceptable / high / limited / minimal) | Legally binding |
| **OECD AI Principles (2019, updated 2024)** | OECD | Inclusive growth, human-centred values, transparency, robustness | Voluntary, adopted by 46+ countries |
| **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design (EAD)** | IEEE Global Initiative | Prioritise human well-being, accountability, transparency | Voluntary, professional standards |
| **India's National Strategy for AI (NSAI, 2018)** | NITI Aayog | #AIforAll, responsible AI for social inclusion | Policy guidance |
| **UNESCO Recommendation on Ethics of AI (2021)** | UNESCO | Human dignity, environmental flourishing, diversity | Non-binding international standard |

### 2.4.4 The Asilomar AI Principles (2017) — 23 Guidelines

Common board-exam reference: 23 principles grouped into **Research Issues**, **Ethics & Values**, and **Longer-term Issues**. Students should memorise at least: *Research Culture, Safety, Transparency, Privacy, Liberty & Privacy, Shared Benefit, Human Control, AI Race*.

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA / METRIC SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important table in this module.** Memorise every formula — these are direct 3-mark and 14-mark question templates.

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Range / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Pr(\hat{Y}=1)$ | Selection Rate | $\frac{TP + FP}{N}$ | Fraction predicted positive |
| $DP$ | Demographic Parity Difference | $\Pr(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0) - \Pr(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)$ | $0$ = perfectly fair |
| $DPD$ | Disparate Impact Ratio | $\frac{\Pr(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=0)}{\Pr(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=1)}$ | $[0.8, 1.25]$ = 4/5 rule (US EEOC) |
| $EOD$ | Equal Opportunity Difference | $TPR_{A=0} - TPR_{A=1}$ | $0$ = equal TPR |
| $AOD$ | Average Odds Difference | $\tfrac{1}{2}\left[ (FPR_{A=0} - FPR_{A=1}) + (TPR_{A=0} - TPR_{A=1}) \right]$ | $0$ = equalised odds |
| $TI$ | Theil Index | $T = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{b_i}{\bar{b}} \ln \frac{b_i}{\bar{b}}$, where $b_i = \hat{y}_i - y_i$ | $0$ = perfect fairness |
| $\phi_i$ | Shapley Value (SHAP) | $\sum_{S \subseteq F\setminus\{i\}} w(S) \left[ f_{S\cup\{i\}} - f_S \right]$ | Sum of $\phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f]$ |
| $L_{\text{stab}}$ | Local Stability (LIME) | $\max_{x' \in B_\epsilon(x)} \vert f(x') - g(x') \vert$ | Small = stable explanation |
| $R^2_{\text{fair}}$ | Counterfactual Fairness Residual | $\mathbb{E}\left[ \left( \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a} - \hat{Y}_{A \leftarrow a'} \right)^2 \right]$ | $0$ = counterfactually fair |

**Note on notation:** Vertical bars in absolute-value / cardinality expressions above have been written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to remain compatible with markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How Ethics is Operationalised |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | FDA's 2024 guidance mandates bias audits for any AI/ML-enabled medical device |
| **Finance** | RBI (India) Master Direction on Digital Lending requires model explainability for credit decisions |
| **Recruitment** | NYC Local Law 144 (2023) requires *annual bias audits* of automated employment decision tools |
| **Criminal Justice** | Wisconsin v. Loomis (2016) requires COMPAS risk scores to be explainable to defendants |
| **Generative AI** | EU AI Act 2024 requires *labelling* of all AI-generated content and disclosure of training data provenance |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section moves from conceptual exposition to *exhaustive analytical work*. The following derivations are **board-exam ready** and explicitly address the KTU 2024 outcome-based expectation that students can *derive, justify, and compare* fairness and explainability metrics.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Demographic Parity Constraint from First Principles

We start with the formal claim: *a model satisfies Demographic Parity (DP) with respect to a binary protected attribute $A \in \{0, 1\}$ if and only if the predicted outcome $\hat{Y}$ is statistically independent of $A$.*

**Step 1: Write the formal independence statement.**

> The model $\hat{Y}$ satisfies demographic parity if and only if $\hat{Y} \perp A$, which by the definition of statistical independence is equivalent to:

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)$$

**Step 2: Expand the right-hand side using Bayes' Rule and marginalisation.**

> We can rewrite the conditional probability by marginalising over the input space $\mathcal{X}$:

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = a) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid X = x) \cdot \Pr(X = x \mid A = a) \, dx$$

**Step 3: Substitute and equate.**

> Setting $a = 0$ and $a = 1$ and equating the two integrals gives the demographic parity constraint. The **Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)** measures *how far* a model is from this ideal:

$$DPD \;=\; \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) \;-\; \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)$$

**Step 4: Compute empirically from a confusion-matrix-style accounting.**

> Partition the dataset into group $A = 0$ and $A = 1$. For each group, count true positives ($TP_a$), false positives ($FP_a$), and total ($N_a$). Then:

$$DPD \;=\; \frac{TP_0 + FP_0}{N_0} \;-\; \frac{TP_1 + FP_1}{N_1}$$

**Step 5: Decision rule.**

> A model is *accepted as demographic-parity-fair* under the commonly used 4/5 rule (US Equal Employment Opportunity Commission) if and only if:

$$\frac{0.8}{\text{ratio}} \;\le\; \frac{\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0)}{\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)} \;\le\; 1.25 \quad \text{(equivalently } DPD \text{ within } \pm 0.1 \text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common student error (board exam trap):** Demographic Parity does *not* require equal accuracy across groups. It only constrains the *positive prediction rate*. A model that always predicts $\hat{Y} = 1$ trivially satisfies DP but is useless. This is why DP is called a *weak* fairness criterion.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Equalised Odds Decomposition

**Step 1: Define the goal.**

> Equalised Odds requires the model to have **equal true positive rates (TPR)** *and* **equal false positive rates (FPR)** across groups, *conditional* on the true label $Y$. Formally:

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 0) = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 1) \quad \forall \, y \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 2: Decompose into two sub-conditions.**

> Setting $y = 1$ gives the **Equal Opportunity** condition (TPR equality). Setting $y = 0$ gives the FPR-equality condition. Equalised Odds = both simultaneously.

**Step 3: Define the Average Odds Difference metric.**

> The *Average Odds Difference* (AOD) collapses both sub-conditions into a single scalar that is $0$ iff Equalised Odds holds:

$$AOD \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \Big[\, (FPR_{A=0} - FPR_{A=1}) \;+\; (TPR_{A=0} - TPR_{A=1}) \, \Big]$$

**Step 4: Express TPR and FPR in confusion-matrix form.**

$$TPR_a = \frac{TP_a}{TP_a + FN_a} \qquad FPR_a = \frac{FP_a}{FP_a + TN_a}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the AOD formula.**

> Combining the two:

$$AOD \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \left[ \left( \frac{FP_0}{FP_0 + TN_0} - \frac{FP_1}{FP_1 + TN_1} \right) + \left( \frac{TP_0}{TP_0 + FN_0} - \frac{TP_1}{TP_1 + FN_1} \right) \right]$$

**Step 6: Interpret the result.**

> An $AOD$ value of $0$ means the model has *equal error rates* across groups. Positive $AOD$ means the model *favours* group $A=0$ (higher TPR, higher FPR). Negative $AOD$ indicates *disadvantage* to group $A=0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Equalised Odds is preferred over Demographic Parity in high-stakes domains** (e.g., medical diagnosis, criminal justice): DP can be trivially satisfied by a useless model, whereas Equalised Odds forces the model to be *equally good* for qualified members of every group.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — SHAP (Shapley Value) for a Toy 3-Feature Model

This derivation follows the KTU 2024 board-exam pattern of "explain SHAP on a small example."

**Step 1: Set up the problem.**

> Consider a model $f$ that takes three binary features $F = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ and outputs a scalar. We want to compute the Shapley value $\phi_1$ for feature $x_1$.

**Step 2: Enumerate all $2^{|F|-1} = 4$ subsets of $F \setminus \{x_1\}$.**

> The four subsets are $S_0 = \emptyset$, $S_1 = \{x_2\}$, $S_2 = \{x_3\}$, $S_3 = \{x_2, x_3\}$.

**Step 3: Compute marginal contributions $\Delta_i(S) = f(S \cup \{x_1\}) - f(S)$ for each subset.**

> Let us denote these as $\Delta_0, \Delta_1, \Delta_2, \Delta_3$. The Shapley value is the *weighted sum* of these four contributions.

**Step 4: Compute the Shapley weight $w(S)$.**

> For a coalition of size $\vert S \vert = s$ in a total of $\vert F \vert = 3$ features, the Shapley weight is the *number of orderings* in which the features of $S$ appear before $x_1$ multiplied by the number after, divided by total orderings:

$$w(S) \;=\; \frac{s! \cdot (3 - s - 1)!}{3!} \;=\; \frac{s! \cdot (2 - s)!}{6}$$

**Step 5: Tabulate the weights.**

> For $s = 0, 1, 2$, the weights are:

$$w(\emptyset) = \frac{0! \cdot 2!}{6} = \frac{1}{3}, \quad w(\{x_2\}) = \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{6} = \frac{1}{6}, \quad w(\{x_3\}) = \frac{1! \cdot 1!}{6} = \frac{1}{6}, \quad w(\{x_2,x_3\}) = \frac{2! \cdot 0!}{6} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

> The weights sum to $\tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{3} = 1$ ✓

**Step 6: Form the Shapley value.**

$$\phi_1 \;=\; \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{x_1\}} w(S) \cdot \Delta(S) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3}\Delta_0 + \tfrac{1}{6}\Delta_1 + \tfrac{1}{6}\Delta_2 + \tfrac{1}{3}\Delta_3$$

> This is the *unique* attribution satisfying Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, and Linearity — the four Shapley axioms.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — LIME's Local Fidelity Objective

**Step 1: Define the explainer model $g$.**

> LIME explains a complex model $f$ at a point $x$ by fitting a *simple* interpretable model $g \in G$ (typically a sparse linear model) over *perturbed samples* $z$ drawn from a neighbourhood $B_\epsilon(x)$.

**Step 2: Write the LIME objective.**

$$\xi(x) \;=\; \arg\min_{g \in G} \; \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) \;+\; \Omega(g)$$

where $\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x)$ measures how *unfaithful* $g$ is to $f$ in the neighbourhood, and $\Omega(g)$ penalises model complexity.

**Step 3: Expand the loss function.**

> The standard LIME loss is a *weighted squared error* over $N$ perturbed samples:

$$\mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) \;=\; \sum_{z \in \mathcal{Z}} \pi_x(z) \cdot \left[ f(z) - g(z) \right]^2$$

**Step 4: Define the proximity weight $\pi_x(z)$.**

> Typically a *cosine or Euclidean* kernel, e.g.:

$$\pi_x(z) \;=\; \exp\left( -\frac{D(x, z)^2}{\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 5: Solve the constrained optimisation.**

> For a fixed complexity budget $\Omega(g) \le K$ (e.g., at most $K$ non-zero coefficients), the minimiser $g^\star$ is obtained by *regularised least squares* (Lasso for sparsity):

$$g^\star \;=\; \arg\min_{g} \; \sum_{z} \pi_x(z) \left[ f(z) - g(z) \right]^2 \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \vert\vert g \vert\vert_0 \le K$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board exam tip:** LIME explanations are *local* — they are valid only in the neighbourhood of $x$. Outside that neighbourhood, the explanation $g$ may be wildly inaccurate. This is the **local fidelity** property and is one of LIME's known weaknesses.

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 5 — The Bias-Variance-Fairness Decomposition (Extension of Bias-Variance Trade-off)

**Step 1: Write the standard squared-error decomposition.**

> For any predictor $\hat{Y} = f(X)$ evaluated against true target $Y$, the expected squared error decomposes as:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (Y - \hat{Y})^2 \right] = \underbrace{\left( \mathbb{E}[\hat{Y}] - Y \right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[ \left( \hat{Y} - \mathbb{E}[\hat{Y}] \right)^2 \right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

**Step 2: Add the group-conditional expectation.**

> Condition on the protected attribute $A$ and take a *weighted expectation* across groups with weights $w_a = \Pr(A = a)$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (Y - \hat{Y})^2 \right] = \sum_{a} w_a \cdot \mathbb{E}\left[ (Y - \hat{Y})^2 \mid A = a \right]$$

**Step 3: Subtract the maximum and minimum group errors.**

> Define the *fairness gap* as the disparity between the worst-served and best-served groups:

$$FG = \max_{a} \mathbb{E}\left[ (Y - \hat{Y})^2 \mid A = a \right] - \min_{a} \mathbb{E}\left[ (Y - \hat{Y})^2 \mid A = a \right]$$

**Step 4: Note the engineering trade-off.**

> Empirically, *decreasing* the overall error by increasing model capacity often *increases* $FG$ — the model fits the majority group better and the minority group worse. This is the **bias-variance-fairness triangle**: improving any two comes at the cost of the third.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in the KTU exam:** Examiners often ask "Can we have a model that is both highly accurate and perfectly fair?" The correct answer invokes this trade-off: *no*, unless the underlying data-generating process is itself group-symmetric.

---

## 3.6 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX — Bias vs. Fairness vs. Explainability vs. Impact

| Dimension | Bias | Fairness | Explainability | Societal Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **What it measures** | Systematic error correlated with $A$ | Group-/individual-level outcome equality | Human-understandability of model behaviour | Aggregate human consequence |
| **Measured by** | DPD, DI, AOD | DP, EO, Theil Index | SHAP, LIME fidelity, user studies | Economic, environmental, civil-liberties audits |
| **Mitigated by** | Resampling, reweighting, data augmentation | Constrained optimisation, adversarial debiasing | Surrogate models, attention, rule extraction | Regulation, governance, public participation |
| **Inherent trade-off** | Accuracy ↓ | Accuracy ↓ | Accuracy ↓ | Innovation ↓ |
| **Failure mode** | Silent, statistical | Silent, statistical | Misleading explanations | Slow, diffuse |
| **Regulatory hook** | EU AI Act Art. 10 | EU AI Act Art. 10; NYC LL 144 | EU AI Act Art. 13 (right to explanation) | EU AI Act Art. 1, OECD Principles |
| **Board-exam weightage** | Very High | Very High | High | High |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks each with internal choice. All questions are aligned to **Course Outcome CO4** and **CO5** and follow the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate)** cognitive levels.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Define algorithmic bias. List and briefly explain any four types of biases that can occur in an AI/ML system.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition (1 mark):** Algorithmic bias is a systematic and repeatable error in a computer system that creates unfair outcomes, such as privileging one group of users over another. It arises when the model's predictions or decisions systematically disadvantage individuals belonging to certain demographic groups defined by protected attributes such as race, gender, age, caste, religion, or disability status.

**Four types (0.5 marks each):**

1. **Historical Bias** — Pre-existing societal inequities encoded in the training data. Example: Past hiring data reflecting gender discrimination.

2. **Representation Bias** — Under-representation of certain subgroups in the dataset. Example: Facial recognition systems performing poorly on darker-skinned females due to over-representation of lighter-skinned males in training data.

3. **Measurement Bias** — Systematic differences in how the target variable is measured across groups. Example: Using arrest records as a proxy for "crime," which over-represents heavily-policed neighbourhoods.

4. **Deployment Bias** — Using a model for a purpose different from its original design intent. Example: Deploying a generic risk-prediction model in a court of law for sentencing.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**What is Explainable AI (XAI)? Compare SHAP and LIME in terms of their approach to generating explanations.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition (1 mark):** Explainable AI (XAI) refers to a set of methods and techniques that allow human users to understand and trust the outputs of machine learning models by providing interpretable justifications for predictions.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | SHAP | LIME |
|---|---|---|
| **Theoretical basis** | Cooperative game theory (Shapley values) | Local linear approximation (surrogate models) |
| **Scope** | Both global and local | Strictly local |
| **Uniqueness** | Unique attribution satisfying four axioms (Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, Linearity) | One of many possible linear fits — not unique |
| **Computational cost** | Exponential in worst case (mitigated by KernelSHAP, TreeSHAP) | Cheaper, scales linearly with samples |
| **Stability** | Stable across runs for the same model | Can vary across runs due to random perturbation sampling |
| **Output** | Shapley values $\phi_i$ per feature | Sparse linear coefficients |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> *(1) Do NOT describe "ethics" in vague philosophical terms — the 2024 scheme expects operational definitions with metrics, equations, and engineering mitigation strategies. (2) When discussing fairness, do NOT equate it with "removing the sensitive attribute" — this is Fairness Through Unawareness and demonstrably fails due to redundant encodings. (3) When discussing XAI, do NOT confuse global vs. local explanations — board examiners will deduct 2–3 marks for this error. (4) Always cite at least one real-world case (COMPAS, Amazon hiring AI, Apple Card, EU AI Act) to demonstrate applied understanding.*

---

### **PART B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3 (a).** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**With suitable mathematical formulation, explain the concept of Demographic Parity and Equalised Odds as fairness criteria in machine learning. Discuss the Chouldechova impossibility result and its practical implications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Demographic Parity (3 marks)**

Demographic Parity (also called *Statistical Parity*) requires that the predicted outcome $\hat{Y}$ is *statistically independent* of the protected attribute $A$. Formally, the model must satisfy:

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)$$

This is measured empirically using the **Demographic Parity Difference (DPD)**:

$$DPD = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0) - \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)$$

A value of $DPD = 0$ indicates perfect demographic parity. In practice, the **4/5 rule** (US EEOC) accepts:

$$\frac{\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 0)}{\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid A = 1)} \in [0.8, 1.25]$$

**Limitation:** Demographic Parity does not account for the *true qualification* of candidates. It can be trivially satisfied by a random classifier, which is useless.

**Step 2: Equalised Odds (2 marks)**

Equalised Odds, introduced by Hardt et al. (2016), requires *equal error rates* across groups, conditional on the true label:

$$\Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 0) = \Pr(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = y, A = 1) \quad \forall \, y \in \{0, 1\}$$

This decomposes into two conditions: equal **True Positive Rate** (Equal Opportunity) and equal **False Positive Rate**. The empirical metric is the **Average Odds Difference (AOD)**:

$$AOD = \tfrac{1}{2} \big[ (FPR_{A=0} - FPR_{A=1}) + (TPR_{A=0} - TPR_{A=1}) \big]$$

A value of $AOD = 0$ indicates perfect Equalised Odds.

**Step 3: The Impossibility Result (2 marks)**

Chouldechova (2017) and Kleinberg et al. (2016) independently proved that under non-trivial conditions (when base rates $P(Y=1 \mid A=0) \neq P(Y=1 \mid A=1)$), it is mathematically impossible to simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Calibration within groups** — predicted probability matches empirical frequency for each group.
2. **Equalised Odds** — equal TPR and FPR across groups.
3. **Predictive Parity** — equal Positive Predictive Value (PPV) across groups.

**Practical implications:** Engineers must make an *explicit ethical choice* about which fairness notion to prioritise. There is no "default fair" model. The choice is normative, not purely technical.

---

#### **Q3 (b).** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Explain the EU AI Act 2024 risk-based classification framework. Describe with examples how the four risk tiers (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal) regulate AI deployment. Discuss two real-world case studies where AI systems were found to violate ethical principles. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: EU AI Act 2024 Risk Tiers (3 marks)**

The EU AI Act, which entered into force on 1 August 2024, classifies AI systems into four risk tiers:

1. **Unacceptable Risk (Banned)** — Systems posing a clear threat to safety, livelihoods, or rights. Examples: government social-scoring systems, real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with narrow exceptions), subliminal manipulation techniques.

2. **High Risk (Strictly Regulated)** — Systems used in critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential services, law enforcement, migration, and justice. Examples: recruitment AI tools, credit-scoring algorithms, medical-device AI, and AI used in sentencing. These must undergo conformity assessments, bias audits, transparency reports, human oversight, and high-quality datasets.

3. **Limited Risk (Transparency Obligations)** — Systems that interact with humans or generate synthetic content. Examples: chatbots must disclose they are AI, deepfakes must be labelled, and AI-generated text must be machine-readable.

4. **Minimal Risk (No Obligations)** — Systems posing negligible risk, such as spam filters, video-game AI, and standard recommendation engines. Voluntary codes of conduct are encouraged.

**Step 2: Real-World Case Study 1 — Amazon's Hiring AI (2 marks)**

In 2018, Reuters reported that Amazon had scrapped an internal AI recruiting tool because it systematically down-ranked resumes that contained the word "women's" (e.g., "women's chess club captain") and graduates of two all-women's colleges. The model had been trained on 10 years of historical resumes, which reflected the male-dominated tech industry. This is a textbook case of **historical bias** compounded by **measurement bias** (resume content as a proxy for job performance).

**Step 3: Real-World Case Study 2 — Apple Card Gender Bias (2 marks)**

In 2019, the Apple Card (issued by Goldman Sachs) was found to give男性 significantly higher credit limits than women, even when the women had *better* credit scores. The New York Department of Financial Services investigated and confirmed the algorithm exhibited **disparate impact** by gender. This is a case of **deployment bias** — the algorithm optimised profit maximisation without a fairness constraint, resulting in discriminatory outcomes.

---

### **PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

#### **Q4 (a).** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Derive the Shapley value for a three-feature classifier. Explain why SHAP is considered the gold standard for model explainability. Discuss the computational challenges and at least two efficient approximations (KernelSHAP and TreeSHAP). (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Set up the Shapley framework (2 marks)**

For a model $f$ with feature set $F = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ and an instance $x$, the Shapley value $\phi_i$ quantifies the marginal contribution of feature $x_i$ to the prediction $f(x)$ averaged over all feature coalitions. The general formula is:

$$\phi_i = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{i\}} \frac{\vert S \vert! \cdot (\vert F \vert - \vert S \vert - 1)!}{\vert F \vert!} \left[ f_{S \cup \{i\}}(x_{S \cup \{i\}}) - f_S(x_S) \right]$$

**Step 2: Enumerate the four coalitions (2 marks)**

For $\phi_1$, the four coalitions of $F \setminus \{x_1\} = \{x_2, x_3\}$ are: $\emptyset, \{x_2\}, \{x_3\}, \{x_2, x_3\}$.

The corresponding Shapley weights (computed in Section 3.3 of this module) are:

$$w(\emptyset) = \frac{1}{3}, \quad w(\{x_2\}) = \frac{1}{6}, \quad w(\{x_3\}) = \frac{1}{6}, \quad w(\{x_2, x_3\}) = \frac{1}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$\phi_1 = \frac{1}{3} \Delta_0 + \frac{1}{6} \Delta_1 + \frac{1}{6} \Delta_2 + \frac{1}{3} \Delta_3$$

where $\Delta(S) = f(S \cup \{x_1\}) - f(S)$.

**Step 3: Why SHAP is the gold standard (2 marks)**

SHAP is the *unique* attribution method satisfying four game-theoretic axioms:

1. **Efficiency:** $\sum_i \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ — attributions sum to the deviation from the mean.
2. **Symmetry:** Features with equal marginal contributions get equal $\phi_i$.
3. **Dummy:** A feature that does not change the prediction gets $\phi_i = 0$.
4. **Linearity:** Attributions are additive across model ensembles.

No other XAI method simultaneously satisfies all four.

**Step 4: Computational challenges and approximations (1 mark)**

- **Exact SHAP** is exponential in the number of features ($\mathcal{O}(2^{\vert F \vert})$ model evaluations).
- **KernelSHAP** approximates SHAP using weighted linear regression on sampled coalitions; complexity $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M^2)$ where $M$ is the number of sampled coalitions and $N$ the number of features.
- **TreeSHAP** exploits decision-tree structure for polynomial-time exact SHAP values; complexity $\mathcal{O}(T \cdot L \cdot D^2)$ where $T$ is the number of trees, $L$ the leaves, $D$ the depth.

---

#### **Q4 (b).** [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Discuss the societal impact of AI/ML across any four of the following domains: (i) Employment, (ii) Privacy, (iii) Algorithmic bias in criminal justice, (iv) Environmental cost of large-scale AI, (v) Generative AI and democracy. For each domain, provide one concrete example and one mitigation strategy. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Domain (i): Employment and Algorithmic Management (1.5 marks)**

**Example:** Uber and Lyft drivers in India and the US report being managed by opaque algorithms that determine ride allocation, route suggestion, and deactivation. A 2022 study found that drivers earn 30% less per hour than the platform advertises, partly because the algorithm assigns lower-paying rides to certain driver profiles.

**Mitigation:** Algorithmic transparency laws (e.g., the proposed EU Platform Work Directive 2024) that require ride-hailing platforms to disclose ranking criteria and provide human-appeal mechanisms.

**Domain (ii): Privacy and Mass Surveillance (1.5 marks)**

**Example:** Clearview AI scraped over 30 billion facial images from social media without consent and sold facial-recognition services to law enforcement. Multiple jurisdictions (Canada, Australia, EU) have ruled this illegal under GDPR and analogous laws.

**Mitigation:** Strict data-minimisation requirements under GDPR Article 5, mandatory data-protection impact assessments (DPIAs) for biometric systems, and consent-based data collection.

**Domain (iii): Algorithmic Bias in Criminal Justice (1.5 marks)**

**Example:** The COMPAS recidivism-prediction tool, used in US courts, was shown in the 2016 ProPublica investigation to have a *false positive rate* of 45% for Black defendants versus 23% for white defendants, despite similar overall accuracy. This violates Equalised Odds.

**Mitigation:** Mandate AOD audits before deployment, require human-in-the-loop judicial review, and disclose model card and datasheet information to defendants as required by *Wisconsin v. Loomis*.

**Domain (iv): Environmental Cost of Large-Scale AI (1.5 marks)**

**Example:** Training GPT-3 (175B parameters) was estimated to consume ~1,287 MWh of electricity and emit ~552 tonnes of CO₂-equivalent, roughly equivalent to 120 transatlantic flights. Training a single large model can have a carbon footprint comparable to the *lifetime emissions of five average cars*.

**Mitigation:** Use of renewable-powered data centres, model-distillation and pruning to reduce parameter counts, and reporting of training carbon costs in model cards (following Strubell et al., 2019).

**Domain (v): Generative AI and Democracy (1.5 marks)** *(optional fifth domain)*

**Example:** Deepfake audio of a CEO was used to authorise a $243,000 fraudulent transfer in 2019 (UK energy firm case). In 2024 elections in over 40 countries, AI-generated misinformation was identified as a major threat to electoral integrity.

**Mitigation:** Watermarking and provenance standards (e.g., C2PA), mandatory labelling of AI-generated content (EU AI Act Article 50), and platform-level content-moderation policies.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your final 30-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Bias** has 7 recognised types: Historical, Representation, Measurement, Aggregation, Learning, Evaluation, Deployment.
- [ ] **Fairness** has 3 families: Group (DP, EO, PP), Individual (Lipschitz), Counterfactual (causal).
- [ ] **Demographic Parity** is *weaker* than Equalised Odds — it can be satisfied by a useless classifier.
- [ ] **Equalised Odds** requires *equal TPR AND FPR* across groups; measured by **AOD**.
- [ ] **Disparate Impact Ratio** in [0.8, 1.25] is the US EEOC "4/5 rule" of acceptable fairness.
- [ ] **Chouldechova's Impossibility Theorem**: Calibration, Equalised Odds, and Predictive Parity cannot *all* hold unless base rates are equal.
- [ ] **Fairness Through Unawareness** (removing the sensitive attribute) *fails* due to proxy variables and redundant encodings.
- [ ] **SHAP** is the unique method satisfying Efficiency, Symmetry, Dummy, and Linearity axioms.
- [ ] **LIME** is a *local surrogate* — its explanation is valid only in a small neighbourhood of the instance.
- [ ] **XAI methods:** Intrinsic (linear, trees, attention) vs. Post-hoc (SHAP, LIME, counterfactuals).
- [ ] **EU AI Act 2024** has 4 tiers: Unacceptable (banned), High (regulated), Limited (transparency), Minimal (no obligation).
- [ ] **Real-world cases** to memorise: Amazon hiring AI, Apple Card, COMPAS, Clearview AI, Uber driver pay.
- [ ] **Governance frameworks to compare:** EU AI Act, OECD Principles, IEEE EAD, UNESCO Recommendation, India NSAI.
- [ ] **The bias-variance-fairness triangle:** improving any two sides typically worsens the third.
- [ ] **Societal impact domains** (KTU 2024): Employment, Privacy, Autonomy, Democracy, Environment.
- [ ] **Asilomar AI Principles** (2017) — 23 principles; remember the three groups: Research, Ethics, Long-term.
- [ ] **The 4/5 rule** for Disparate Impact applies to *selection rate* ratios, not error rate ratios.
- [ ] **SHAP axiom of Efficiency:** $\sum_i \phi_i = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ — a board-exam favourite identity.
- [ ] **Model Cards** (Mitchell et al., 2019) and **Datasheets for Datasets** (Gebru et al., 2021) are the standard documentation instruments for ethical AI deployment.
- [ ] **Right to Explanation** is enshrined in EU GDPR Articles 13–15 and operationalised by EU AI Act Article 13.
- [ ] **LIME loss function:** $\xi(x) = \arg\min_g \mathcal{L}(f, g, \pi_x) + \Omega(g)$ — a Lasso-style regularised local fit.
- [ ] **Counterfactual explanation** is a *minimal change* to an input that would flip the prediction — answers "what if?" questions.
- [ ] **Differential privacy** ($\epsilon$-DP) is the mathematical framework for quantifying privacy leakage; the smaller $\epsilon$, the stronger the privacy guarantee.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Application of AI for Intrusion Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT413 - INTRODUCTION TO AI AND ML
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Application of AI for Intrusion Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism that monitors network traffic or system activities for malicious actions, policy violations, or active threats, and reports them to a management system. An **AI-based IDS** leverages Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) algorithms to automatically learn normal and malicious behavior patterns from data, enabling the detection of both known signatures and previously unseen (zero-day) attacks with minimal human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *case study* in this module is expected to demonstrate the **end-to-end AI pipeline** — from raw network data ingestion to model evaluation — applied to a real-world security problem. The most cited academic benchmark in literature is the **KDD Cup 99 / NSL-KDD** dataset.

### Classification of IDS (by deployment)

| Deployment Type | Monitors | Scope | Typical AI Use Case |
|-----------------|----------|-------|---------------------|
| **NIDS** (Network-based) | Packets on a network segment | Perimeter / subnet | Deep packet inspection using CNN/LSTM |
| **HIDS** (Host-based) | System calls, logs, file integrity | Single host | Behavioral modeling using autoencoders |
| **Hybrid IDS** | Both network and host | Enterprise-wide | Ensemble learning (RF + DNN) |

### Classification of Detection Approaches

- **Signature-based (Misuse Detection):** Matches incoming traffic against a database of known attack patterns. Cannot detect novel attacks.
- **Anomaly-based:** Builds a model of "normal" behavior and flags deviations. Capable of detecting zero-day attacks but prone to false positives.
- **Stateful Protocol Analysis:** Tracks protocol states to identify deviations from expected sequences.
- **Hybrid Detection:** Combines signature + anomaly to balance precision and recall.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Check:** The term *Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)* differs from IDS in that it actively blocks threats, while IDS only alerts. For examination purposes, treat them as separate concepts unless explicitly asked to compare.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The AI Security Guard at a Mall":**
>
> Imagine a mall entrance guarded by an officer who has worked there for 20 years. He does not check a list of "banned faces" (that would be **signature-based detection**). Instead, he has built an internal **mental model** of how a normal shopper behaves — carrying bags, walking at a normal pace, entering through gates. If someone suddenly runs, abandons a bag, or lingers suspiciously near a jewelry store, he raises an alarm even if he has never seen that exact person commit a crime before. This is **anomaly-based detection**.
>
> Now, equip that guard with a **body camera, a microphone, and an AI brain** that can analyze gait, voice stress, and trajectory in real-time across thousands of entrances simultaneously. That is an **AI-driven IDS** — it scales human intuition through learned statistical patterns.

---

## 1.3 Why AI for Intrusion Detection?

Traditional rule-based systems suffer from:
1. **Manual Rule Engineering:** New attack variants require expert-written signatures.
2. **High False Positive Rates** on encrypted or zero-day traffic.
3. **Inability to Generalize** across evolving threat landscapes.

AI addresses these by **automatically learning discriminative features** from large volumes of network telemetry, achieving detection rates often exceeding **95%** with FPR below **1%** in benchmark studies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Metrics to Memorize for KTU:** **Accuracy**, **Precision (Detection Rate)**, **Recall**, **F1-Score**, and **False Positive Rate (FPR)**. These five appear in nearly every AI-IDS question paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI-IDS Operational Pipeline

The end-to-end workflow of an AI-based IDS consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition:** Raw packet capture (e.g., via `tcpdump`, NetFlow, Zeek logs).
2. **Preprocessing:** Parsing PCAPs → flow records, handling missing values, label encoding, normalization.
3. **Feature Engineering:** Extracting statistical features (packet size, inter-arrival time, flags, byte distribution). For the NSL-KDD dataset, each flow has **41 features**.
4. **Model Training:** Feeding labeled `(feature_vector, label)` pairs to a supervised/unsupervised learner.
5. **Inference / Classification:** The trained model assigns a class (`Normal`, `DoS`, `Probe`, `R2L`, `U2R`) to unseen flows.
6. **Alerting & Response:** Thresholding the confidence score and forwarding to a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Many students lose marks by confusing *preprocessing* with *feature engineering*. Preprocessing makes data usable (scaling, encoding); feature engineering creates *new informative variables* (e.g., `bytes_per_second = total_bytes / flow_duration`).

---

## 2.2 Categories of ML Algorithms Used in IDS

### A. Supervised Learning (Labeled Data Required)

- **Decision Trees (DT)** — interpretable, fast, prone to overfitting.
- **Random Forest (RF)** — ensemble of DTs; robust against overfitting.
- **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** — effective in high-dimensional feature spaces; uses kernel trick.
- **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** — instance-based, computationally expensive at inference.
- **Naive Bayes (NB)** — probabilistic baseline, very fast.

### B. Unsupervised Learning (No Labels)

- **K-Means Clustering** — groups flows into `k` clusters; anomaly = distant from centroid.
- **Isolation Forest** — isolates anomalies via random partitioning.
- **One-Class SVM** — learns a boundary around normal data.
- **Autoencoders** — neural networks that reconstruct input; high reconstruction error = anomaly.

### C. Deep Learning (Hierarchical Feature Learning)

- **Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)** — fully connected DNN baseline.
- **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** — applied to flow-feature matrices (e.g., 1D-CNN over feature vectors).
- **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** — captures temporal dependencies in sequential packet flows.
- **Hybrid CNN-LSTM** — combines spatial + temporal feature extraction.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are evaluated against the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary intrusion-vs-normal classifier.

| Metric | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------------|------|
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness of the IDS | % |
| **Precision** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of all alerts raised, how many are real attacks | % |
| **Recall (Detection Rate)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of all real attacks, how many were caught | % |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of all normal traffic, how much was correctly passed | % |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Probability of false alarm | % |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | unitless |
| **MCC (Matthews CC)** | $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced data | $-1$ to $+1$ |

Where:
- $TP$ = True Positives (attack correctly classified as attack)
- $TN$ = True Negatives (normal correctly classified as normal)
- $FP$ = False Positives (normal misclassified as attack — *false alarm*)
- $FN$ = False Negatives (attack misclassified as normal — *missed attack*)

### Anomaly Score Formulations

| Method | Anomaly Score | Interpretation |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x,\mu) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu_i)^2}$ | Distance from cluster centroid |
| **Mahalanobis Distance** | $d_M(x) = \sqrt{(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)}$ | Accounts for feature covariance |
| **Autoencoder Loss** | $\mathcal{L}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2$ | Reconstruction error squared norm |
| **Isolation Forest Depth** | $s(x,n) = 2^{-\frac{E(h(x))}{c(n)}}$ | Shorter path = more anomalous |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use raw $|x|$ syntax in the table cell. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lVert x \rVert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Telecom Backbone Operators** deploy NIDS with LSTM autoencoders to monitor terabit-scale traffic.
- **Cloud Providers (AWS, Azure)** integrate AI-IDS into VPC Flow Logs and GuardDuty.
- **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** use HIDS with one-class SVMs to detect SCADA anomalies.
- **Banking & FinTech** employ ensemble RF + DNN models to flag fraudulent transactions and lateral movement.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** According to published benchmark studies on NSL-KDD, Random Forest classifiers achieve ~**99% accuracy** with sub-millisecond inference per flow, making them suitable for high-throughput environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study Setup — NSL-KDD Benchmark

We consider the binary classification task: **Normal vs. Attack** on the NSL-KDD test set.

**Hypothetical Confusion Matrix** (from a Random Forest IDS evaluated on **10,880 flows**):

|  | **Predicted: Attack** | **Predicted: Normal** | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Attack** | $TP = 950$ | $FN = 50$ | $1000$ |
| **Actual: Normal** | $FP = 30$ | $TN = 9850$ | $9880$ |
| **Column Total** | $980$ | $9900$ | $10880$ |

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Core Metrics (Exhaustive)

### Step 1 — Accuracy

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

Substituting the matrix values:

$$Accuracy = \frac{950 + 9850}{950 + 9850 + 30 + 50}$$

$$Accuracy = \frac{10800}{10880}$$

$$Accuracy = 0.9936 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 99.36\%$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Out of 10,880 flows, the IDS classifies 10,800 correctly. High accuracy is necessary but **insufficient alone** — a model that predicts "Normal" for everything in a 99% normal dataset would also score 99% accuracy while catching zero attacks.

### Step 2 — Precision

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{950}{950 + 30} = \frac{950}{980} = 0.9694 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 96.94\%$$

**Interpretation:** When the IDS raises an alert, it is correct 96.94% of the time. Only 3.06% of alerts are false alarms — acceptable for most SOC (Security Operations Center) workflows.

### Step 3 — Recall (Detection Rate / Sensitivity)

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{950}{950 + 50} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.9500 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 95.00\%$$

**Interpretation:** The IDS catches 95% of all real attacks. The 5% miss rate represents 50 attacks slipping through — in critical infrastructure, this is often unacceptable, motivating **ensemble or threshold-tuning strategies**.

### Step 4 — False Positive Rate (FPR)

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 9850} = \frac{30}{9880} = 0.00304 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.304\%$$

**Interpretation:** Out of 9,880 normal flows, only 30 are wrongly flagged. Industry target for high-precision SOCs is **FPR < 1%** — this model comfortably meets it.

### Step 5 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9694 \times 0.9500}{0.9694 + 0.9500}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.92093}{1.9194} = 2 \cdot 0.47980 = 0.95960 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 95.96\%$$

**Interpretation:** Single composite score balancing false alarms against missed attacks. F1 is preferred over accuracy on **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., 99% normal vs 1% attack).

### Step 6 — Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)

$$MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

Numerator:

$$TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN = (950)(9850) - (30)(50) = 9{,}357{,}500 - 1{,}500 = 9{,}356{,}000$$

Denominator:

$$\sqrt{(980)(1000)(9880)(9900)} = \sqrt{9.58 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\sqrt{9{,}580{,}000{,}000{,}000} = 9{,}788{,}771.21$$

Therefore:

$$MCC = \frac{9{,}356{,}000}{9{,}788{,}771.21} = 0.9558$$

**Interpretation:** $MCC = 0.9558$ indicates a strong, near-perfect correlation between predicted and actual labels, robust to class imbalance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Anomaly Score (Autoencoder Reconstruction Error)

For an autoencoder mapping input $x \in \mathbb{R}^n \mapsto \hat{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the per-sample squared reconstruction error is:

$$\mathcal{L}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2 = (x - \hat{x})^T (x - \hat{x})$$

A traffic flow is flagged as anomalous if:

$$\mathcal{L}(x) > \tau$$

where $\tau$ is a threshold set from validation data (e.g., 95th percentile of $\mathcal{L}$ on normal traffic).

**Why this works:** The autoencoder, trained *only on normal flows*, learns a low-dimensional manifold of "normal" behavior. Malicious flows, having never been seen, reconstruct poorly, producing high $\mathcal{L}(x)$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Metric | Computed Value | KTU Examiner Threshold | Status |
|--------|----------------|------------------------|--------|
| Accuracy | $99.36\%$ | $> 95\%$ | ✅ Pass |
| Precision | $96.94\%$ | $> 90\%$ | ✅ Pass |
| Recall | $95.00\%$ | $> 90\%$ | ✅ Pass |
| F1-Score | $95.96\%$ | $> 90\%$ | ✅ Pass |
| FPR | $0.30\%$ | $< 1\%$ | ✅ Pass |
| MCC | $0.9558$ | $> 0.9$ | ✅ Pass |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Answer-Writing Tip:** When asked "evaluate the model," present all six metrics in a single comparison table. Examiners award full marks only when *all* standard metrics are reported.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End AI-IDS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Network Traffic Capture PCAP and NetFlow] --> B[Flow Extraction Zeek and CICFlowMeter]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Missing Value Handling and Normalization]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering 41 Features from NSL-KDD Schema]
    D --> E[Dataset Split 80 percent Train and 20 percent Test]
    E --> F[Model Training Random Forest and LSTM Autoencoder]
    F --> G[Inference Engine Per Flow Classification]
    G --> H{Confidence Score}
    H -->|Above Threshold| I[Alert Generation to SIEM]
    H -->|Below Threshold| J[Mark as Normal and Log]
    I --> K[SOC Analyst Review and Triage]
    F --> L[Model Retraining on New Threat Feeds]
    L --> F
```

## 4.2 Detection Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Incoming Packet Flow] --> Q[Feature Vector Construction]
    Q --> R[Trained Classifier]
    R --> S{Predicted Class}
    S -->|Class equals Normal| T[Pass and Increment TN Counter]
    S -->|Class equals Attack| U[Compute Confidence Score]
    U --> V{Confidence greater than Tau}
    V -->|Yes| W[Raise Intrusion Alert Increment TP]
    V -->|No| X[Soft Flag Increment FP]
    W --> Y[Log to SIEM and Notify SOC]
    X --> Y
    T --> Z[Forward to Destination]
    Y --> AA[Block and Quarantine]
```

## 4.3 ML Algorithm Selection Matrix for IDS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1{Is Training Data Labeled}
    D1 -->|Yes| D2{Is Dataset Balanced}
    D1 -->|No| D3[Unsupervised Path]
    D2 -->|Yes| D4[Supervised Balanced Path]
    D2 -->|No| D5[Use SMOTE or Class Weights]
    D4 --> D6[Try Random Forest or XGBoost]
    D5 --> D7[Try SVM with RBF Kernel or DNN with Focal Loss]
    D3 --> D8[Try Isolation Forest or One Class SVM or Autoencoder]
    D6 --> D9{Baseline Accuracy above 95 percent}
    D7 --> D9
    D8 --> D10{Reconstruction Error Threshold Set}
    D9 -->|Yes| D11[Deploy to Production]
    D9 -->|No| D12[Hyperparameter Tuning or Deep Learning]
    D10 -->|Yes| D11
    D10 -->|No| D13[Adjust Latent Dimension or Threshold Tau]
    D12 --> D6
    D13 --> D8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note for KTU Exams:** When a question asks "draw the architecture of an AI-based IDS," examiners expect **at minimum**: a data source block, a preprocessing block, a model block, and a decision/alert block. The above flowcharts exceed this baseline and earn full marks.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based intrusion detection. State one advantage of each in the context of AI.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Signature-based IDS:** Matches incoming traffic against a database of predefined attack patterns (signatures/rules). *Advantage:* Very high precision and low false positive rate for known attacks.
- **Anomaly-based IDS:** Builds a statistical/ML model of "normal" behavior and flags deviations as potential intrusions. *Advantage:* Capable of detecting **zero-day (previously unseen) attacks** because it does not rely on prior signatures.
- **AI relevance:** Machine learning enables the anomaly model to *learn* normal behavior automatically, eliminating the need for hand-crafted rules. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List any three commonly used public datasets for evaluating AI-based intrusion detection systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **KDD Cup 99 / NSL-KDD** — classical benchmark with 41 features, 4 attack categories (DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R). *(1 mark)*
2. **CICIDS2017** — modern dataset from Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity containing benign + modern attacks (Brute Force, Web Attack, Infiltration, etc.). *(1 mark)*
3. **UNSW-NB15** — hybrid real/synthetic dataset with 45 features and 9 attack types; addresses the redundancy issues in KDD99. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]`**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option I**

**(a)** Explain the end-to-end architecture of an AI-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS), with a labeled block diagram. Discuss the role of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The AI-NIDS architecture consists of **six interconnected stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer:** Captures raw network traffic using tools like `tcpdump`, `Wireshark`, or hardware TAPs; logs flow records via NetFlow/sFlow. *(1 mark)*
2. **Preprocessing Layer:** Converts PCAPs into structured flow records, removes duplicates, handles missing values via imputation, and normalizes numerical features using Min-Max or Z-score scaling. Categorical fields (e.g., `protocol_type`) are label-encoded. *(1 mark)*
3. **Feature Engineering Layer:** Extracts discriminative features per flow — duration, src_bytes, dst_bytes, flag, count, srv_count, etc. For the NSL-KDD dataset, 41 features are used. *(1 mark)*
4. **Model Training Layer:** A supervised classifier (e.g., Random Forest) or deep model (e.g., LSTM autoencoder) is trained on labeled `(X, y)` pairs. The model learns the decision boundary separating "Normal" from "Attack" classes. *(1 mark)*
5. **Inference / Classification Layer:** For each incoming flow, the trained model produces a predicted class and a confidence score. *(1 mark)*
6. **Alert & Response Layer:** Flows exceeding the confidence threshold $\tau$ trigger alerts forwarded to a SIEM platform (e.g., Splunk, ELK) for SOC analyst review. *(1 mark)*
7. **Continuous Learning Loop:** Feedback from labeled alerts retrains the model to adapt to new threats (concept drift handling). *(1 mark)*

**Suggested Block Diagram:**

```
Network → Capture → Preprocess → Features → ML Model → Decision → Alert
                                            ↑                        ↓
                                            └── Feedback / Retrain ──┘
```

---

**(b)** A Random Forest classifier evaluated on a test set of 5000 network flows yields the following confusion matrix:
$TP = 450$, $FP = 50$, $FN = 50$, $TN = 4450$.
Compute: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Precision, (iii) Recall, (iv) F1-Score, (v) FPR. Comment on whether the model is suitable for production deployment. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Total flows** = $450 + 50 + 50 + 4450 = 5000$ ✓

**(i) Accuracy:**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{5000} = \frac{450 + 4450}{5000} = \frac{4900}{5000} = 0.9800 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 98.00\% \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

**(ii) Precision:**

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{450}{450 + 50} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.9000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 90.00\% \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

**(iii) Recall:**

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{450}{450 + 50} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.9000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 90.00\% \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

**(iv) F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.90 \times 0.90}{0.90 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.81}{1.80} = 0.9000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 90.00\% \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

**(v) False Positive Rate:**

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{50}{50 + 4450} = \frac{50}{4500} = 0.0111 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.11\% \quad (1\text{ mark})$$

**Production Suitability Comment:** *(1 mark)*

- The model achieves **98% accuracy, 90% F1, and 1.11% FPR**, meeting typical industry thresholds.
- However, FPR slightly exceeds the strict **1% SOC target**, and the **90% recall** means 50 attacks slip through — risky in critical infrastructure.
- **Verdict:** Suitable for *non-critical* enterprise networks; for high-security environments (banking, defense), an **ensemble (RF + LSTM)** or **threshold tuning** is recommended to push recall above 95% and FPR below 0.5%.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option II**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **feature engineering** in AI-based intrusion detection. With reference to the **NSL-KDD dataset**, list any **five** features and explain how each contributes to detecting attacks. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Feature Engineering (3 Marks):**

Feature engineering transforms raw network data into informative numerical representations that ML models can consume. In IDS, it is **the most critical step** — even sophisticated deep networks underperform on poorly engineered features. The objectives are:
- *Discrimination:* Make attack and normal flows linearly separable.
- *Dimensionality Control:* Reduce the 41+ raw features to a manageable subset.
- *Robustness:* Remove features that cause overfitting (e.g., highly correlated byte counters).

**Five NSL-KDD Features (4 Marks — 0.8 each):**

1. **`duration`** — Length of the connection in seconds. Long-duration connections (e.g., brute-force SSH attempts) are suspicious. *Contributes to:* DoS, R2L detection.
2. **`src_bytes`** — Bytes sent from source to destination. Unusually high values may indicate exfiltration (e.g., FTP write attacks). *Contributes to:* R2L, U2R detection.
3. **`dst_bytes`** — Bytes from destination to source. In a normal HTTP GET, this is large; in a back-channel exfil, this is small. *Contributes to:* Probe, R2L detection.
4. **`count`** — Number of connections to the same host in the past 2 seconds. High count = possible port scan. *Contributes to:* Probe detection.
5. **`srv_count`** — Number of connections to the same service in the past 2 seconds. Spike indicates service-targeted attack (e.g., SYN flood on port 80). *Contributes to:* DoS detection.

---

**(b)** Describe the **anomaly detection approach** using an **autoencoder**. Derive the **anomaly score** and explain the **thresholding strategy**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Autoencoder Concept (2 Marks):**

An autoencoder is a neural network with two parts:
- **Encoder:** $z = f_\theta(x)$ maps input $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a latent representation $z \in \mathbb{R}^k$ (where $k < n$).
- **Decoder:** $\hat{x} = g_\phi(z)$ reconstructs the input from the latent code.

The model is trained **only on normal flows**, minimizing reconstruction loss. After training, the latent space encodes the manifold of "normal" behavior.

**Anomaly Score Derivation (3 Marks):**

The per-sample squared reconstruction error is:

$$\mathcal{L}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$$

**Derivation steps:**

$$\mathcal{L}(x) = (x - g_\phi(f_\theta(x)))^T (x - g_\phi(f_\theta(x)))$$

**Thresholding Strategy (2 Marks):**

1. Compute $\mathcal{L}(x)$ on a held-out validation set of *normal* flows.
2. Set the threshold $\tau$ as the **95th percentile** (or 99th, depending on acceptable FPR) of these values:

$$\tau = Q_{0.95}\big(\{\mathcal{L}(x_i) : x_i \in \mathcal{D}_{val}^{normal}\}\big)$$

3. At inference, flag any flow as anomalous if $\mathcal{L}(x) > \tau$.

**Why it works:** Normal flows reconstruct with low error (they lie on the learned manifold); malicious flows fall outside it, producing high $\mathcal{L}(x)$ and crossing $\tau$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — Precision answers "of the alerts, how many are correct?"; Recall answers "of the actual attacks, how many were caught?" KTU examiners specifically look for the *correct* question each metric answers.
> 2. **Forgetting units / percentages** — Always write `0.95 → 95%` explicitly. Bare decimals lose marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the threshold $\tau$** — A "perfect" autoencoder anomaly detector with no threshold is not deployable; you MUST specify how $\tau$ is set.
> 4. **Not stating dataset assumptions** — The NSL-KDD is a *legacy* benchmark. Modern evaluations require CICIDS2017 or UNSW-NB15; mentioning outdated datasets may cost 1 mark.
> 5. **No diagram** — Part B sub-questions on "architecture" or "process" MUST include a labeled diagram. Text-only answers lose up to 2 marks.
> 6. **Computational errors in F1 derivation** — Use $2PR/(P+R)$, NOT $(P+R)/2$ (that is the arithmetic mean, not the harmonic mean).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **IDS** = Intrusion Detection System; **IPS** = Intrusion Prevention System (active blocking).
- ✅ Three deployment types: **NIDS, HIDS, Hybrid**.
- ✅ Two detection paradigms: **Signature-based** (known attacks, high precision) vs **Anomaly-based** (unknown attacks, high recall).
- ✅ **NSL-KDD** has 41 features and 5 classes (Normal, DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R).
- ✅ **Six core metrics:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, FPR, MCC.
- ✅ **Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data** — always pair it with F1 or MCC.
- ✅ **F1 = Harmonic mean of P and R**, NOT arithmetic mean.
- ✅ **Autoencoders** trained on normal data use **reconstruction error** as the anomaly score.
- ✅ **Threshold $\tau$** = 95th–99th percentile of normal validation losses.
- ✅ **Random Forest** is a strong baseline (≈99% acc on NSL-KDD); **LSTM** wins on temporal flow sequences.
- ✅ **Concept drift** must be handled via periodic retraining on fresh labeled data.
- ✅ **Adversarial robustness** is an open challenge — attackers can craft inputs that fool the model.
- ✅ **Public datasets to cite:** KDD99, NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, CICIDS2017, TON_IoT.

> **Mnemonic for Metrics — "PReF":**
> **P**recision = Predicted positive correctness.
> **Re**call = Real positive coverage.
> **F**1 = Fusion of P and R.
